File.Name: 6512b_6512-090.jpg
Notes: Remarkable flowers of a native Larkspur in the canyon. I guess this is Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum but cp. D. parryi. It's not common. The color ranges from maroon to deep blue. The flowers offer sweet nectar at the end of the spur, but long-tongued insects have to snuffle past the stamens to reach it. Pollination happens. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2006)

File.Name: 6512c_6512-093.jpg
Notes: Grass flowers - like fern seeds - seem like fairy stuff, but here they with yellow pollen-bearing male stamens and tiny furry white female stigmas. This grass is native California Brome (Bromus carinatus). We have to be careful weeding the garden not to confuse this native with the non-native bromes like Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus) and kin that are trying to take over. Wind-pollinated grass flowers seem simple, but they are considered "highly evolved" because they have gone a step further and lost everything that is not essential. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2006)

File.Name: 6512d_6511-064.jpg
Notes:
Looking close at the female Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) - the lower of the pair in my [Previous] photo. It's beautiful how the occasional scale gets green or turquoise color. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2006)

I believe the diamond-shaped green patch between and behind her eyes is the mysterious "third eye" - see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=9087674

File.Name: 6513a_6513-004.jpg
Notes: I took my usual weekend morning coffee on the porch looking for little songbirds in the trees when this non-native Rock Dove aka City Pigeon (Columba livia) almost knocked my camera over when it tried to land right next to me! Here it is sitting on an old plastic chair from school. Note the bands on both its legs. There was deep fog on both sides of the mountains this morning. I reckon this homing pigeon was waiting in the sun for the fog to clear. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2006)

File.Name: 6514a_6514-018.jpg
Notes:
New Edith's Checkerspot (Occidryas editha) butterfly in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2006)

I took my morning walk and saw many butterflies - but got few photos. I'm preoccupied with the school slide show I'm putting together. I spent most of the day staring at my computer. Que lastima!

File.Name: 6515a_6514-016.jpg
Notes:
Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae) making more Lady Beetles on a plant in the canyon. Note how the female (?) on the right has black spots that the male (?) on the left almost completely lacks. The question marks (?) are a reminder not to take anything for granted. Tilt your head to the left to admire the robot faces they have painted on head and thorax! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2006)

In some winters I find thousands of these "Lady Bugs" converging in our canyon. This year I saw none. I'm glad a few have returned. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=5622374

File.Name: 6515b_6514-010.jpg
Notes:
Little Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly working tiny flowers of Lavender (Lavandula officinalis or kin) in the garden. You can see it's whisker-thin proboscis. These little butterflies really are blue on top, and they look blue when they fly. The color almost disappears when they land with wings folded like this. You can see a hint of blue on the body and just a sliver of upper wing showing along the top edge of its front forewing. This individual seem unusually pale (or worn?) underneath. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2006)

Lavender has showy "petals" on top that are really just leafy bracts. That's the color in the background. This butterfly has found the small real flowers down below. The flowers are so purple that they are almost black. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=10557094

File.Name: 6516a_6514-020.jpg
Notes: First flower of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) - a kind of Mariposa Lily in the Liliaceae family - on the dry side of our garden. They might be my favorite wildflower - not showy like the poppies, but elegant. They have six petals like all lilies, but the upper three are sepal-like, and the other three form a tender trap to guide pollinator-insects to the sweet nectar glands at the base of the petals. One of the glands is visible in this photo. See my [Previous] photo to understand the trap. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2006)

File.Name: 6516b_6515-024.jpg
Notes: Looking almost straight up into a new flower of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) - a kind of Mariposa Lily in the Liliaceae family. The flower is a trap, but it's a "tender trap" not a deadly one. Insects push in between the fringed petals past the inward-pointing hairs to get to the nectar glands and goodies inside. It's harder getting out, and pollination happens. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2006)

File.Name: 6517a_6517-034.jpg
Notes:
First new flowers of native Rock Phacelia (Phacelia imbricata) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family. True to its common name, it grows on rocky outcrops and slides that are naturally disturbed. It also does well along mountain roads that are disturbed by people and cars. This one is doing well on the dry side of our garden. It's one of the hairiest plants I know - even the stamens are hairy! (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2006)

Insects are still scarce, but the warm weather this week is starting to bring them out. The dark pellets scattered below the flowers are insect "frass" - poop - from unknown insect larvae still waiting for their wings.

File.Name: 6518a_6518-053.jpg
Notes:
Impressive thunderstorm growing over Figueroa Mountain this afternoon as seen from the poppy field at school. It looks like a massive explosion or eruption over the mountains! The growing mushroom-cap crown on top shows strong uplift. As moist surface air rises into an area of lower pressure, the air cools and the water vapor is "squeezed out" as rain and storm. I talked with a Forest Service person today and heard there have been lighting stricks and small fires in the backcountry. (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 May 2006)

I expect summer thunderstorms as it warms up for summer, but blow me down, there's a chance of rain from another real storm this weekend. I'm off on another school trip to the desert next week, and I have that feeling of deja vu. What a strange weather year this has been!

File.Name: 6518b_6518-058.jpg
Notes: Beautiful thunderstorm rising over Big Pine Mountain, looking from the upper end of Lake Cachuna. I followed this storm home from school today. Pretty as it is, it's becoming less threatening as it moves. See my [Next] photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 May 2006)

File.Name: 6519a_6519-150.jpg
Notes:
Finally we have new flowers on the native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivivus) in the Storax (Styracaceae) family. The flowers are fragrant and beautiful and unusual. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2006)

These head-high shrubs are common here on San Marcos Pass both in the woodland and into the chaparral. They have a very spotty distrubution in Southern California, common here and there, but not in-between. They demonstrate the naturalist adage that "rare" really means "rarely common". It's true.

The full species name is "Styrax officinalis L. var. redivivus (Torrey) H. Howard". The strange thing is that this is a California variety of the same species that grows in the Mediterranean region . The species name dates back to Linnaeus (L.) and the genus name "Styrax" goes way back to Theophrastus in ancient Greece. The Mediterranean species is the source of "gum storax". You'd recognize the perfume-like smell.

This is an example of an "intercontinental disjunction". How can a single species grow just here and there across the world? I reckon it was once widespread and then became isolated due to changing conditions. It's interesting that this plant has spotty distrubution both in California and across the world. These unusual native plants with "disjunct distributions" tell us something about the history of the earth. There's more info at:
   http://www.botgard.ucla.edu/html/MEMBGNewsletter/Volume5number2/Styraxofficinalis.html
ScientificName: Styrax officinalis; redivivus; Styracaceae; Styrax officinalis; redivivus; gum storax
associatedTaxa: Styrax officinalis; redivivus; Styracaceae; Styrax officinalis; redivivus; gum storax

File.Name: 6520a_6511-051.jpg
Notes: New flowers of native Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) family. The waxy buds formed way back in autumn, but they wait until now to open. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2006)

File.Name: 6521a_6521-051.jpg
Notes:
This male (I guess) House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird has just delivered a caterpillar snack to the female (I guess) wren sitting on new eggs inside. I have no time for photos today, but these wrens came to me. They took over a bird nest sitting on our upstairs' porch railing about six feet or two meters away from the door and my usual chair. I had to back away for this photo! Now we have three nesting wrens at home. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2006)

I'm taking our graduating 8th grade school kids to our cabin in the Alabama Hills at the base of Mt. Whitney near Lone Pine next week. It's raining tonight, I hope it will clear for our trip. It's been that kind of year. I'm sure I'll come back with new photos to share in a week.

File.Name: 6527a_6523-101.jpg
Notes:
Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) with full "pollen baskets" working a flower head of Bush Sunflower (Encelia actoni) in the Alabama Hills desert at the base of Mount Whitney. This bee is obviously collecting pollen, but it's also using its proboscis to suck nectar from the composite florets. (Owens Valley, 23 May 2006)

I'm back from my school trip to our cabin in the Alabama Hills at the base of Mt. Whitney near Lone Pine. Now I have a slide show to finish, then graduation. And summer!

File.Name: 6527b_6525-111.jpg
Notes: Little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in a flower head of native Mojave Thistle (Cirsium mohavense). This spider looks like it's hanging on in a great wind, but it's really just sitting on the composite florets of the flower. It has a happy face on its back! (Owens Valley, 23 May 2006)

File.Name: 6527c_6523-104.jpg
Notes: Little ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) on a new flower bud of this Silver Cholla (Opuntia echinocarpa) cactus. There are ants everywhere in the desert! (Owens Valley, 23 May 2006)

File.Name: 6528a_6523-079.jpg
Notes: There's a beetle (Coleoptera) rave happening inside this flower of Beavertail Cactus (Opuntia basilaris)! It's like one of those candy "Easter eggs" with a scene inside. (Owens Valley, 23 May 2006)
ScientificName: Coleoptera) rave happening inside this flower of Beavertail Cactus (Opuntia basilaris
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera) rave happening inside this flower of Beavertail Cactus (Opuntia basilaris

File.Name: 6528b_6523-077.jpg
Notes: Little ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) on flowers of American Dogwood - aka Creek Dogwood or Redosier Dogwood - (Cornus sericea) in the Dogwood (Cornaceae) family. This shrub lacks the large white petal-like bracts that some species have. There are ants everywhere! (Owens Valley, 23 May 2006)
CommonNames: ants; American Dogwood; Creek Dogwood; Redosier Dogwood; Dogwood

File.Name: 6528c_6523-098.jpg
Notes: Incoming Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) with full "pollen baskets" working a flower head of Bush Sunflower (Encelia actoni) in the Alabama Hills desert at the base of Mount Whitney. See my [Previous] photo. (Owens Valley, 23 May 2006)

File.Name: 6529a_6524-092.jpg
Notes: This female Blue Grouse (Dendragapus obscurus) bird attacked us on the Mount Whitney trail to Lone Pine Lake! I reckon she was trying to to move us away from her nest. She's the size of a banty chicken! (Owens Valley, 24 May 2006)

File.Name: 6530a_6523-096.jpg
Notes: Looking into a surreal flower head of native Mojave Thistle (Cirsium mohavense) growing at the base of a rock outcrop in the Alabama Hills desert at the base of Mount Whitney. There's nothing commonplace about these thistles. (Owens Valley, 25 May 2006)

File.Name: 6530b_6525-128.jpg
Notes: Beautiful composite flower head of native Mojave Aster (Xylorhiza tortifolia). The flower seems to glow with it's own light. (Owens Valley, 25 May 2006)

File.Name: 6531a_6525-175.jpg
Notes:
Native Desert Needlegrass (Achnatherum speciosum aka Stipa speciosa) in the Alabama Hills desert with the sun behind. I love the twisted and bent awns. The native grasses have so much more character than the usual introduced weedy annuals. (Owens Valley, 25 May 2006)

My school slide show is almost done - 1,891 photos in 23 segments with music sequenced to video - 60 minutes and 37 seconds - showtime is Thursday night at 7:00 pm, and graduation is Friday morning. I look forward to getting out in the woods with my camera again!

File.Name: 6601a_6523-080.jpg
Notes:
Golden composite flower heads of (I think) Cooper's Goldenbush (Ericameria cooperi) growing in the Alabama Hills desert at the base of Mount Whitney on the east side of the Sierras last week. This plant is being attacked by golden tendrils of a parasitic Dodder (Cuscuta sp.) plant in the Cuscutaceae family. It's creepy. Dodder grows without green chlorophyll or a connection to the earth, but it sucks nutrients from other green plants like this. It grows in the salt marshes and mountains at home, and here it is in the desert as well. (Owens Valley, 23 May 2006)

My school slide show is done. Graduation is tomorrow. I look forward to a walk in the woods with my camera this weekend - and many more to follow - to get a few new photos!

File.Name: 6602a_6602-009.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working a flower of native Rock Phacelia (Phacelia imbricata) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family. These interesting native bees hover and fly like the Syrphid Flower Flies (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) that mimic other bees. It's a bee that mimics a fly that mimics a bee! (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2006)

It's summer!
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Rock Phacelia; Waterleaf; Syrphid Flower Flies

File.Name: 6602b_6602-007.jpg
Notes:
This "Wild Bee" (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working a flower of native Rock Phacelia (Phacelia imbricata) seemed too big and clumsy when I took the photo on the dry side of our garden this afternoon. Look close... can you see what this is?

.

.

.

.

Two wild bees are mating - look for the antennae on top and bottom! (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2006)

File.Name: 6603a_6603-041.jpg
Notes:
Little hummer - I guess a female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) - collecting soft fluff from a native Cattail (Typha domingensis?) seed head in our little "pond" for her nest. Note the yellow pollen on her head - she's been working the flowers! See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2006)

It's not much of a pond - just a meter-square fiberglass tub a few inches deep filled with native water plants. It's good for wildlife and photos. You should have one.

File.Name: 6603b_6603-042.jpg
Notes: Little hummer - I guess a female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) - with a beak-full of soft fluff from a native Cattail (Typha domingensis?) in our little "pond". Note the yellow pollen on the hummer's head. She's been working the flowers! See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2006)

File.Name: 6603c_6603-021.jpg
Notes:
Clown-colored Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly licking our concrete wellhead by the creek. The well doesn't leak, and the creek is running, so maybe it's after salt rather than water? See my [Previous] photo for more of the story. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2006)

I was surprised to see several of these Checkerspot butterflies in the woods today. Usually I first see the colonial web-spinning caterpillars on native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) shrubs, but I haven't seen any new caterpillars yet. It's been a strange year with late rain and late insects. Que pasa?

File.Name: 6603d_6603-025.jpg
Notes: Face to face with a new clown-colored Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly trying to roll up its proboscis/tongue. Look close. Butterflies fold their long proboscis into two tight tubes to suck nectar, but this veteran is missing half its stuff. (Butterfly left, screen right.) I wonder if this is like trying to drink through a straw with a hole in it? Maybe this is why I managed to get so close? (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2006)

File.Name: 6603e_6603-037.jpg
Notes: Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) branch by the creek. I kept moving in closer until it flew off, but it always came back to the same roost. This feisty butterfly was trying to chase everything away from its territory - even birds! (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2006)

File.Name: 6603f_6603-031.jpg
Notes: Some kind of "Blue" (subfamily Polyommatinae) butterfly? - even though there's no blue on it. It's resting on a not-quite-flowering stem of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the (Pea (Fabaceae) family. The broad orange bands on upper and lower hindwings don't match anything in my books. In the air, it looks brownish with an orange tail. My first thought was a Copper. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2006)

File.Name: 6604a_6604-058.jpg
Notes:
Tiny caterpillar munching a leaflet of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) near home. It's using special effects to look scary to preditors. It's real head is hunkered down to munch lupine in this photo. But it has groups of thicker black hairs in front to mimic claws and bigger red bumps to mimic eyes. See my [Previous] two photos to appreciate the effect! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2006)

Zooming into the original hi-res photos, I can see that the body hairs are simple, but the "antennae" hairs are plumose with side hairs and tufts at the end. That's why they look thicker. Impressive design for a humble caterpillar!

File.Name: 6604b_6604-065.jpg
Notes: Tiny caterpillar on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) near home. It's less than a centimeter long, but it sure looks like a nightmare psychedelic scorpion to me! (Or maybe a lobster?) It has an upturned tail, and special dark hairs in front to mimic claws, and big red "eyes" that are really just bumps like all the others on its back - but these are in just the right place to be eyes. Sometimes you just have to smile at Mama Nature's tricks. I wonder if the disguise really helps protect them from preditors? (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2006)

File.Name: 6604c_6604-062.jpg
Notes: Tiny caterpillar on a leaflet of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) near home. I got too close with my camera and it starting writhing like a demon! That's the tail on top and the head below. It's less than a centimeter long, but it still looks pretty frightening. That's special effects! See my [Next] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2006)

File.Name: 6604d_6604-044.jpg
Notes: I got a good look, and I'm pretty sure this is a Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly working a daisy flower in the garden. But you sure can't tell from this photo! Butterflies have this trick of turning to look right at you when you try to position your camera. It fluttered away when I tried to move my tripod to the side. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2006)

File.Name: 6604e_6604-045.jpg
Notes: A native Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) - not a honey bee - working a flower of the same non-native Sage (Salvia - maybe S. interrupta?) shown in the [Previous] photo. This bee is actually leaving rather than entering. It's not slurping from a bowl. It completely disappears inside the flower for a few seconds to get the nectar, and it has to back out like this. You can see the male stamens touching it's head at the base of the antennae, but I don't understand how the arching female style receives that pollen. Maybe this garden flower from a distant land is designed for bigger bees? (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2006)

File.Name: 6604f_6603-012.jpg
Notes: Little hummer - I guess a female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) - in the air working the blue flowers of a non-native sage in the garden. (Maybe it's Salvia interrupta? - though our plant is more like a mosh pit than the "beautiful and dignified" plant my "Book of Salvias" describes!) Notice the pollen on her bill. The hummers seem to get this stiff "S"-shaped pose with tail in the air when they work the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2006)

File.Name: 6605a_6603-027.jpg
Notes:
Some kind of "Blue" (subfamily Polyommatinae) butterfly oviposting - laying eggs - on a branch of not-quite-flowering Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the (Pea (Fabaceae) family) growing in the canyon near the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2006)

I think this is the same butterfly (same species at least) as my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=13984485

File.Name: 6605b_6603-040.jpg
Notes:
California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly perched in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our upstairs deck. I got this photo with my 500 mm "big lens" while looking for birds. These feisty butterflies rarely visit flowers for fuel, though I sometimes catch them "puddling" on the ground. They try to chase everything away from its territory - even birds! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2006)

I got a photo of her cousin Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly a few days ago. The California Sister has a dark border around the orange wingtip that her sister lacks. Compare my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=13984568
CommonNames: California Sister; Coast Live Oak; Lorquin's Admiral; California Sister
ScientificName: Adelpha bredowii; californica; Quercus agrifolia; Basilarchia lorquini
associatedTaxa: Adelpha bredowii; californica; Quercus agrifolia; Basilarchia lorquini

File.Name: 6605c_6603-057.jpg
Notes: Look close - do you see the tiny red spider-relative Mite (Acari) on these prickly seeds of Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine) in the garden? This plant is a non-native weed that's here for good - competing with many native Galium species. The fishhook-like spines are perfect to stick to your socks. Check your socks and shioe laces after every hike! People and cattle have carried this plant around the world. Too late for blame, but it's interesting to see where these wandering plants get established. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2006)

File.Name: 6605d_6602-006.jpg
Notes:
Seeds of life - I noticed this little coccoon in a curled up leaf of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2006)

Insects are late this year, but they are here!

File.Name: 6605e_6603-032.jpg
Notes: Little flowers of native Common Rush-Rose (Helianthemum scoparium) in the Rock-rose (Cistaceae) family. The fallen petals are captured in a Sheet Web Spider (Linyphiidae) web. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2006)
CommonNames: Common Rush-Rose; Rock-rose; Sheet Web Spider

File.Name: 6605f_6604-050.jpg
Notes: Dandelion-like seeds of native Mountain Dandelion (Agoseris grandiflora?) ready to take off. Fly on little wing! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2006)

File.Name: 6606a_6606-112.jpg
Notes: Little hummer - I guess a female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) - working the blue flowers of a non-native sage in the garden. (Maybe it's Salvia interrupta?) (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2006)

File.Name: 6606b_6606-084.jpg
Notes: Looking into a native wildflower of Catalina Mariposa (Calochortus catalinae). The little beetles inside know it's a rave! There are hundreds of these flowers on one hillside on the pass! I'll get back when it's not so windy. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2006)

File.Name: 6606c_6606-106.jpg
Notes: Pencil lead-thin wasp working native flowers of native Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa) in the canyon. Life is good! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2006)

File.Name: 6606d_6606-089.jpg
Notes: Immature Long-Horned Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) - maybe a Katydid or Cricket - on a composite flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2006)
CommonNames: Long-Horned Grasshopper; Katydid; Cricket; Canyon Sunflower

File.Name: 6606e_6606-072.jpg
Notes: Native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) caterpillar munching a leaf of the non-native invasive weed Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare). The butterfly's name is doubly wrong. The food plant is Fennel not Anise, and they also feed on other native plants in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family like they always has before the weeds arrived a hundred or two years ago. I'm glad they enjoy munching the weeds! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2006)

File.Name: 6606f_6606-102.jpg
Notes: I wish this photo was sharp, but I love the action and affection. A little Wasp (order Hymenoptera) is sucking pollen from the blue anther in its hands. The flower is a native Phacelia, maybe Phacelia distans or maybe P. cicutaria (?) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2006)

File.Name: 6607a_6607-133.jpg
Notes:
I learned something today. This is a female Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. I figured she was ovipositing - laying eggs - on leaves of this native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Figwort Family (Scrophulariaceae) in the canyon. She had that ovipositing pose. She let me get close with my camera as she fluttered back and forth over the same penstemon branch again and again. I took 50+ photos and looked close, but I never saw her actually deposit an egg. Que pasa?

I didn't understand until I saw this photo on my computer. (Not quite sharp - it was breezy.) The butterfly is laying webs! I know the caterpillars are web-spinners and live in communal web-tents as they feed and mature. I didn't know that mama lays down the first webs that connect them into a colony! I haven't seen this in any of my books, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2006)

I have photos of the caterpillar web-nests in the archive, e.g.
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=7958213
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=7984110 http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=450832

Interesting - I didn't see any web-nests last year (and few of the butterflies). I haven't seen any nests this year either, but I hope to. All insects are late this year after our unusually cold and wet spring following a dry winter. Where do these first Checkerspot butterflies come from? Or are they laying tracks for next year?
Locality: and few of the butterflies).  I haven't seen any nests this year either
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Figwort Family

File.Name: 6607b_6607-129.jpg
Notes: Female Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a leaf of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Figwort Family (Scrophulariaceae) in the canyon. I first thought she was ovipositing - laying eggs - but I think she's laying webs instead. See my [Next] photo. You can see webs on the bottom left and right. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2006)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Figwort Family

File.Name: 6607c_6607-130.jpg
Notes: Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly taking wing, seen from below. I always try for sharp and sometimes get lucky! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2006)

File.Name: 6607d_6607-141.jpg
Notes: I found this beautiful mostly-white butterfly caterpillar hiding under a leaflet of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) on the dry side of the garden today. I have no idea what it will become. That elegant leaf curled in a spline curve under its back is native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus). Butterflies are still scarce in the canyon, but here they come! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2006)

File.Name: 6607e_6607-121.jpg
Notes:
Small caterpillar of native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on a leaf of the introduced Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) that is invading our mountains and islands. It's an interesting pose. Maybe this caterpillar is about to molt into it's next "instar" stage? (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2006)

Compare my [Previous] photo, and my caterpillar series at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=7976165
and [Next] from there.

File.Name: 6607f_6607-122.jpg
Notes:
Tiny new caterpillar of native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly munching the introduced Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) that is invading our mountains and islands. The caterpillars go through several molts or "instars" that look quite different. This is the "bird turd" stage. It's good camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2006)

I have a series of Anise Swallowtail caterpillar photos starting at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=7976165
and [Next] from there.

File.Name: 6608a_6608-155.jpg
Notes:
Sometimes I gotta lay my head down...

Female Crane Fly (Tipulidae in the order Diptera) resting her head on a leaf of native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the canyon. These huge flies look like giant mosquitoes, and kids at school call them "Mosquito Hawks". As far as I know, the adults have reduced mouthparts and don't feed at all. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2006)

Fun stumper - who sings "lay my head down"?

File.Name: 6608c_6608-146.jpg
Notes: I got the blues... At least I got a photo of this "Blue" (subfamily Polyommatinae) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. The more I go through my books, the more confused I get. I'd rather not give a name than get it wrong. Please help me if you know California butterflies! (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2006)

File.Name: 6608d_6608-143.jpg
Notes: My first thought was that this is a Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly perched on an annual grass seed head along the road in the canyon. Then I got to my books. It has no eyespots! Maybe a Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus)? Nah. I'm pretty sure this really is a Common Ringlet without eyespots. It is a moth-like butterfly - weak flight, dull colors, but it does have the right butterfly clubs on its antennae. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2006)
CommonNames: Common Ringlet; Brown Elfin; Common Ringlet

File.Name: 6608e_6608-147.jpg
Notes:
Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly working flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the canyon. These pretty little butterflies do not like being photographed! I chased it up and down our gravel road for a while this morning. It would fly 100 feet and rest on the gravel, then take off again as soon as I got close. It finally settled on the flowers. I had the wrong tripod, so this is handheld - difficult up close - but it shows the color pattern just fine. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2006)

That's good advice for photographing butterflies - catch them when they're busy!

File.Name: 6608f_6607-137.jpg
Notes: Face to face with a female Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a leaf of native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Figwort Family (Scrophulariaceae) in the canyon. See the [Previous] few photos. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2006)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Figwort Family

File.Name: 6609a_6609-180.jpg
Notes:
One of our House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) birds with a big green caterpillar for its almost-fledgling young in the birdhouse on the deck. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2006)

It was a gloomy day. But I started watching the wrens and set my camera up with my big lens about 20 feet away from the nest on the porch. I figured they were finding more insects than I would on such a foggy day, so I'd photograph them instead of taking my usual walk!

Both hard-working parents are out of the nest hunting food for the young. I tried to get a photo of every visit to the nest - about 80 photos in over an hour. I'm sure I missed a few visits. I logged 36 different food deliveries like this:

  • 16 crickets
  • 5 small flying insects
  • 5 brown caterpillars
  • 3 green caterpillars
  • 2 crane flies
  • 2 spiders

The parents also removed poop from the nest at least 10 times, probably more. (They leave faster than they arrive, hard to get the photo.)

That comes to a food delivery about every 2 minutes, and a poop removal about every six minutes.

We have three House Wren nests in birdhouses on different ends of our house, and the birds work all day from dawn to dark. Now think of all the other birds in the woods. They make a difference!
Locality: They leave faster than they arrive

File.Name: 6609b_6609-176.jpg
Notes: Look close - this House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird has TWO little caterpillars - green and brown - as snacks for its almost-fledgling young in the birdhouse on the deck. This is surely a stumper. How can this bird catch a caterpillar when it already has one in its beak?! (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2006)

File.Name: 6609c_6609-189.jpg
Notes: House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird with a little Cricket (order Orthoptera) snack for its almost-fledgling young in the birdhouse on the deck. I watched the wrens for a long time, and crickets seem to be their favorite. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2006)

File.Name: 6609d_6609-201.jpg
Notes: Food goes in, poop comes out! Our House Wrens (Troglodytes aedon) are such hard-working parent birds. They deliver fresh food to their almost-fledgling young every few minutes all day long and carry out the poop about every five minutes (!) to keep the nest clean. I watched them close today. The poop seems to come in neat little packages like this - wren pampers! The birds fly a few seconds and drop it, then back to hunting. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2006)

File.Name: 6609e_6609-197.jpg
Notes: Like the role of a die - this female Crane Fly (Tipulidae in the order Diptera) became prey for this House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird about to feed her almost-fledgling young in the bird house on our deck. The fly's unlaid eggs will nourish the bird's young. This dance between food and prey is constant. Look ahead and look behind! Compare my [Previous] two photos, and my [Next] few wren photos. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2006)

File.Name: 6609f_6608-158.jpg
Notes: Crane Flies (Tipulidae in the order Diptera) mating on leaves of native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the canyon. This is a good example of "sexual dimorphism" - and why it's so hard to identify insects to species! That must be the female on the left, she is large with eggs. It would never occur to me that these are the same species if I saw them separately. I showed the same female in the [Previous] photo with a dipterous-madonna look. This is why. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2006)

File.Name: 6610a_6610-003.jpg
Notes:
Sometimes you just have to laugh out loud at Mama Nature's tricks!

I got too close to this caterpillar of a native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly munching a leaf of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) weed, and it erected this orange "thing" from the top of it's head! It looks like a gummy bear kind of thing. It disappeared after a second or too, but the caterpillar showed it again for this photo when I poked at it a few minutes later. It sure startled me! (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2006)

See my [Previous] photo for the same caterpillar without the "thing". How can you describe this - a Pillsbury Doughboy hat? A new Teletubbie? Does this "thing" have another biological function besides startling photographers?

File.Name: 6610b_6610-002.jpg
Notes:
New last-stage caterpillar of native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on a leaf of the non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) weed that is invading our mountains and islands. Notice the asymmetrical eyes that are different colors and shapes! Reminds me of "Mad-Eye Moody" in the Harry Potter books! (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2006)

This is the normal view, but these caterpillars have a surprising trick when disturbed - see my [Next] photo!

File.Name: 6610c_6608-152.jpg
Notes:
I guess this is a clown-colored Western Tussock Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Lymantriidae, maybe Orgyia vetusta or kin) caterpillar on a leaf of native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in our canyon. The females emerge without wings to mate, lay eggs, and die. Some related species defoliate conifer trees up north. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2006)

I think this is the same caterpillar I found munching Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) near home a few days ago. See my photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=14001116
and [Previous] to that.
CommonNames: Western Tussock Moth; California Blackberry; Canyon Lupine
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Lymantriidae; Orgyia vetusta; Rubus ursinus; Lupinus latifolius
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Lymantriidae; Orgyia vetusta; Rubus ursinus; Lupinus latifolius

File.Name: 6610d_6609-207.jpg
Notes: Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) diving into a non-native flower of so-called "Autumn Sage" (Salvia greggii) in the garden. Despite the common name, this Mexican native flowers all summer for us. I'm looking down from the deck. Look close and you can see the hummer picking up yellow pollen on its bill from the stamens on the upper lip of the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2006)

File.Name: 6610e_6608-163.jpg
Notes: Sometimes you do everything right, and it comes out wrong - but then you discover it's good in a different way! I was pre-focused on the front flower of this non-native sage in the garden (maybe Salvia interrupta?) and the female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) went for the flower behind it. I like it. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2006)

File.Name: 6611a_6611-015.jpg
Notes:
Like a rip in the heavens,
a screaming comes across the sky...

This is looking very close at the hindwings of a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly perched immobile on a cold drizzly day - so I could get close. I guess a bird took a bite from the butterfly's right wing (as well as a tail) to interrupt the galaxy-like scales of its wings. Every scar is a story. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2006)

I have a few more photos of this butterfly if you click [Previous].

File.Name: 6611b_6611-010.jpg
Notes:
I moved in close to this Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly resting on native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the canyon on this cold overcast day. I guess this is what butterflies do on cold overcast days - they hang on. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2006)

I guess that's what I try to do on stressful days!

File.Name: 6611c_6611-009.jpg
Notes: Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly resting on native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) leaves in the canyon. This is how I found it perched on this cold overcast day - with wings folded. I moved in closer and closer for photos and finally disturbed it enough to open its wings, but it didn't fly away. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2006)

File.Name: 6611d_6611-013.jpg
Notes:
Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly with wings spread - like it wants to be a museum specimen - on native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the canyon. Note it's missing a tail and has a slice in its right hindwing. See my [Next] few photos. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2006)

It's been overcast and drizzly all day with few insects on the wing. (I did see a few bumblebees.) I often wonder what butterflies do on these cold days. Now I know. They hang on a leaf trying to warm up! It looks pretty vulnerable - I spotted it.

File.Name: 6611e_6611-007.jpg
Notes: It's been cold and overcast with some drizzle all day. This little bee is still curled up in this native flower of Checker Bloom (Sidalcea malviflora) in the Mallow (Malvaceae) family - butts up, dusted with pollen! The bees like to hide inside when the flowers close at night. This bee never woke up today. It's been that gloomy. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2006)

File.Name: 6611f_6611-008.jpg
Notes:
New flower of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula var. vacillans) opening in the canyon with more open flowers behind. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2006)

We're up in the clouds today - we never saw the sun. I used to love these gloomy days shooting Kodachrome 25, but it's harder to get the color with digital. I like this one.

File.Name: 6612a_6612-033.jpg
Notes: Tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) resting on a flower of California Buttercups (Ranunculus californicus) in the canyon. It's remarkable that the buttercups are still flowering, but it's been an unusually cool and wet spring. Insects are scarce and early native wildflowers like this are still going! (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2006)

File.Name: 6612b_6612-044.jpg
Notes:
I thought "checkerspot" when I first saw this butterfly. I was confused when I got back to my books and readjusted. It seemed checkerspot-size, but maybe this is a Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo)? I chased it for a while and couldn't tell what flowers it was interested in. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2006)

Birders used to carry shotguns rather than binoculars, and some butterfly collectors still carry nets and cyanide jars. It's harder with a camera, but I'm good with questions!

File.Name: 6612c_6612-036.jpg
Notes: New Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly resting on the ground among the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves. Is it really trying to hide with all those brilliant eyespots? (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2006)

File.Name: 6612d_6612-047.jpg
Notes: I guess this is a bristly little Tachnid Fly (order Diptera, family Tachinidae) that's been working the new flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum). (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2006)

File.Name: 6612e_6607-113.jpg
Notes:
Tiny immature Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Acrididae) resting on spiny seeds of Hedge Parsley (Torilis arvensis or maybe T. nodosa). That gives a sense of scale if you've ever pulled the seeds from your socks. I got the photo, then tried to move that dry grass blocking the view, of course the little hopper hopped away. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2006)

The weed Torilis is otherwise known as "those $#@%! sticky seeds that ruined my socks!" The seeds are like tiny cockleburs, a terrible pest for socks and dogs. This horrible weed is having a good year in our canyon. We're fighting it.
ScientificName: Orthoptera; Acrididae; Torilis arvensis; T. nodosa; Torilis
associatedTaxa: Orthoptera; Acrididae; Torilis arvensis; T. nodosa; Torilis

File.Name: 6612f_6612-026.jpg
Notes:
Spent flower of native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) with spider webs and raindrops. These aren't real raindrops in June - rare in California - but we've been in the clouds for a few days, and it was especially wet and gloomy this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2006)

Overcast days used to be my favorite shooting Kodachrome 25. It's not so good with digital. The real problem is that the sun and wind arrive together when the sky finally clears in the afternoon!

File.Name: 6613a_6612-052.jpg
Notes: Garden flowers - and native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) - with a tea set catching a beam of late sunshine. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2006)

File.Name: 6614a_6614-066.jpg
Notes: I believe this is a female Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly. It's resting on a dried grass, but it was interested in the Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) and Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) flowers nearby. These little butterflies all look the same to me in the air, but this one is quite distinctive when it lands. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2006)
ScientificName: Leptotes marina; Lotus scoparius; Eriophyllum confertflorum
associatedTaxa: Leptotes marina; Lotus scoparius; Eriophyllum confertflorum

File.Name: 6614b_6614-077.jpg
Notes: I believe this is a California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly working a new flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2006)

File.Name: 6614c_6614-064.jpg
Notes: Beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly with coiled proboscis on a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2006)

File.Name: 6614d_6614-070.jpg
Notes: Little Robber Fly (family Asilidae in the order Diptera) perched on a dead stem in the woods. These flies hunt like dragonflies. They catch their prey in the air with those bristly legs and suck them dry. They're not common - at least it's not common to get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2006)

File.Name: 6614e_6614-057.jpg
Notes: Immature Long-Horned Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) - maybe a Katydid or Cricket on a Salvia leaf in the garden. These tiny nymphs are less than 1/4 inch (6mm) long. They remind me of fancy little ponies! (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2006)

File.Name: 6615a_6614-080.jpg
Notes:
Looking close at a California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly working a new flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family. The flat filaments of the stamens remind me of those wide matches that restaurants used to give out. I have another photo of the same butterfly three shots [Previous]. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2006)

No time for new photos today. Our kids and friends are starting to assemble for the Live Oak Music Festival starting tomorrow just down the road near Lake Cachuma. Check out
   http://www.liveoakmusic.org/
to see what you're missing!
CommonNames: California Hairstreak; Wedge-leaved Horkelia; Rose; Live Oak Music Festival

File.Name: 6615b_6614-062.jpg
Notes: Playing peek-a-boo with a beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. I have another photo of the same butterfly three shots [Previous]. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2006)

File.Name: 6619a_6617-098.jpg
Notes:
Live Oak Music Festival 2006! It's just down the road from home near Lake Cachuma. There was fine music on the festival stage last weekend, but my favorite music is always our late night campground jams. Here are some old and new friends who come together for this event year after year. (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 June 2006)

Check http://www.liveoakmusic.org/ to see what you missed.

File.Name: 6620a_6620-019.jpg
Notes:
It's almost graduation day for these fledgling House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) chicks in their birdhouse nest on our front porch. I could barely make out their faces inside the box yesterday. Today, the lower bird came completely outside for the first time and had to fight its way back in past its sibling! In a few days, they will be on their own. *Sigh* (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2006)

Today's photos are all action-shots - wrens, butterflies, music. The birdhouse is in deep shade under the porch - hard to photograph. I'm a little blurry too after our late-night campground jams at the Live Oak Music Festival last weekend!

File.Name: 6620b_6620-005.jpg
Notes: Mom or dad House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) delivering a tasty green caterpillar to one of its young in the birdhouse on the front porch at home. Sure the parent seems reticent - see my [Previous] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2006)

File.Name: 6620c_6620-011.jpg
Notes: That's the mom or dad House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) delivering food to one of its young in the birdhouse on the front porch. Parents of teenagers - and nursing moms - will appreciate this photo! The two little wrens in this household are just about ready to fly off on their own. It's about time! (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2006)

File.Name: 6620d_6620-042.jpg
Notes: A pair of butterfly lovers sharing the moment. These Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies are face-to-face (look for the eyes) on a flowering branch of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Diplacus longiflorus) in the canyon. I believe that's the male on the left - smaller, with more red on the upper wings - and the female on the right - larger, with very little red on top. This pair was fluttering together in the air just before I got this photo. A moment later, she was oviposting - laying eggs - on the same branch. See my [Previous] photo. Maybe it's coincidence - could conception happen that fast for these butterflies? (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2006)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus

File.Name: 6620e_6620-044.jpg
Notes: Female Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly oviposting - laying eggs - on a flowering Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Diplacus longiflorus) in the canyon. I usually find the caterpillars on Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia), but both plants are native "scrophs" in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2006)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Bush Monkey Flower; Climbing Penstemon; Figwort
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: 6621a_6620-047.jpg
Notes:
Large Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a new flower head of our beautiful native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the canyon. Don't confuse the native thistles with the weedy ones! (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2006)

Today is the Summer Solstice - and my three year anniversary at Fotolog! I posted my first photo to the f'log on June 21, 2003. It's still one of my favorites!
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=251716

File.Name: 6621b_6620-024.jpg
Notes: Another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working composite flowers of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. I believe this is a male because of the red patches on the upper front wing margin. There are hundreds of these beautiful butterflies in the canyon right now! (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2006)

File.Name: 6621c_6620-036.jpg
Notes: This tattered Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly is still working the native composite Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) flowers in the canyon. It took me a while to identify this veteran, but it's still frisky, and I'm sure it has many stories to tell! (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2006)

File.Name: 6621d_6621-061.jpg
Notes: I saw many of these California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterflies "puddling" in the garden today - sucking the wet ground for water and/or minerals. They let me get close when they're engaged. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2006)

File.Name: 6621e_6620-045.jpg
Notes: I believe this is a male Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly resting on a dry grass stem - you can see some iridescent blue spots along the hind wing margin. This butterfly was cavorting with the female in the [Next] photo, but they sure look different. I catch them resting on many plants, but they seem mostly interested in the native Small-Flowered Lotus (Lotus micranthus) - in the Pea Family (Fabaceae) - that grows nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2006)
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Small-Flowered Lotus; Pea Family

File.Name: 6621f_6621-068.jpg
Notes:
Little female "Blue" (subfamily Polyommatinae) butterfly perched on a dry stem in the canyon. The females are mostly copper-gray on top, while the males show the blue color. We have several species that are hard to tell apart. I'll leave it open - is this the same species as the male (I think) Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly in the [Next] photo? (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2006)

Colors are a bit strange today and yesterday. There is a 10,000+ acre wildfire in the backcountry and smoke in the air when the wind blows right - sunset colors in midday!

File.Name: 6622a_6622-104.jpg
Notes: Mating pair of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies resting on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) branch. I believe that's the female (larger, more white) on top and the male (smaller, more red) below. There are hundreds of these beautiful butterflies in the canyon right now. Peace and love! (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2006)

File.Name: 6622b_6622-078.jpg
Notes:
I noticed an inch-long chain of these tiny insect eggs strung along a spider web in the woods today. I guess that's living in the lion's den! (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2006)

I've been trying to imagine the mother insect laying these eggs while moving along the web... how goes it end up like this?

File.Name: 6622c_6622-106.jpg
Notes: Little Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae or kin, in the order Hymenoptera) in the garden, with a little Bellflower (Campanulaceae) flower behind. This little wasp is as thin as a needle. Blink and you miss it - but I got this one. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2006)

File.Name: 6622d_6622-089.jpg
Notes:
I saw this Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae in order Hymenoptera) land on this native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub that's just starting to flower. The wasp has a long tail - really an ovipositor - to lay her eggs within other insects to consume them. I had to change tripods to get the photo - and I noticed something wrong. The preditor became prey to an Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera). (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2006)

See my [Previous] two photos.
Be careful where you land!
CommonNames: Ichneumon Wasp; California Buckwheat; Ambush Bug
ScientificName: Ichneumonidae; Hymenoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Phymatidae; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Ichneumonidae; Hymenoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Phymatidae; Hemiptera

File.Name: 6622e_6622-097.jpg
Notes:
Like meat on a hook... this Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera) is sucking the nutrients from its Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae in the order Hymenoptera) prey. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2006)

Sometimes I wonder why it's so hard to photograph insects, but photo moments like this remind me. This preditor wasp become prey in the blink of my eye!

File.Name: 6622f_6622-094.jpg
Notes:
Preditor becomes prey! This Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae in the Hymenoptera order) has been captured by an Ambush Bug - family Phymatidae in the Hemiptera order - maybe the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). The preditor is perfectly disguised among the flowers of this native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub that's just starting to flower. It's hard to see, even in this close photo. It has crab-like forelegs for holding its victims and a knife-like proboscis to suck them dry. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2006)

Look close at the white "spear" that seems to penetrate the wasp's left shoulder. I see this thing in every photo I took. Do these Ambush Bugs fire a harpoon?
CommonNames: Ichneumon Wasp; Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Ichneumonidae; Hymenoptera; Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Eriogonum fasciculatum
associatedTaxa: Ichneumonidae; Hymenoptera; Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Eriogonum fasciculatum

File.Name: 6623a_6623-132.jpg
Notes: Little Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae) dancing "tippy-tarsi" over tiny native flowers of Turkish Rugging (Chorizanthe staticoides). It never quite lands - it just hovers over the flower and holds on with its toes. I guess with a prodigious proboscis like that, it has no choice! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2006)

File.Name: 6623b_6623-135.jpg
Notes:
I found this native Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) belly-up dead at the bottom of a yellow Prickley Pear (Opuntia sp.) cactus flower in the garden. The bee is dusted with golden pollen for its shroud. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2006)

I don't think insects can smile - their chitinous exoskeleton is too rigid - but look close! I thought of these words from W.C. Fields: "Drown in a vat of whisky? Death where is thy sting?

File.Name: 6623c_6623-118.jpg
Notes:
D'oh - I stepped on this venomous Southern Pacific Rattlesnake (Crotalus viridis helleri) while chasing butterflies today. Fortunately the snake went one way and I went the other. Then we had a face-off. The snake was curled up in deep shade under a bush, sounding electric. I got this photo at 1/25 second with 105 mm lens and tripod, as close as I cared to be. I count 9 rattles. See my [Previous] photo for a close crop. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2006)

After that close encounter I got the hell outa there to a less brushy place where I could at least see my feet. But I keep having these rattlesnake thoughts. Be careful chasing butterflies!

File.Name: 6623d_6623-118.jpg
Notes: The old flag said "Don't tread on me." I guess the modern equivalent is "Don't f*ck with me!" However you say it, this Southern Pacific Rattlesnake (Crotalus viridis helleri) shows the attitude! D'oh - I did step on this snake today. See my [Next] photo for the whole picture. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2006)

File.Name: 6623e_6622-087.jpg
Notes:
Impressive Bee Assassin Bug (maybe Apiomerus crassipes or A. immundus?). It's a true "bug" in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. It's big - about 3/4 inch or 18 mm long. Check my [Previous] photo to see its armored sucking proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2006)

A local relative of this critter attacks humans - the Western Bloodsucking Conenose Bug (Triatoma protracta) - also in the same Reduviidae family. This bug is all black without the red and white trim. It mostly lives in wood rat (Neotoma) nests, but sometimes invades homes and acts like a "Big Bed Bug" (one of it's common names). Apparently it carries a Trypanosome parasite that is non-virulent (?) in California, but the bites are still nasty. There was an outbreak of bites in the nearby Painted Cave community a few years ago in our mountains.

I've read that other Reduviidae species in South America can pass Chagas' Disease. Any info on that Nano or CHiS or Entomofauna?
CommonNames: Bee Assassin Bug; Western Bloodsucking Conenose Bug
ScientificName: Apiomerus crassipes; A. immundus; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Triatoma protracta; Reduviidae; Neotoma; Trypanosome; Reduviidae; Chagas' Disease

Notes: Gnarly Bee Assassin (maybe Apiomerus crassipes or A. immundus?). It's a true "bug" in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. It's large - about 3/4 inch or 18 mm long. All true bugs have sucking mouth parts. This preditor keeps its impressive proboscis folded under its head. See my [Next] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2006)

File.Name: 6624a_6624-021.jpg
Notes: Yum - first new squash from the garden, with flowers still attached! (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2006)

File.Name: 6624b_6624-008.jpg
Notes:
Open seedpod of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) - a kind of Mariposa Lily in the Liliaceae family. I've been shaking out the seeds and tossing them into the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2006)

I have many photos of the flowers in the archive, always in May, like this:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=13714232

File.Name: 6624c_6623-113.jpg
Notes: Looking very close at a Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly in the canyon. I love the speckled eyes. If you look close, you can see that it is standing on FOUR legs instead of six. This is the defining characteristic of the Brushfoot family (Nymphalidae) of butterflies. If you look even closer, you can see one of the greatly reduced front legs covered with white fur, held close to the body between the flat part of the "front" leg and the eye. Maybe it's a T.rex thing! (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2006)

File.Name: 6624d_6623-109.jpg
Notes: I don't think I've ever gotten so close to a Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly. Usually they are aggressive and sit on a perch to chase away other butterflies - and even birds and photographers! This Admiral let me get very close, and of course a branch was in the way. If I tried to move the branch, it would fly away for sure. Instead I moved in even closer. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2006)

File.Name: 6624e_6624-011.jpg
Notes: Tiny blue Damselfly (Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) resting on a dry grass stalk. Damselflies fold their wings when they perch, but their cousin dragonflies keep them open. They are preditors that grab their prey in the air between the bristles on their front legs. I always wonder why they have such bright colors. Maybe they are so fast, the color doesn't matter? (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2006)

File.Name: 6624f_6623-123.jpg
Notes: Any guide book will tell you that Spring is the time for wildflowers in California. But we have a rich flora of summer flowers that are just starting to kick in even though it won't rain for months. This is beauriful native Canchalagua (Centaurium venustum) in the unusual (for us) Gentian (Gentianaceae) family. It's growing by a seep in the chaparral. It has an ancient name that suggests medicinal uses. It's not common. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2006)

File.Name: 6625a_6625-026.jpg
Notes:
I believe this is a female Lesser Goldfinch (Carduelis psaltria) bird munching new seeds of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. I love this native shrub - it feeds the birds and butterflies and propagates everywhere. That's what Peter Kropotkin called "mutual aid - a factor in evolution". (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2006)

I found a Lesser Goldfinch nest last year. See my photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=10564896
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=10664003

File.Name: 6625b_6625-033.jpg
Notes:
Little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working a native flower of Farewell-to-Spring aka Speckled Clarkia (Clarkia cylindrica) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. These little bees dive to the bottom of the flower looking for a bit of nectar (I guess), then they dance and shake to collect pollen. This one has a full load! (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2006)

Hard to photograph this action. I gave up trying to follow the bees from flower to flower always a step behind. I pre-focused and waited. Bingo!
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Farewell-to-Spring; Speckled Clarkia; Evening Primrose

File.Name: 6625c_6624-001.jpg
Notes:
Like a flying pin - this tiny Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae in the order Hymenoptera) is working new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the canyon. They use that long ovipositor tail to deposit eggs inside their prey to mature as internal parasites - even if the prey happens to be hidden inside a plant stem or coccoon. Hard to photograph. Blink and they disappear! (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2006)

I think this is the same kind of wasp that I captured as victim to an Ambush Bug a few days ago. Compare my photos starting at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=14286290
CommonNames: Ichneumon Wasp; California Buckwheat; Ambush Bug

File.Name: 6625d_6624-014.jpg
Notes: Little immature Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Acrididae) watching me as I try to watch him. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2006)

File.Name: 6625e_6625-032.jpg
Notes: New Woodland Cicada (family Homoptera, family Cicadidae - maybe genus Platypedia?) in the woods. Our local cicadas don't sing - they just "click click click" by rattling their wings together, like stroking your comb or a samba Guiro instrument. They are really hard to find in the woods - they stop clicking whenever you get close! (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2006)

File.Name: 6625f_6625-029.jpg
Notes:
The twinkie that ate our firewood! No wait, it gets better - it's also known as the "Dog Vomit Slime Mold" - really! This is NOT a fungus - for one reason, it can move. This is the slime mold Fuligo septica in the order Physarales of the fascinating Myxomycetes. This photo shows the mobile plasmodium stage becoming the solid lime-encrusted spore-bearing aethalium stage. It really does feel like a twinkie!

It's more of a "them" than an "it". They live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in a substrate like this oak log in our woodpile. But sometimes they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form this visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" that can crawl along like a slug. It finally dries and forms a mushroom-like "aethalium" structure to release tiny spores that will disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy for these critters' prime mission - to carry mother nature's seed to a new decaying log in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2006)

There's more good info on the web from Wayne Armstrong:
   http://waynesword.palomar.edu/slime1.htm
ScientificName: Fuligo septica; Physarales; Myxomycetes; plasmodium; aethalium
associatedTaxa: Fuligo septica; Physarales; Myxomycetes; plasmodium; aethalium

File.Name: 6626a_6626-051.jpg
Notes:
I learned something today. This is not an ant (order Hymenoptera) but a remarkable "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) ant-mimic on a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the garden. You can tell it's not a real ant by the long antennae and the long sucking proboscis mouth-part folded under it's head. The closer you look, the more differences you see.

The mimicry is remarkable. It has a green bottom to disguise it's fat waist rather than the usual narrow ant "petiole", and it has a faux "nodal bump" made by the upturned "scutellum" - that triangular piece you see between the upper wings of all true bugs. See my [Previous] two photos of the same critter. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2006)

Ants are so common - I'm not surprised there are so many insect (and spider) ant-mimics. There's a word for it: "Myrmecomorphy". Don't bother looking in your dictionary, but try a web search to learn a lot more:
   http://www.google.com/search?q=myrmecomorphy
Locality: and spider) ant-mimics. There's a word for it: "Myrmecomorphy". Don't bother looking in your dictionary

File.Name: 6626b_6626-047.jpg
Notes: This is a "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) - but it's a remarkable ant-mimic. Real ants have a narrow "petiole" between abdomen and thorax, and most native ants have one or two "nodal" bumps between them. This Hemiptera mimics the petiole with its green bottom, and it mimics the ants' node with it's upturned "scutellum" - that triangular piece you see between the upper wings of all true bugs. This is remarkable mimicry! (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2006)

File.Name: 6626c_6626-054.jpg
Notes: Sure looks like an ant in the garden - but it isn't! Try to figure it out if you're seeing this photo first, then see my [Next] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2006)

File.Name: 6626d_6626-055.jpg
Notes: Like a little pony prancing on a crinkly field of flower petals! This is an immature Long-Horned Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) - maybe a Katydid or Cricket - prancing on a flower of Flanders Field Poppy aka Shirley Poppy aka Field Poppy (Papaver rhoeas) in the garden this morning. Life is good. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2006)
CommonNames: Long-Horned Grasshopper; Katydid; Cricket; Flanders Field Poppy; Shirley Poppy; Field Poppy

File.Name: 6626e_6626-057.jpg
Notes: This Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) was absolutely motionless in this flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the canyon. I got this one photo up close, and *bam* it was gone. I believe the bee was just overwhelmed for a moment by the beauty of it all. I know the feeling! (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2006)

File.Name: 6626f_6626-060.jpg
Notes: Be the flower, man... I guess it's an old hippie thing - but this Beetle (Coleoptera) understands. The flower is native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2006)
ScientificName: Coleoptera) understands. The flower is native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum
