File.Name: 6627a_6627-073.jpg
Notes:
Eye to eye with this little Damselfly (Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) resting on an old wood stake in the garden. You can't sneak up on these critters - they see everything. But if you wait quietly, they often come back to the same perch again and again. This one came very close. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2006)

Those eyes... we learn that insects have "primitive" compound eyes, but when there are tens of thousands of facets, that's not so different from a retina. You can gaze into these eyes - like the eyes of a lover. What is that faux "retina" - the dark center that seems to follow me around?

File.Name: 6627b_6627-077.jpg
Notes: Little Damselfly (Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata). It's perching on a stake in the garden. Damselflies fold their wings when they perch, but their cousin dragonflies keep them open. You can be sure it's checking me out with that amazing eye. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2006)

File.Name: 6627c_6627-078.jpg
Notes:
One of those nasty nickel-sized Horse Flies (Tabanus punctifer or kin) working a flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. This one wasn't nasty at all. I lifted it up with my finger and moved it to a better flower for the photo. Maybe this is a male and only the females need the "blood meal" to produce eggs? (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2006)

These big biting flies are disturbing. If you count off the legs in pairs - left and right, from tail to head - you will spot the odd extra appendage. It's not a stinger, but it sure hurts!

File.Name: 6627d_6627-083.jpg
Notes: You gotta look close to notice the Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the lower-right corner of this native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) flower. It sure looks like another floret in this composite - it's set like a mousetrap ready to grab its prey! (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2006)

File.Name: 6627e_6626-046.jpg
Notes: Tiny Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) about to dive into the stamens of this Oriental Poppy (Papaver somniferum) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2006)

File.Name: 6627f_6623-107.jpg
Notes:
Immature green Long-Horned Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) in the garden. Sharper isn't always better for photos! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2006)

I wonder if this is the same critter I show here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=1059439

File.Name: 6628a_6628-093.jpg
Notes:
Interesting little Wasp (order Hymenoptera) working the Yarrow (Achillea) flowers in the garden. It has a big face in front of its eyes - made it hard to focus - and it seems to have a beard of hairs on its face to collect pollen from the flowers. I watched this wasp for a while. It was snuffling the flowers for pollen, not trying to suck nectar. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2006)

Any idea what this is Nano?

File.Name: 6628b_6628-098.jpg
Notes: Two tiny flies (order Diptera) working one of the last flowers of the native Phacelia - maybe Phacelia cicutaria? - on the dry side of our garden. These little flies look so similar, but one is looking up for pollen, and the other is diving down for nectar. That's a big difference! (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2006)

File.Name: 6628c_6628-103.jpg
Notes: Three tiny "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) nymphs on a pair of prickly seeds of Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine) in the garden. I guess two different species, but they got along just fine. This cozy scene is really tiny - a deep crop into a 1:1 macro shot. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2006)

File.Name: 6628d_6628-088.jpg
Notes:
I don't know what this native Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly caterpillar is doing. Here it is sitting upright on its non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) foodplant, touching its "fingers" together like it's pondering something important. Maybe it's time to transform? (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2006)

I did see my very first adult Anise Swallowtail butterfly of the summer flutter by today!

File.Name: 6628e_6628-102.jpg
Notes:
Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) working new flowers of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula var. vacillans) in the canyon. It's an unusual kind of Sphinx Moth (order lepidoptera, family Sphingidae) related to "tomato worms". This uncommon native moth works the day shift. It really does have clear wings without scales. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2006)

It's one of my ongoing summer challenges to get a sharp photo of this Clearwing Moth. This isn't it. These amazing moths dart hover at flowers like little hummingbirds and never stop moving. I'll keep trying!
CommonNames: Snowberry Clearwing Moth; California Honeysuckle; Sphinx Moth
ScientificName: Hemaris diffinis; Lonicera hispidula; vacillans; lepidoptera; Sphingidae
associatedTaxa: Hemaris diffinis; Lonicera hispidula; vacillans; lepidoptera; Sphingidae

File.Name: 6628f_6627-084.jpg
Notes: Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly diving into a composite flower head of California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the canyon. This is still our most common butterfly by far. I've already taken many photos, but sometimes I can't resist. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2006)

File.Name: 6629a_6629-112.jpg
Notes:
Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working native flowers of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum). The butterfly moves around the flower counter-clockwise collecting male pollen from the stamens on the underside ribs of its wings. At some point in the circle, it passes the female stigma at the end of the pistil - visible in the lower left of this photo - and pollination happens. These butterflies and flowers are such a perfect match. I won't argue evolution and design here, but this is what it's all about! See my [Previous] two photos for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2006)

The Humboldt Lilies in our canyon have all fallen prey to some critter that destroys the stem bud and keeps them from flowering. The beautiful flowers are also vulnerable to people cutting the stems. I found these flowers along San Jose Creek, one canyon west of home. Our swallowtails are doing fine on other flowers at home, but I wonder if they feel something missing.

File.Name: 6629b_6629-122.jpg
Notes: Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working native flowers of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum). It's interesting how the butterfly looks for nectar between the petals on top rather than at the base of the stamens below. You can see the pollen on its wings. They wouldn't rub the stamens with their wings to tranfer pollen if they came from below. How do they know to do it right? See my [Next] and [Previous] photos for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2006)

File.Name: 6629c_6629-121.jpg
Notes: Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working native flowers of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum). Butterfly and flower are a perfect match. Look how the pollen transfers from flower stamens to the thick ribs of butterfly wings! See my [Next] two photos for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2006)

File.Name: 6629d_6629-126.jpg
Notes: Little web-spinning caterpillars on native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia in the Scrophulariaceae family). Now I'm really confused. I've been assuming these web-spinner caterpillars are the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies that have been so abundent in the canyon this year. Now I'm not sure. I have recent photos of the butterflies mating and ovipositing and even spinning webs. I hope this means we'll have a second brood. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2006)

File.Name: 6629e_6629-133.jpg
Notes: Looking into a single flower of native Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei in the Lily (Liliaceae) family). There are hundreds or thousands of flowers on each flowering stalk. Our yuccas die after flowering, but they usually leave "pup" plants behind to carry on. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2006)

File.Name: 6629f_6629-137.jpg
Notes: Oriental Poppy (Papaver somniferum) seed head in the garden with a new flower behind. This traditional garden flower has many names. It's maybe the most "architectural" flower of all, going back to the tops of ancient greek columns. In architecture, "poppy heads" are still "the finials or other ornaments which terminate the tops of bench ends, either to pews or stalls" - whatever that means! (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2006)

File.Name: 6630a_6630-154.jpg
Notes:
Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) working native flowers of Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. This uncommon native moth works the day shift, and works flowers lke a hummingbird. It really does have clear wings without scales. It's an unusual kind of Sphinx Moth (order lepidoptera, family Sphingidae) related to "tomato worms". They hover like hummingbirds and never sit still. I'm proud of this photo! (Santa Ynez River, 30 June 2006)

I went to the river early this morning, and it was already windy. We have dangerous "fire weather" today - dry and windy. Since everything is already moving with the wind, I went for photos of insects in the air. I got a few!
CommonNames: Snowberry Clearwing Moth; Mustang Mint; Mint; Sphinx Moth
ScientificName: Hemaris diffinis; Monardella lanceolata; Lamiaceae; lepidoptera; Sphingidae
associatedTaxa: Hemaris diffinis; Monardella lanceolata; Lamiaceae; lepidoptera; Sphingidae

File.Name: 6630b_6630-154.jpg
Notes: Flower head of native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family on a dry flat near the river. There is a visiting Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) working the flowers - see my [Next] photo for a closer photo of the moth. This annual has the most intense "peppermint" smell I know from any plant, up a notch from Dr. Bronner's, not quite pleasant. It's one of those "rarely common" rare plants that really is common here and there along the river if you find them. The flower heads have a cup-like involucre. They almost look like composites until you look very close, but your nose will tell you it's a mint. (Santa Ynez River, 30 June 2006)
CommonNames: Mustang Mint; Mint; Snowberry Clearwing Moth

File.Name: 6630c_6630-155.jpg
Notes: Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) approaching native flowers of Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. See my [Next] two photos. (Santa Ynez River, 30 June 2006)
CommonNames: Snowberry Clearwing Moth; Mustang Mint; Mint

File.Name: 6630d_6630-161.jpg
Notes:
Incoming Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae) in the air approaching a flower head of native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. What an impressive feat to guide that prodigious proboscis into the narrow tube of a single mint flower! (Santa Ynez River, 30 June 2006)

Interesting how the fly holds its rear legs out from its body like skids on a sled. I've seen this before, e.g. my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=10372501

File.Name: 6630e_6630-160.jpg
Notes: Incoming Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae) engaging it's long rigid proboscis into a native flower of Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata). Note the spread of its hind legs and see my [Next] photo. (Santa Ynez River, 30 June 2006)

File.Name: 6630f_6630-163.jpg
Notes:
Little Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae) hovering before a flower head of native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) with proboscis fully engaged. It vibrates its wings more like a helicopter than a butterfly. See my [Next] two photos for more. (Santa Ynez River, 30 June 2006)

I took all of today's photos on a dry flat near the river on a windy day, and they all involve this uncommon flower in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. It has the most intense "peppermint" smell of any plant I know, up a notch from Dr. Bronner's!
Locality: Lamiaceae) family. It has the most intense "peppermint" smell of any plant I know

File.Name: 6701a_6701-012.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of Scarlet Larkspur (Delpinum cardinale) with the sky behind. I did no tricks with levels or saturation for this photo. (I tried - didn't work!) Our climate is winter-wet and summer-dry. Summer flowers like these break all the rules. I guess it's a hummingbird flower, but I didn't see any visitors today. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2006)

I had a toe-hold on a steep hillside to take this photo. I kept slipping down and was worried about getting back up! This is another of those "rare" - meaning "rarely common" - wildflowers. There were many here, dozens of flowers per plant - but not easy to get to!

File.Name: 6701b_6701-019.jpg
Notes: Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly reeling in its proboscis on a flower cluster of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon Traskiae). These are still our most common butterflies by far. I can't resist taking new photos! (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2006)

File.Name: 6701c_6701-032.jpg
Notes: This was a new butterfly for me, so I hit my books. I'm pretty sure it's a worn male Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly. My books say the males appear before females - that checks - also that the food plant is Gooseberries (Ribes sp. in the Grossulariaceae family) - that doesn't check. This butterfly is perched on a native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) - see the new acorn. I chased this little butterfly for a while before I got this photo, and it never left this oak. The butterfly is territorial. It kept coming back to the same perches. I saw a few more once I noticed, always on scrub oak. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2006)
CommonNames: Tailed Copper; Arota Copper; Gooseberries; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Lycaena arota; Ribes; Grossulariaceae; Quercus berberidifolia
associatedTaxa: Lycaena arota; Ribes; Grossulariaceae; Quercus berberidifolia

File.Name: 6701d_6701-020.jpg
Notes:
Little Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae) working a flower of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon Traskiae). It vibrates its wings more like a helicopter than a butterfly - so fast they become invisible. See my photos [Yesterday] to appreciate how long that proboscis is! (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2006)

This plant is our mainland form of a shrub mostly limited to Santa Catalina Island. It's named for Blanche Trask, one of the most interesting early California naturalists. Try a web search to learn more about this fascinating lady - or just click here:
   http://www.geocities.com/Yosemite/Gorge/5604/blanchetraskanthology.htm

File.Name: 6701e_6630-150.jpg
Notes: A smaller Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. Look close. Start with the bug's eye and follow the proboscis down... it's skewered a little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) with head to the right. I got too close with my camera, and it took off - with prey. (Santa Ynez River, 30 June 2006)

File.Name: 6701f_6630-165.jpg
Notes:
Male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterfly taking off from a flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) on the edge of the garden. Look close - it's reeling in it's proboscis, only touching with its right legs, and it's wings are slightly open showing that the top is not plain yellow. Is the butterfly flipping over as it takes off? I had my camera set up on tripod, manual focus, and my finger down on the button shooting 3 frames per second. He's feeding in the previous photo of the series, and he's gone in the next! These butterflies are really fast! (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2006)

It's my summer challenge to capture the upper wings of this amazing butterfly. I reckon I got my other challenge - the Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) - in the [Previous] photo. Thanks for noticing Schattenlicht! I took a hundred photos to get this one (at 3 fps). Usually I keep about 1 in 10 photos and show about 1 in 5 of those. Sometimes you only get one chance. Gotta love digital!
CommonNames: California Dogface; Canyon Sunflower; Snowberry Clearwing Moth
ScientificName: Zerene eurydice; Venegasia carpesioides; Hemaris diffinis
associatedTaxa: Zerene eurydice; Venegasia carpesioides; Hemaris diffinis

File.Name: 6702a_6702-046.jpg
Notes: Tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hanging on the long exerted stamens of a flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. It's a big flower with very long stamens and style that extend well beyond the flower. Some little bees and flies skip the throat of the flower and go right for the pollen. This little spider is ready. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2006)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Woolly Blue Curls; Mint

File.Name: 6702b_6702-065.jpg
Notes: Looking into a flower head of native Red Thistle (Cirsium occidentale - maybe var. venustum?) - like looking into the universe. Don't confuse the brilliant native thistles with the weedy ones and pull them by mistake! I didn't fake this - see my [Previous} photo. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2006)

File.Name: 6702c_6702-068.jpg
Notes: Brilliant red flower head of Red Thistle (Cirsium occidentale) - maybe var. venustum? I posted this photo mostly to show I didn't fake the [Next] photo! I found this flower near La Cumbre Peak today, the high point of our mountains above Santa Barbara. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2006)

File.Name: 6702d_6702-056.jpg
Notes:
Gotta look close to understand this - it's a mating pair of Ambush Bugs - family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera - maybe the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). The plant is flowering native California Goldenrod (Solidago californica). It must be hard for such nasty preditors to get this close together! I reckon he's on top - she seems larger with eggs - except "on top" is "on the bottom" in this photo since they are hanging upside down. Does that make sense? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2006)

I have another photo of mating Ambush Bugs at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=10573192
CommonNames: Ambush Bugs; Pacific Ambush Bug; California Goldenrod; Ambush Bugs
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Solidago californica
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Solidago californica

File.Name: 6702e_6702-083.jpg
Notes:
Tiny mystery insect resting on a manzanita leaf. When it flies, I only see the long white tails with a blur in front, like a flying "V" shape. I caught one in my hand and it seemed to fall apart - but then it flew away with naked tails - just threads, leaving the fluff behind - and it seemed fine. My first thought was a moth, but looking at this and my [Previous] photo, I think it might be a Fly - order Diptera - it only seems to have two wings. I saw several of these critters flying near La Cumbre Peak today, the high point of our mountains. I've seen them at home too. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2006)

Here's a stumper for you Living_details! N@no, Chisburgue, anyone - do you recognize this critter?

File.Name: 6702f_6702-079.jpg
Notes: This photo probably doesn't make sense until you see my [Next] photo and come back. It's a tiny mystery insect with long white cottony tails that make a "V" shape when it flies. I caught this one at rest on a flowering Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) shrub. It had trouble taking off! This photo shows how the long tails are attached. It sure looks like it only has one wing on the side. Is it a strange cottony Fly - order Diptera? I saw several of these critters flying near La Cumbre Peak today, the high point of our mountains. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2006)

File.Name: 6703a_6703-117.jpg
Notes: Another immature Long-Horned Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) - maybe a Katydid or Cricket. It's munching a female style of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the canyon near the creek. All insects have six legs, but those mouthparts - "palps" - work like extra hands! (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2006)
CommonNames: Long-Horned Grasshopper; Katydid; Cricket; California Thistle
ScientificName: Orthoptera; Tettigoniidae; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; palps
associatedTaxa: Orthoptera; Tettigoniidae; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; palps

File.Name: 6703b_6703-097.jpg
Notes: Little Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Acrididae) wishing he could hide behind the narrow leaves of this native Rush in the Juncaceae family. What a face! This is a wetland plant growing at an unusual seep along the summit ridge of our summer-dry mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2006)

File.Name: 6703c_6701-027.jpg
Notes: Tiny web-spinning caterpillars taking over a branch of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia in the Scrophulariaceae family) flowering in the canyon. They are disturbing in a sci-fi movie kind of way, but they really only take over a branch here and there - they don't kill the whole plant. I thought these web-spinner caterpillars are the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies that have been so abundent in the canyon this year. Now I'm not sure. I'll watch what happens. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2006)

File.Name: 6703d_6703-108.jpg
Notes: Sometimes I need to be reminded why the "Blue" (subfamily Polyommatinae) butterflies deserve their name. This veteran Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly is missing part of it's wing, so the deep blue upper wing shows through. When they fly, you see the blue. When they land for a photo with wings folded, you mostly see the gray. This blue really is a veteran - the Spring Azures are one of our earliest butterflies. This blue is working flowers of White Sweet Clover (Melilotus albus) along our road. It's an introduced weed, but at least it supports life. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2006)
CommonNames: Blue; Spring Azure; Spring Azures; White Sweet Clover

File.Name: 6703e_6702-061.jpg
Notes: I believe these pretty blue flowers are Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus. This beautiful plant is not common, but there are many flowering at a still damp seep near the top of the Arroyo Burro trails. There's something disturbing about the "faces" of these flowers! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2006)

File.Name: 6703f_6702-048.jpg
Notes:
This is the same flower as my [Previous] photo - native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. I wonder whom this flower is designed for - certainly not this little Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae) that hardly even touches the flower as it hovers. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2006)

This flower must be designed for a large insect that weighs enough to land on the lower petal and trigger the fulcrum to bring the long exerted stamens and pistil down to its back - AND it must have a long enough proboscis to get to the nectar. Instead I see bee flies and butterflies. It would be an interesting bio-engineering project to work backwards from the flower and design the ideal pollinator it's meant for.
CommonNames: Woolly Blue Curls; Mint; Bee Fly

File.Name: 6704a_6704-122.jpg
Notes:
Little female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) working flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) on the dry side of our garden. This is not a classic "hummingbird flower" - the hummers visit all flowers in a pinch. This photo is a nice emblem for something. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2006)

We're heading out early to Santa Barbara Island tomorrow for a long day exploring!

File.Name: 6704b_6630-139.jpg
Notes: Little Damselfly (Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) resting on a flower head of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the canyon. The sharp bristles on this critter's legs are for catching flying prey in the air. Those eyes... tens of thousands of facets are not so different from a retina. You can gaze into them like the eyes of a lover. What makes eyes - human, animal, insect - so mysterious? (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2006)

File.Name: 6704c_6701-003.jpg
Notes: Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working flowers of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the garden, with the sun behind. I think this same butterfly is working the flowers every morning - it's missing its left tail so I can recognize it day after day. The wings are like panels of stained glass. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2006)

File.Name: 6704d_6703-098.jpg
Notes:
Unknown Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - genus Hesperia or kin) - butterfly working composite flowers of Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare). This noxious weed in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family is becoming more common every year in our mountains. I saw my first one in the mountains about 8 years ago on a burned hillside after a wildfire. Now it's spreading along Forest Service fire breaks. At least some butterflies like it. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2006)

Not to get political (on the Fourth of July!), but the NFS and CALTRANS are responsible for spreading noxious weeds that will cost millions of dollars to control in the future - IF it's still possible to control them. We need real "Weed Laws" and oversight in California!

File.Name: 6704e_6702-092.jpg
Notes: Male catkins of native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) in the Oak (Fagaceae) family. The erect or spreading staminate catkins distinguish it from the pendent catkins of the true oaks in the genus Quercus. (No comment ;=) See my [Previous] photo of the acorns. We have these trees in the canyon at home at 2,400 feet elevation, but I don't see many acorns. I got this photo near the top of La Cumbre Peak, near 4,000 feet, one of the high points of the mountain range. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2006)

File.Name: 6704f_6702-091.jpg
Notes: New acorns of native Tanbark Oak (Lithocarpus densiflorus). These beautiful not-quite-real-oak-trees in the Oak (Fagaceae) family have erect catkins and Chestnut-like bristles on the acorn cups. They remind me of something from the ocean. We have these trees in the canyon at home, but they only flower up high where I can't get close. I got this photo (and the [Next]) near the top of La Cumbre Peak, near 4,000 feet elevation. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2006)

File.Name: 6706a_6705-219.jpg
Notes:
Big male California Sea Lion (Zalophus californianus) at Landing Cove on Santa Barbara Island. It's a sea-dog! You can see its external ear - seals don't have them. (Santa Barbara Island, 5 July 2006)

Santa Barbara Island has been closed to visitors all year because the endangered Brown Pelican (Pelecanus occidentalis) birds started nesting here. Pelicans are everywhere! This is something new. We had the opportunity to visit the distant island with National Park Service biologists.

It should be a rule NOT to bring a camera on the first visit anywhere! After the 3+ hour boat ride, we only had 1-1/2 hour on the island. I didn't know what to expect, and I always seemed to have the wrong lens or tripod. Now I know. I want to go back!
Locality:
Pelecanus occidentalis) birds started nesting here. Pelicans are everywhere! This is something new. We had the opportunity to visit the distant island with National Park Service biologists.

It should be a rule NOT to bring a camera on the first visit anywhere! After the 3+ hour boat ride

File.Name: 6706b_6705-207.jpg
Notes: Tiny Western Pygmy-Blue (Brephidium exile) butterfly working a flower of Santa Barbara Island Buckwheat (Eriogonum giganteum ssp. compactum). This is a rare combination. It's the smallest butterfly in North America visiting flowers of an endemic buckwheat that only grows on the one-mile-square Santa Barbara Island! (Santa Barbara Island, 5 July 2006)
CommonNames: Western Pygmy-Blue; Santa Barbara Island Buckwheat

File.Name: 6706c_6705-177.jpg
Notes:
Fledgeling Brown Pelican (Pelecanus occidentalis) on its nest on Santa Barbara Island right along the only trail from the landing! The island has been officially closed by the NPS since winter because the endangered pelicans started nesting everywhere. We were lucky to be the first visitors to the island in the company of NPS biologists Dan and Sarah. There are sea lions on the "beach" below. This looks so prehistoric! (Santa Barbara Island, 5 July 2006)

I've seen how gulls and pelicans raise their young. We take them for granted, but these birds are so vulnerable with their open nests. In Santa Barbara, we have the Channel Islands National Park as our bird refuge. Looking up the coast, there are protected places at Point Conception, Point Buchon, Big Sur, and beyond. It's important to keep these places wild for the birds. I can't imagine the beach with pelicans!

File.Name: 6706d_6705-194.jpg
Notes: Immature Brown Pelicans (Pelecanus occidentalis) on Santa Barbara Island. I guess the "nest" is made in the middle of a clump of the endemic shrub Island Sagebrush (Artenisia nesiotica

File.Name: 6706e_6705-192.jpg
Notes: National Park Service biologists Dan and Sarah (I hope I got that right ;=) looking over the southern landscape of Santa Barbara Island. This is a strange barren landscape where the tallest "tree" is Giant Coreopsis (Coreopsis gigantea). The white spots are nests of gulls and Brown Pelicans (Pelecanus occidentalis). This island has been officially closed by the NPS since winter because the endangered pelicans started nesting here. Pelicans are everywhere! See my [Next] few photos. This is something new. (Santa Barbara Island, 5 July 2006)

File.Name: 6706f_6705-212.jpg
Notes:
This foot-high succulent growing on a bluff over the ocean doesn't look very special, but it really is remarkable. This is the Santa Barbara Island Live-forever (Dudleya traskiae) in the Stonecrop Crassulaceae family. It's endemic to Santa Barbara Island - a one-mile square wind-blown rock 55 miles out across the channel. It grows nowhere else without help. It took us 3+ hours just to get here from Ventura yesterday on a fast boat! (Santa Barbara Island, 5 July 2006)

This plant is named for Blanche Trask, one of the most interesting early California naturalists. Try a web search to learn more about this fascinating lady - or just click here:
   http://www.geocities.com/Yosemite/Gorge/5604/blanchetraskanthology.htm

File.Name: 6707a_6707-258.jpg
Notes:
Look close - but not too close! This venomous Southern Pacific Rattlesnake (Crotalus viridis helleri) is resting its head on a board in our woodpile. This is a tight crop - I didn't really get this close to it. These snakes look so nasty - they have a real "Don't f*ck with me" attitude! I did poke the wood to get a reaction, but it just stuck its tongue out and stayed in that tight curl. Two hours later it was still there. An hour after that, it was gone. I'll be careful in the garden tomorrow! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2006)

This is my fourth close encounter with a rattlesnake this year. That's four more than usual. I'm being careful where I step!

File.Name: 6707b_6706-244.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working the very first row of disk flowers of this composite Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. (The ray flowers are barren.) I love the shadows. Compare my [Previous] photo to see just how deep this butterfly has to go down to get to the good stuff in the tiny flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2006)

These checkerspot butterflies are disappearing fast. A week ago I could find a dozen or more working every Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. Now there's just a few. There are many web-spinning caterpillars on native Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia), so I hope we'll have a second brood. They are so photogenic!

It's an interesting year - hot and dry January/February followed by a cold and wet spring. I've seen few interesting flies. Usually we have a dozen Polistes paper wasp nests under the roof of our porch, but this year we have just one. Only one of our three House Wrens is trying for a second brood. Every year is different. That keeps it interesting. These daily fotolog photos are my way of documenting what I see, and a chance to compare year by year in the archive.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Gloriosa Daisy; Canyon Sunflower; Climbing Penstemon
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Rudbeckia hirta; Venegasia carpesioides; Keckiella cordifolia; Polistes
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Rudbeckia hirta; Venegasia carpesioides; Keckiella cordifolia; Polistes

File.Name: 6707c_6706-248.jpg
Notes: Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly going deep into one of the very first disk flowers of this Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. (The ray flowers are barren.) Note the two-laned proboscis. Compare my [Next] photo to see just how deep this butterfly is going! (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2006)

File.Name: 6707d_6707-261.jpg
Notes:
I'm confused by the "Blues" - those little butterflies in the Polyommatinae subfamily - but I'm pretty sure this is an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) resting on a flower of native trifoliate Small-Flowered Lotus (Lotus micranthus) - in the Pea Family (Fabaceae). There is California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) starting to flower about five feet away, but this butterfly was definately interested in the lotus. It has pretty iridescent blue spots around the black spots on its hind wing. This is the first blue I've seen this summer with that iridescence. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2006)

It looks so sad with those droopy antennae!
CommonNames: Blues; Acmon Blue; Small-Flowered Lotus; Pea Family; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Polyommatinae; Plebejus acmon; Lotus micranthus; Fabaceae; Eriogonum fasciculatum
associatedTaxa: Polyommatinae; Plebejus acmon; Lotus micranthus; Fabaceae; Eriogonum fasciculatum

File.Name: 6707e_6707-269.jpg
Notes: Often the little "Blues" - butterflies in the Polyommatinae subfamily - don't look blue at all in photos. They fold their wings when they perch - the only time to get a photo. I caught this one flexing its wings on a flower of native Small-Flowered Lotus (Lotus micranthus) - in the Pea Family (Fabaceae). I'll call it a Lupine Blue (Plebejus lupinus) just to distinguish it from the definate Acmon Blue (P. acmon) in the [Next] photo. To really identify these little butterflies, you have to catch them and kill them. Our whole system of taxonomy is based on "specimens in hand" and those characteristics that you can still see in faded 100 year old museum specimens. No thanks. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2006)
CommonNames: Blues; Small-Flowered Lotus; Pea Family; Lupine Blue; Acmon Blue
ScientificName: Polyommatinae; Lotus micranthus; Fabaceae; Plebejus lupinus; P. acmon
associatedTaxa: Polyommatinae; Lotus micranthus; Fabaceae; Plebejus lupinus; P. acmon

File.Name: 6707f_6706-233.jpg
Notes: Unknown Grass-Skipper (order Lepidoptera - subfamily Hesperiinae - genus Hesperia or kin) - butterfly working native flowers of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. There are many local species that all look about the same to me. I haven't tried to tell them apart. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2006)
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Hesperiinae; Hesperia; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Hesperiinae; Hesperia; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae

File.Name: 6708a_6708-014.jpg
Notes: Astounding new flower of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) starting to flower in the dry summer chaparral. This is a native Mariposa Lily in the Lily (Liliaceae) family. It's named for the amateur botanist Amos Weed (1828-1918) - definately not a weed! This uncommon flower is astounding for its color and form, also because it flowers in midsummer on bare mountain hills when the ground is bone dry. The flowers are quite variable. Compare my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2006)
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Mariposa Lily; Lily

File.Name: 6708b_6708-025.jpg
Notes: Astounding new flower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) starting to flower in the dry summer chaparral. This is a native Mariposa Lily in the Lily (Liliaceae) family, definately not a weed. The flowers are quite variable. Compare my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2006)
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Mariposa Lily; Lily

File.Name: 6708c_6708-006.jpg
Notes:
Here's another stumper photo for N@no and CHiS and anyone else who looks close at nature!

These are ripe berries of native Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) plant family. The berries are "sorta edible" - pleasantly sweet & sour at first, but they soon turn bitter. It's another "suck and spit" fruit, a great design for seed propagation! But these ripe berries have sprouted white whiskers! I've seen this before, and I figured that the whiskers are a fungus growing from the fruit. Not true. What are these whiskers that are even whiter than mine? (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2006)

File.Name: 6708d_6708-003.jpg
Notes:
I saw this "thing" on a rather bug-eaten leaf of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum Xanti). I first thought *insect* - then I thought *nah, just a seed*. (I'm past ready for new glasses.) I took the photo anyway to show how confusing seeds can be. Hah! It's an insect for sure - I guess order Homoptera - the strange ones. Looking closer at the photo, I see at least two more critters. There's one to the right underneath the rear of the big one, and another hanging down from the leaf at the bottom. No wonder the nightshades are looking so bug-eaten! (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2006)

Summer weeks of hot weather are finally taking a toll. Big insects are getting scarce. When I get closer looking for my daily photos, everything looks very strange!
ScientificName: Solanum Xanti).  I first thought *insect* - then I thought *nah, just a seed*.  (I'm past ready for new glasses.)  I took the photo anyway to show how confusing seeds can be.  Hah!  It's an insect for sure - I guess order Homoptera

File.Name: 6708e_6708-010.jpg
Notes: I'm sure this is an Argiope Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae - genus Argiope) perched upside-down on it's web, but I'm not sure which species. It's about the size of a quarter. You can see a bit of the spider's "stabilimentum" - that bright white zigzag structure - almost like smoke - at the bottom of the photo. As far as I know, all Argiope spiders produce this distinctive structure, though it's purpose is still a mystery. This spider seemed to have two webs. There's the neat "orb-web" it's perched on, but also a random web an inch or two away, parallel to the first, between the spider and my camera. You can see some out of focus strands in this photo. There's so much to learn about these critters! (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2006)

File.Name: 6708f_6707-260.jpg
Notes: Venomous Southern Pacific Rattlesnake (Crotalus viridis helleri) curled up in our woodpile. Don't wait to hear the rattle or see the rattles - you can recognize these dangerous snakes in a glance by the triangular shape of their head. This snake isn't large or small. I could hold it in both hands, but that would be a really dumb thing to do. It was very calm. It never hissed or rattled or even showed it's rattles, even when I poked the wood it's resting by. That's dangerous. Now it's out there *somewhere* near the garden. I've seen several rattlesnakes this summer. I watch where I step! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2006)

File.Name: 6709a_6709-050.jpg
Notes:
Unknown Grass-Skipper (order Lepidoptera - subfamily Hesperiinae - genus Hesperia or kin) - butterfly working native flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. This is another uncommon summer flower in our summer-dry mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2006)

I'm busy with school all spring when most of our native wildflowers are showing their stuff. I'm really interested in these unusual summer flowers that appear when it's even too hot and dry for the weeds. The woodland needs these late flowers to keep the insects alive for next year!
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Hesperiinae; Hesperia; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Hesperiinae; Hesperia; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: 6709b_6709-044.jpg
Notes: Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly perched on a stalk of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family in the canyon. Amazing eyes - the colorful faux eyes on the wings get your attention, but the diffraction patterns on the real eye are remarkable! Where did your eyes look first? (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2006)

File.Name: 6709c_6709-039.jpg
Notes: Look close before you land! This pretty Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) failed the test and became prey for the camouflaged Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hiding among the florets of this Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon. Notice that the fly has a slightly distended abdomen - like one of those Star Wars lightsaber toys. I hope it has already mated or laid eggs. Interesting flies are scarce this summer. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2006)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Crab Spider; Canyon Sunflower

File.Name: 6709d_6709-034.jpg
Notes: Tiny metallic green Fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon - and its shadow. Even the fly's eyes are green! (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2006)

File.Name: 6709e_6707-265.jpg
Notes: A pair of tiny flies in the woods - maybe Syrphid Flower Flies (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - caught in the act of conceiving more flies. It's a tender moment, but it makes me think of trailer hitches and ball joints! *Sigh* Fly reproductive anatomy is much more complicated than that. My camera was on the short tripod, but the flies were higher. This photo is handheld, tripod and all. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2006)

File.Name: 6709f_6708-006.jpg
Notes:
This is a very close crop into my already close stumper photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=14546739

The white "whiskers" seem to be growing from the anal openings of bristly little critters that look like the seeds that get stuck in my socks. The closer you look in this photo, the more critters you see. N@no, this does not rule out a fungus, but the insect-fungi (aka "entomopathogenic fungi") that I know usually erupt from all seams rather than a single functional opening like this. I'm impressed that the "whiskers" have a structure - they are "septate" - divided into chambers. I notive that some are attached to the berry without an insect. My favorite critter is in the very top-right of the photo - it's something different, looks like a little white bear holding a bit of "whisker" in it's mouth! (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2006)

Ain't nature grand - there's mystery and discovery all around us!

File.Name: 6710a_6710-083.jpg
Notes: I learned something today... This furry bee-mimic Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae) is working composite flowers of Yarrow (Achillea) in the garden. It has a short proboscis, but it's picking up pollen with its feet and transfering it to its mouth. It's the motto for nature as well as computer programming: "There's always another way!" (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2006)

File.Name: 6710b_6702-075.jpg
Notes:
This is another photo of my stumper critter from last week. See my photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=14446159 and [Previous] to that. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 July 2006)

CHiS from Brazil - http://www.fotolog.com/chisburgue_/ - recognized my mystery insect. It's order Homoptera: Sternorryncha: Coccoidea: Pseudococcidae: genus Pseudococcus. It's a Mealy Bug. Everything fits - the males have just two wings like flies and "two conspicuous, long, white anal filaments or tails". They have a complicated life cycle - search the web for details. Thank you CHiS - you deserve credit and glory for this. I love the web for letting us answer questions across the world!

I'm still looking for a photo or drawing on the web or in my books just to be sure, and I want to learn a little more. My classic book on "Insects of Western North America" (1936) by E.O. Essig mentions the "White Sage Mealybug" (Pseudcoccus crawi) and the "Eriogonum Mealybug" (Pseudcoccus eriogoni). Neither critter has a tail so much longer than body size as this.

Looking at the mealybug photos in my book, I wonder if this could be the critter in my other stumper photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=14546739
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=14560884

I'll leave it for you smart college kids - CHiS and N@no and all the rest of you - to figure this out. I'd rather go for a hike in the canyon and get new photos!
Locality:

File.Name: 6710c_6710-070.jpg
Notes:
I literally walked into this photo - a big Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider web across my trail. Then I did a double-take - the spider was hanging motionless from its own web. The Spider Wasp (Pompilidae in the order Hymenoptera) came back in a minute or two. A moment later both wasp and spider fell to the ground and I lost them. I was lucky to get this fast photo.

The spider who captures insect prey and paralyses them for her young has been captured and paralyzed by the wasp to feed her own young! Nature is one tough mother. Watch where you step! (Santa Ynez River, 10 July 2006)

File.Name: 6710d_6710-060.jpg
Notes: Golden Bumblebee (Bombus, order Hymenoptera) snuggling into native summer flowers of Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. The flower heads look a lot like composites - they even have a cup-like "involucre" to hold the flowers. I'm really intrigued by these native "summer flowers" that only appear after every trace of winter rain is gone. (Santa Ynez River, 10 July 2006)

File.Name: 6710e_6710-066.jpg
Notes: An opening flower bud of Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Nightshade (Solanaceae) family growing on a dry flat near the river. Is that sacred geometry or an insect rave? (Santa Ynez River, 10 July 2006)

File.Name: 6710f_6710-068.jpg
Notes:
I'm pretty sure this butterfly is a female Checkered White (Pontia protodice) butterfly - the first I've seen this year. It's ovipositing on a non-native Wild Mustard (Brassica sp.) on a flat near the river. This butterfly has more black spots than the common introduced Cabbage White (Pieris rapae butterflies. This photo is not quite sharp - me too! (Santa Ynez River, 10 July 2006)

What did the native White butterflies do before Father Serra brought mustard to California?
CommonNames: Checkered White; Wild Mustard; Cabbage White; White

This is another of our unusual summer-desert flowers that start flowering when the ground is bone dry and spring rains are just a memory. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2006)

File.Name: 6711b_6711-099.jpg
Notes: Gotta look close to understand this photo - here are two mating Ambush Bugs - family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera - maybe Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica) hiding in the new flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) by our mailbox. While they were "doing it", she speared a little beetle with her proboscis! I took about 30 photos of these bugs to get two keepers, and that beetle is not there in the early photos. Mating must be a real problem for these solitary preditors. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2006)

File.Name: 6711c_6711-105.jpg
Notes: Here are two just-finished-mating Ambush Bugs - family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera - maybe Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica) hiding in the new flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) by our mailbox. This sure looks like a tender moment between these awesome preditors. It looks like "he" - the darker and greener bug at the bottom of the photo(?) - is whispering to his lady either "Thank you m'am" or "let's get together when that #%&$ photographer is gone"! A moment later, they went their separate ways. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2006)

File.Name: 6711d_6711-090.jpg
Notes: Mating Grasshoppers (order Orthoptera, family Acrididae) on summer-dry Deerweed (Lotus scoparius). Pollinating insects are getting scarce as the mountains dry out, but there are grasshoppers everywhere! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2006)

File.Name: 6711e_6711-089.jpg
Notes: This looks like a photo of a little Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Acrididae) hanging on a plant in the dry chaparral. Nope. It's really a photo of the bright red spider-relative Mite (Acari) that seems to be sucking the grasshopper's toe/tarsus - look at the very bottom of the photo in the center! I zoomed very close in the original photo - it's definately sucking like a tick rather than hitchhiking. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2006)

File.Name: 6711f_6711-077.jpg
Notes:
New summer flowers of native Rothrock`s Lobelia (Lobelia dunnii var. serrata) - now included in the Bellflower (Campanulaceae) family. I got this photo on the bank where the creek in Lewis Canyon meets the river. I'm not sure, but I usually find these uncommon flowers where the creek has lime that builds up on the rocks. (Santa Ynez River, 10 July 2006)

I'm really interested in our summer flowers. We have a Mediterranean climate with winter-rain and summer-desert. But there are always new flowers, often in unusual plant families like this. I think these summer flowers are a clue to our climate history - and they keep the insects alive!

File.Name: 6712a_6712-129.jpg
Notes:
Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) working summer flowers of native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family on a dry flat near the river. I was looking for flowers rather than insects today, but I will always go for a photo of these remarkable daytime moths that hover like hummingbirds! One shot - I was lucky! (Santa Ynez River, 12 July 2006)

I went to the Santa Ynez River today - the section known as "Paradise" - good name despite the weeds. I'm looking for the summer flowers that wait until the rain is done and this becomes summer-dry desert country. It's remarkable how many summer flowers we have. They're important to keep the insects alive to work the (mostly) spring wildflowers. I'm cataloging. This might become my school science fair project for next year.
CommonNames: Snowberry Clearwing Moth; Mustang Mint; Mint

File.Name: 6712b_6712-135.jpg
Notes:
Remarkable native flowers of Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala). This is an unusual composite (family Asteraceae) plant with heads of two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know. The head-high plants have weak stems that fall over and look weedy, but the flowers are beautiful up close. (Santa Ynez River, 12 July 2006)

Just by chance, the [Next] photo shows Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. The flower heads look a lot like composites - they even have a cup-like "involucre" to hold the flowers.

File.Name: 6712c_6712-139.jpg
Notes: New flower head of native Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale - maybe var. venustum?). The non-native thstles are invasive weeds that destroy the woods. But the natives are remarkable wildflowers like this. Be sure you know the difference if you are clearing weeds! (Santa Ynez River, 12 July 2006)

File.Name: 6712d_6712-119.jpg
Notes: Pretty new flowers of Scarlet Monkeyflower (Mimulus cardinalis) in the Scrophulariaceae family growing along a little still-running creek that joins the river near the Second Crossing at Paradise. The flowers look built for something big like bumblebees, but all I saw were tiny beetles. Summer insects are still scarce this year. No one at the river is complaining! (Santa Ynez River, 12 July 2006)

File.Name: 6712e_6712-114.jpg
Notes: This beautiful native summer flower is a Blazing Star (Mentzelia laevicaulis) in the Blazing Star (Loasaceae) family. The flowers are the size of my hand, and the foliage feels like sandpaper. It's growing on a steep dry exposed shale slope above the river at Paradise. It was windy in the morning, so I almost didn't stop for the photo. When I came back in the afternoon, the flowers were all closed. I guess these flowers are night-bloomers like Evening Primrose. (Santa Ynez River, 12 July 2006)

File.Name: 6712f_6712-148.jpg
Notes:
This is about 1 square centimeter of my socks after hiking at Paradise by the river today. I forgot my gaiters - those fabric things that cover shoes and protect against snow and weeds. I'll probably have to burn these socks, and it will take an hour to clean my shoes so I don't carry the seeds on my next hike into the woods. Most of the bristly seeds are Hedge Parsley (Torilis arvensis or maybe T. nodosa). This is a bad invasive weed that takes the fun out walking into the woods. There's a few foxtail-type grass seeds (Bromus) here too - the ones that hurt. There's no mystery how these weeds spread, but I sure don't understand why there's no National Forest policy in place to stop it. (Santa Ynez River, 12 July 2006)

We MUST clean our shoes and socks after hiking through weeds. Try a web search for "weed gaiters":
   http://www.google.com/search?q=weed+gaiters

File.Name: 6713a_6713-169.jpg
Notes:
Brilliant summer flowers of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae family - like a choir. The flowers grow out, but I'm down low looking up into the sun. These are classic hummingbird flowers, but I didn't see any visitors today.

I'm really interested in these late flowers that wait to flower in our desert-dry summer when the spring wildflowers are all done. They keep the insects (and hummingbirds) alive!

Warning - the rest of today's photos are all nasty (but beautiful) preditor insects and spiders!

File.Name: 6713b_6713-179.jpg
Notes: Little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for a kill in the garden. I'm not sure what the flower is - a smaller Black-Eyed Susan or Cone flower in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. I guess these spiders are color-shifters. It sure does match the flower! Look close and see that the spider is missing a claw/leg on it's right side. I see this often, I reckon it's "the one who got away". (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2006)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Black-Eyed Susan; Cone flower; Composite

File.Name: 6713c_6713-174.jpg
Notes:
Unknown "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) perched on a flower in the garden. I'm not sure what the flower is - a smaller Black-Eyed Susan or Cone flower in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. Remarkable how insect and flower are a perfect color match. My first thought was that it's a yellow Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera) - it has those sharp-pointed "shoulders" - but the front legs are all wrong. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2006)

Something I don't understand... Look at the dark proboscis coming down from the front of the head. Just left (in front) of it you can see another very thin mouthpart structure with a forked tip. What is this??
CommonNames: True Bug; Black-Eyed Susan; Cone flower; Composite; Ambush Bug

File.Name: 6713d_6713-151.jpg
Notes: Tiny Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera) waiting for a victim among flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the woods. Those amazing crab-like claws on its front legs! These awesone preditors can bring down prey an order of magnitude larger. An ant wandered by as I watched, but the Ambush Bug didn't even blink. I guess they're selective as well as aggresive. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2006)
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; California Buckwheat; Ambush Bug

File.Name: 6713e_6713-161.jpg
Notes:
Interesting "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) perched on an old flower stalk of native Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) plant family in the woods. My first thought was Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. It's big, nearly an inch long - maybe 20 mm. It has a preditor look - you can see it's proboscis folded under its head. But look at the leaf-like structure on its hind leg. I believe this is a Western Leaf-footed Plant Bug (Leptoglossus clpeatus) in the Squash Bug (Coreidae) family of the order Hemiptera.

My classic book on "Insects of Western North America" (1936) by E.O. Essig says that this bug can be a pest on commercial crops. I wonder. The false "leaf" on its hind tibia sure looks bug-eaten. That would be good camouflage for harvesting Mealy Bugs (Pseudococcidae in the order Homoptera) on this plant. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2006)
CommonNames: True Bug; Sugar Bush; Sumac; Assassin Bug; Western Leaf-footed Plant Bug; Squash Bug; Mealy Bugs

File.Name: 6713f_6713-157.jpg
Notes:
My first thought was that this is an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. It's nearly an inch long - maybe 20 mm. It has that preditor look and checkered abdomen, maybe it's sucking the tiny Mealy Bugs (Pseudococcidae in the order Homoptera) on these fruit of native Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) plant family.

But maybe not. Look very close at the hind legs. It has enlarged leaf-like tibia that - along with the colors - match the description for for the Western Leaf-footed Plant Bug (Leptoglossus clpeatus) in the Squash Bug (CoreidaeHemiptera. One of my books says it's known in the west as a "Chincha" - but I think that just means "bug". This bug is different! See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2006)

File.Name: 6714a_6714-188.jpg
Notes:
I've been collecting photos of our "summer flowers" - those plants that wait until the winter rain is done and this becomes summer-dry desert country. I believe this rare summer flower is native Green Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata - or maybe P. michaelii?) flowering in our summer-dry woods. The plants look more like asparagus stalks than the orchids you get from a florist, but they are real native orchids growing in the summer heat. Note the long spur and how the inferior ovary is twisted to present the flower upside down. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2006)

It's remarkable how many summer flowers we have. They're important to keep the insects alive to work the (mostly) spring wildflowers. I'm cataloging. This might become my school science fair project for next year. Our summer flowers often come from interesting plant families. This might say something about the history of our native flora.

File.Name: 6714b_6714-186.jpg
Notes: California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly "puddling" on wet ground in the garden. I love those eyes! Even if you don't have a garden, you can attract some butterflies just by spilling water. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2006)

File.Name: 6714c_6714-193.jpg
Notes:
I believe this is a Thread-legged Bug - it's a tiny member of the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera - maybe genus Ploiariola or Barci? It's only about 1/4 inch or 6 mm long (not counting legs or antennae). You can see it's sucking proboscis folded under its head. It's hard to give a scale in all these photos, but if you look close in front, there are some spider webs. Legs and antennae aren't much larger. Hard to imagine all the machinery of life fitting into a form so small! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2006)

File.Name: 6714d_6713-163.jpg
Notes:
I went back to the mystery insect on ripe fruit of native Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) family. Thanks to CHiS, I'm pretty sure these critters are Mealy Bugs (Pseudococcidae) in the order Homoptera. Look close! I guess the white critter on the left with wings is a male and the spiny critter on the right that he's snuggling is a female, and the white fungus-like threads are eggs - lots of eggs. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2006)

I guess the winged critter is a male. It sure doesn't look like any preditor I know. But it doesn't have the long white tails of the other (maybe) male mealy bug I got a photo of. See my photo at:

As a teacher and naturalist, I always love a mystery. And I'm happy to admit I don't know!

File.Name: 6714f_6712-126.jpg
Notes:
Every flower has a story - really many stories between from seed to seed, and all the critters they engage along the way! This pretty blue flower is Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus. The [Previous] photo shows Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia. Both flowers are in the Scrophulariaceae family, and both were "Penstemon" when I learned my plants, but the woody members of the group were split into a new genus a few years ago. It's really just another penstemon. "Pen-stemon" means five stamens. But if you look close at this photo (and the last) you'll only see four stamens. The fifth stamen is always a sterile "staminode" on the bottom. In this flower you can see the white female pistil on top and the fifth stamen sticking out the bottom like a white tongue. (The hairy staminode is hidden in the previous photo.) (Santa Ynez River, 12 July 2006)

Of course this flower - every flower - has many more stories. I don't know what insects visit this pretty flower. I guess big bumblebees, but I didn't see any.
ScientificName: Penstemon heterophyllus; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; staminode
associatedTaxa: Penstemon heterophyllus; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; staminode

File.Name: 6715a_6715-030.jpg
Notes:
Interesting native summer flower of Broad-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias eriocarpa) in the Asclepiadaceae family. If the Honey Bees (Apis mellifera) were in charge, they would put a warning label on every one of these flowers. This bee died in the flower's trap! Note how the bee's leg is stuck between the 'hoods" of the flower. You can see golden "pollinia" or pollen sacks on it's rear leg - it's stuck on another one in the flower. Sometimes the bees can crawl away - even if they leave a leg behind. I didn't notice until I got home to my computer, but there's another preditor insect inspecting the prey - look between the bee's rear legs! (Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2006)

See my photo about milkweed flower anatomy - not quite complete - at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=8108247

We hiked up the Santa Ynez River today a few miles past Red Rock to the best swimming hole on the river. It was so hot and the water was perfect. I'm still a little "sun-sick". I managed to get a few photos.

File.Name: 6715b_6715-002.jpg
Notes: Unusual butterfly on an uncommon native flower of Broad-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias eriocarpa) in the Asclepiadaceae family. The little gray butterfly sure looks like a Hairstreak without a tail. I believe this is a tailless "Dryope" member of the Sylvan Butterfly (Satyrium sylvinus) species. It looks like the California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterflies that are common at home, but it has no tail, the orange spot on the hind wing is very small, and the blue spot is barely visible with no orange crown at all. I've been working to learn our local butterflies. It seems the more I learn, the harder it is - that's how it is! (Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2006)
CommonNames: Broad-leaved Milkweed; Hairstreak; Sylvan Butterfly; California Hairstreak
ScientificName: Asclepias eriocarpa; Asclepiadaceae; Dryope; Satyrium sylvinus; Satyrium californica
associatedTaxa: Asclepias eriocarpa; Asclepiadaceae; Dryope; Satyrium sylvinus; Satyrium californica

File.Name: 6715c_6715-007.jpg
Notes:
This unusual butterfly knows the secret entrance to this unusual flower - I learned something today, like I do most every day in the woods with my camera!

This little gray butterfly sure looks like a Hairstreak without a tail. I believe this is a tailless "Dryope" member of the Sylvan Butterfly (Satyrium sylvinus) clan. Some authorities consider it a new species, but the lepidopterists are such "splitters"! See my [Next] photo.

The uncommon summer flowers are Broad-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias eriocarpa) in the family Asclepiadaceae. It's not common. I only know a few colonies of this plant - here at the river, and in a meadow near home on the pass, and a few more. The flowers have a remarkable complex structure second only to orchids. This butterfly seems to know that the good stuff is inside the "petals" - except the real petals are lower on the flower, and these are the "hoods" of the "corona" - the unique milkweed flower structure above the petals and sepals. I didn't know that the "hoods" had this secret passage for insects to find the good stuff. Sometimes bees get stuck on these flowers, but this butterfly knows just what to do. So much to learn! (Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2006)

File.Name: 6715d_6715-027.jpg
Notes: New flower of native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) in the Mallow (Malvaceae) family. This gray shrub grows double-overhead and has many small hibiscus-like flowers. It has an odd distribution - common in the Santa Barbara foothills on the ocean side down in the fog-belt, but also here along the upper Santa Ynez River past Red Rock where it's summer hot and dry. (Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2006)

File.Name: 6715f_6715-023.jpg
Notes: I'm collecting photos of mid-summer flowers in our summer-dry climate. These are new flowers of Creek Clematis aka Yerba de Chivato (Clematis ligusticifolia) in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family. This vine gets huge and grows up into the trees and hangs clusters of small flowers back down near creeks and the river. It's cousin Chaparral Clematis (C. lasiantha) has bigger flowers and fewer leaflets and makes its flowers earlier in the mountains. (Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2006)
CommonNames: Creek Clematis; Yerba de Chivato; Buttercup; Chaparral Clematis

File.Name: 6716a_6714-181.jpg
Notes:
This Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) got caught under the sprinkler in the garden. It's trying to dry off on a rock in the sun - if the darn photographer would just stay away! It's remarkable how colorful these lizards are - like an impressionist painting. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2006)

It's getting harder every day to get new photos in the woods. Flowers and insects are getting scarce after many weeks of hot weather. Just as well that we're heading off to Oregon in a few days.

File.Name: 6716b_6716-036.jpg
Notes:
Today was hot and windy - a dangerous combination. This Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly is trying to hang on to this flower head of Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) in the canyon. This non-native thistle is cousin to our beautiful native Cirsium thistles that I've been showing in recent photos. It's hard to tell the young plants apart, and the non-native flowers are attractive to butterflies. We work to keep our canyon weed-free. We missed this thistle, but we'll get it before it spreads gazillions of seeds in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2006)

Interesting - I just started thinking of recent immigration issues. Invasive weeds are different - they simplify nature rather than making it more diverse!

File.Name: 6716c_6714-195.jpg
Notes:
Seeds of life - tiny insect egg perched on a bristle of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf. This is about as close as I can get - 1:1 magnification and tight crop - stalk and egg are 3 or 4 mm long. I've seen photos like this in my books that attribute the stalked eggs to Green Lacewings (Chrysopidae) in the order Neuroptera. The rationale is that the insects put the eggs on stalks so the first to hatch won't eat the rest. But this egg is solitary, so I'm not sure. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2006)

We think of generations, but most insects live from year to year. The changeable weather of any single year could end their legacy here or there if not everywhere. Everything depends on tiny seed like this - lots of them!

File.Name: 6716d_6716-035.jpg
Notes:
Big green caterpillar and little brown caterpillar sharing a branch of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae family in the canyon. I reckon the size difference is about "an order of magnitude" - millimeter vs. centimeter, inch vs. ten feet (close enough) - a power of ten difference. It's interesting to think how we fit into the orders of magnitude of living things from smallest to largest! (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2006) Try a web search on "powers of ten" - http://www.google.com/search?q=powers+of+ten - and have fun exploring. My favorite exploration of this idea is still the original brilliant "Cosmic View: The Universe in 40 Jumps" by Kees Boeke (1947). I have the thin book in my library. You can find a copy on the web at:
   http://www.vendian.org/mncharity/cosmicview/

File.Name: 6716e_6715-017.jpg
Notes:
Ripe berries of native Basket Bush aka "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) family near the river. The shrub sure looks like Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) with reddish stems and leaves in threes. It's a poison oak relative and look-alike, but the middle leaf does not have a separate stalk; it has berries rather than dry seeds; it has a distinctive "skunk-like" smell; and it does not make you itch. The stems were the preferred basket material for the "warp" of native American Chumash baskets. The berries are tart and tasty if you "suck and spit" - but don't suck too long or you notice the skunk! These fruit by the river are much more colorful than that the berries in the mountains. (Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2006)

Homer Simpson said: "leaves of three, let it be..." You can scare your friends by casually eating these berries that are NOT poison oak. Suck and spit and enjoy, but don't suck poison oak by mistake!
CommonNames: Basket Bush; Squaw Bush; Sumac; Poison Oak

File.Name: 6716f_6715-003.jpg
Notes: Another Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) trapped by a flower of native Broad-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias eriocarpa) in the Asclepiadaceae family. Common as they are, honey bees are not native here. These flowers are adapted for heavier bumblebees to get trapped and pull free with a pollen sack attached to their leg. Honey bees can't quite escape. This bee managed to pull free by crawling over the furry leaf. The bee in my [Previous] photo didn't make it. (Santa Ynez River, 15 July 2006)

File.Name: 6717a_6717-079.jpg
Notes:
Hummingbirds in the garden (1 of 6).

Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) approaching a flower on a tall Prickley Pear (Opuntia sp.) cactus in the garden. Look close - she's catching some color from the flower! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2006)

Today's photos are all hummingbirds working flowers in the garden this morning - maybe all the same hummingbird! It was an odd morning - overcast and cool for the first time in weeks. Not many butterflies, so I set up my long lens. We seem to have Anna's, Allen's, and Black-chinned hummers all at once, but this female Anna's chased them all away from HER prime corner of the garden. After every chase, she could come back to her flowers, so I had a chance for many photos.

File.Name: 6717b_6717-080.jpg
Notes:
Hummingbirds in the garden (2 of 6).

Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) going deep into a flower on a tall Prickley Pear (Opuntia sp.) cactus in the garden. Look how she's catching some color reflected from the flower! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2006)

File.Name: 6717c_6717-049.jpg
Notes:
Hummingbirds in the garden (3 of 6).

Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) working a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the garden. Look close and you can see the stamen and pistil of the flower making contact with her bill. More than once, I saw this bird perch in a nearby Bay Laurel tree and wipe her bill on the branch. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2006)

File.Name: 6717d_6717-075.jpg
Notes:
Hummingbirds in the garden (4 of 6).

Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) working a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the garden. I love the patterns of her wings. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2006)

File.Name: 6717e_6717-064.jpg
Notes:
Hummingbirds in the garden (5 of 6).

Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) working a flower of an unknown Salvia in the garden. The little birds work their wings so fast and sure to manoeuver from flower to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2006)

File.Name: 6717f_6717-060.jpg
Notes:
Hummingbirds in the garden (6 of 6).

Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) working a flower of an unknown Salvia in the garden. The little birds fly so fast from flower to flower! Sure they are hard to capture. My only "trick" is to look where it's going, not where it is - pre-focus on the next flower and sometimes you'll get lucky! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2006)

I shot all of today's hummingbird photos in the garden this morning. Maybe there's a second trick - spend a lot of time in the garden. They really do get to know you!

File.Name: 6718a_6717-050.jpg
Notes:
Perfect female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) working a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2006)

Shihiro - http://www.fotolog.com/shihiro/ - says "The humingbird is wonderful omen of "Good Luck" and beauty that is coming forth, in our culture - Dineh or Navajo." I reckon this photo a good omen for our trip to Oregon to visit kids and parents. No photos for a couple of weeks, but I look forward to chasing butterflies with my camera in the Cascade Mountain meadows. I'm sure I'll return with a few photos to share!

File.Name: 6718b_6717-051.jpg
Notes: I guess this is a Fox Sparrow (Passerellia iliaca) come to visit our garden - probably the Thick-billed (California) subspecies. It lives up to its name. It's visiting the "bird-bath" - just an old saucer - that we keep in the garden to catch water from the sprinkler for the birds. I don't think I've ever noticed this bird in the woods. This is like netting a creature from deep in the ocean. There it is, but there are so many questions! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2006)

File.Name: 6718c_6717-071.jpg
Notes:
You won't find this critter in any of your books, but I'm sure it's an immature Rufous-sided Towhee (Pipilo erythrophthalmus) bird scratching in the garden. It's the right size and shape, it has a white border on its tail wings, hints of deeper color in the right places, and it moves just like a Towhee. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2006)

Compare my photo of the adult at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=13578865

File.Name: 6718d_6714-212.jpg
Notes: Lesser Goldfinch (Carduelis psaltria) bird - I believe - munching new seeds of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) on the edge of the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2006)

File.Name: 6718e_6717-054.jpg
Notes: A little flock of crested Oak Titmouse (Baeolophus inoratus) - aka "Plain Titmouse" - birds flew through the garden this morning. Are they "titmouses" or titmice"? I got the photo, and a moment later they were gone. I'm good with flowers and mushrooms and butterflies, but I usually look down not up. I haven't noticed these little birds in the woods for awhile. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2006)

File.Name: 6718f_6718-089.jpg
Notes:
I captured the other hummingbird! Compare my [Previous] photos. I believe this is a female Allen's Hummingbird (Selasphorus sasin) visiting one of the last flowers of non-native Salvia greggii) in the garden. Hard to get this photo because the female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) has been chasing her of the garden. This morning, the Allen's hummer seems to have the upper hand. She has more "rufous" red-brown colors on her body. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2006)

With time - another life - I'd like to photograph the hummers every day and plot their territories!
ScientificName: Selasphorus sasin; Salvia greggii; Calypte anna
associatedTaxa: Selasphorus sasin; Salvia greggii; Calypte anna

File.Name: 6801a_6726-136.jpg
Notes:
Clodius Parnassian (Parnassius clodius) butterfly embracing a flower head of Nettleleaf Giant Hyssop (Agastache urticifolia) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. This butterfly is in the Swallowtail (Papilioidae) family, though you wouldn't know by looking at it. It has beautiful translucent forewings. My book says that after mating, the males place a waxy cap - called a sphragis - over the females' abdomen to prevent other males from mating, kind of a "chastity belt". I guess that's the white thing in the lower-left of the photo at the end of her abdomen. It's not all free love in nature! I got this photo high on the mountain divide between Steamboat Creak and Boulder Creek. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 July 2006)

We just got back today from two weeks in Oregon. I'm still sorting out my photos and ready to take more!
CommonNames: Clodius Parnassian; Nettleleaf Giant Hyssop; Mint; Swallowtail
ScientificName: Parnassius clodius; Agastache urticifolia; Lamiaceae; Papilioidae; sphragis
associatedTaxa: Parnassius clodius; Agastache urticifolia; Lamiaceae; Papilioidae; sphragis

File.Name: 6802a_6724-076.jpg
Notes:
I love the name of this large butterfly - it's a male Great Spangled Fritillary (Speyeria cybele). The "white" spots look like silver when the sun is right. The female looks very different - compare my [Previous] photo. I took both photos near Twin Lakes. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 July 2006)

The pretty flower is an alien weed that is taking over the mountain meadows in Oregon - Spotted Knapweed (Centaurea maculosa) or maybe the hybrid Meadow Thistle (Centaurea x pratensis). We drove home along the Klamath River in California heading to Arcata. Interesting that the spiny Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis) and Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) take over.

We just got back from two weeks in Oregon "walking in the tall trees" - family time, fix the water system, go for a hike - I found some time to photograph butterflies and other small critters in the woods.
Locality:
Foeniculum vulgare) take over.

We just got back from two weeks in Oregon "walking in the tall trees" - family time

File.Name: 6802b_6723-070.jpg
Notes: Female Great Spangled Fritillary (Speyeria cybele) butterfly working a composite flower head of Canada Thistle (Cirsium arvense) in the Oregon woods near Twin Lakes high in the mountains. These butterflies are sexually dimorphic - the females are mostly black like this, and the males are mostly orange. Compare my [Next] photo. The plant is an invasive weed, but the butterflies like it, it's not spiny like the thistles at home, and it doesn't seem to be taking over the meadows - yet! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 July 2006)

File.Name: 6802c_6723-026.jpg
Notes: The Oregon mountain meadows are full of fritillaries! I believe this butterfly is an Atlantis Fritillary (Speyeria atlantis). The pretty flower is an alien weed that is taking over the mountain meadows - Spotted Kapweed (Centaurea maculosa) or maybe the hybrid Meadow Thistle (Centaurea x pratensis). This Centaurea isn't spiney like our Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis) - and the butterflies like it - but it is taking over whole meadows along the road to Twin Lakes where I got this photo. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 July 2006)
CommonNames: Atlantis Fritillary; Spotted Kapweed; Meadow Thistle; Yellow Star Thistle
