File.Name: 6802d_6726-145.jpg
Notes: I believe this butterfly is a Hoffmann's Checkerspot (Chlosyne hoffmanni). It's "puddling" - sucking moisture and salts - from the same wet seep as the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) in my [Previous] photo. If you compare the photos, you can see that they are different. For one thing, this butterfly has dark antennae rather than golden like the other one. I sure can't remember all the color patterns in the field! "Shoot them all" (photos of course) and then sort them out! This is high on the mountain divide between Steamboat Creek and Boulder Creek. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 July 2006)

File.Name: 6802e_6726-153.jpg
Notes:
I believe this beautiful butterfly is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona). It's "puddling" - sucking moisture and salts from a wet seep - high on the mountain divide between Steamboat Creek and Boulder Creek. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 July 2006)

We have the same butterfly at home. It really is variable - compare my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=14430037

File.Name: 6802f_6722-007.jpg
Notes:
Little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly working a flower head of (I think) California Aster (Aster chilensis). The flower is native despite the species name, though there are many different aster species in the Oregon mountains. This butterfly has frittilary colors like all of my butterfly photos today. It's the little cousin of the greater frittilaries and checkerspots. I got this photo near our favorite swimmng hole along Steamboat Creek. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 July 2006)

We have the same butterfly at home - compare my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=10387045

File.Name: 6803a_6724-077.jpg
Notes:
Unknown Grass-Skipper (order Lepidoptera - subfamily Hesperiinae - genus Hesperia) - butterfly resting on a branch of Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) in the Oregon woods. Note how the butterfly is sitting on four legs and holding its front legs up in T-rex fashion. Unlike the Brushfoot (family Nymphalidae) butterflies, the skippers really do use those front legs. I love this photo for the conifer needles at least as much as the butterfly. I took this in a meadow along the dirt road to Twin Lakes. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 July 2006)

My photos today feature more butterflies from my recent two weeks in Oregon "walking in the tall trees". These are some of the smaller butterflies that are a challenge to identify from photos. I've done my best. It's cloudy and windy at home in the Santa Barbara mountains - a relief after the hottest week of the year in Oregon!

File.Name: 6803b_6725-102.jpg
Notes: I reckon this is a Common Checkered Skipper (Pyrgus communis) butterfly resting in a mountain meadow. The body hairs have a "blue dun" color - that subtle blue-gray color that is so important for dry fly fishing. I got this photo in the woods near Susan Creek. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 July 2006)

File.Name: 6803c_6726-149.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful Lilac-bordered Copper (Lycaena nivalis) butterfly "puddling" - sucking moisture and salts - on wet soil from a seep high on the mountain divide between Steamboat Creek and the Boulder Creek Wilderness. The lilac color is subtle but visible on the distal end of the hind wing. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 July 2006)

I have a photo in the archive showing the upper side of the wings:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=10830504

File.Name: 6803d_6726-159.jpg
Notes: I guess this is an Edith's Copper (Lycaena editha) butterfly resting on a flower I don't know high on the mountain divide between Steamboat Creek and the Boulder Creek Wilderness near the Wild Rose trailhead. The black-outlined brownish blotches are distinctive. Catching a photo of a butterfly like this is like netting a fish from the ocean deep. I got the trophy-photo, but I know nothing about the critter. So much to learn... (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 July 2006)

File.Name: 6803e_6724-079.jpg
Notes: I thought this was a hairstreak butterfly for sure because of the tails, but now I'm pretty sure this is an Eastern Tailed-blue (Everes comyntas). Yes there is a Western Tailed-blue (E. amyntula), but the ranges overlap on the west coast. Note the metallic silver ring around the spot near the tail and several spots on forewing and hindwing that are rusty brown color rather than black. I found this butterfly near Twin Lakes after a short hike. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 July 2006)

File.Name: 6803f_6726-156.jpg
Notes: I guess this little butterfly is a male Boisduval's Blue (Plebejus icarioides). You can see it's a "blue" by the bit of upper forewing that shows. The pattern of black dots doesn't quite match the description in my books - there are no white borders around the forewing spots and NO spots at all on the hindwings. This butterfly is variable and known to form unique isolated populations - so maybe it's ok to be different. This butterfly is "puddling" - sucking moisture and salts - on wet soil from a seep high on the mountain divide between Steamboat Creek and the Boulder Creek Wilderness. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 July 2006)

File.Name: 6804a_6724-084.jpg
Notes:
This beautiful Lepidoptera works the day-shift, but note the antennae have no knobs. It's a moth, not a butterfly! I believe this is a Red-shouldered Ctenucha (Ctenucha rubroscapus ) moth. Its a kind of Tiger Moth (family Arctiidae - or is it Amatidae or Ctenuchidae?). Look how the moth is guiding its long proboscis between the labial palps into the flowers of Canada Thistle (Cirsium arvense). I got this photo in a clearing along the dirt road to Twin Lakes. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 July 2006)

My photos today are insects that are NOT butterflies from my recent two weeks in Oregon "walking in the tall trees". The first two critters are *almost* butterflies. At home I try to photograph everything. In Oregon I mostly chased butterflies, though I couldn't resist shooting a few other insects I got close to. I'm glad I have these Oregon photos to post. It's cloudy and windy at home in the Santa Barbara mountains - we had 1/4 inch of summer drizzle today! What a change from last week when the temperature was 100+ degrees!

File.Name: 6804b_6723-039.jpg
Notes:
Look close - this critter is not a wasp and not a butterfly, it's a Clear-winged Moth (order lepidoptera, family Aegeriidae) working flowers of Canada Thistle (Cirsium arvense) in the Oregon woods near Susan Creek in Oregon. This little moth works the day-shift. It has perfect wasp colors and even flies like a wasp, but the fat antennae and long proboscis give it away. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 July 2006)

I think we have the same diurnal moth at home in the Santa Barbara mountains:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=10737966

File.Name: 6804c_6726-150.jpg
Notes: This pretty little fly (order Diptera) was sucking water or salt from a seep near the Reynolds Shelter high on the mountain divide between Steamboat Creek and the Boulder Creek Wilderness. Lying in the mud trying to focus, I thought it was a skinny Syrphidae - but now I see it's way too bristly. After some research, I'm pretty sure this is a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae) - probably subfamily Phasiinae, genus Cylindromyia. The bristles and white "calypters" at the base of the wings are distinctive. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 July 2006)

File.Name: 6804d_6723-059.jpg
Notes: Furry Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae - maybe subfamily Anthracinae?) working a flower head of Nettleleaf Giant Hyssop (Agastache urticifolia) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. I got this photo in a beautiful mountain meadow near Twin Lakes at about 5,000 feet elevation. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 July 2006)

File.Name: 6804e_6726-123.jpg
Notes: Mating Grasshoppers (family Acrididae) on a flower head of White False-hellebore (Veratrum album ssp. lobelianum). The plant sure looks like Corn Lily (V. californicum), but the flowers are dull green rather than bright white - it has herbal uses. Learning insects is hard enough without males and females looking so different! I got this photo near the Reynolds Shelter high on the mountain divide between Steamboat Creek and the Boulder Creek Wilderness. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 July 2006)

File.Name: 6804f_6725-107.jpg
Notes: Beautiful red Skimmer Dragonfly (order Odonata, suborder Anisoptera, family Libellulidae) perched near a wet seep in the Oregon woods near Susan Creek. It looks like a biplane at rest the way the wings are set at angles. This critter kept coming back to the same perch, so I positioned my tripod and waited for the photo. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 July 2006)

File.Name: 6805a_6723-060.jpg
Notes:
Perfect flower of native Western Columbine (Aquilegia formosa) in the Oregon mountains near Twin Lakes. These flowers seem so fancyful and ornate, but they are in the same Ranunculaceae family as our common spring buttercups. Ain't nature grand! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 July 2006)

Today's photos are mostly interesting flowers from my recent two weeks in Oregon "walking in the tall trees". I'm ready to post new photos from the canyon at home, but the weather is not cooperating. After record heat in Oregon, it's been cloudy and drizzly at home - almost rain - and then windy as it clears. I'll keep trying!

File.Name: 6805b_6723-063.jpg
Notes: New flower of native Leopard Lily (Lilium pardalinum) growing in a soggy bog near the Twin Lakes in Oregon. The lilies were struggling to raise their flowers above the head-high rushes and other marsh plants. It's startling to see such beautiful flowers growing untended in the woods! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 July 2006)

File.Name: 6805c_6723-020.jpg
Notes: Such Goth flowers! These are the strange flowers of Indian Pipe (Monotropa uniflora) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family. The plant is related to manzanita, but it has no green chlorophyll and cannot do photosynthesis to support itself. It depends on a shared fungal root-association with a nearby tree in a relationship known as "mycoheterotrophy". The tree does photosythesis and provides sugars to the fungus, and the fungus returns soil nutrients to the tree. That's a classic mutually beneficial symbiotic relationship. The Indian Pipes tap into this exchange, but I don't think they return for what they get. Sometimes I think all the woods are connected as one organism wired together by fungi! I got this photo near my Dad's cabin not far from Susan Creek. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 July 2006)

File.Name: 6805d_6723-066.jpg
Notes: I believe this elegant orchid is a White Bog Orchid (P. leucostachys) aka Platanthera dilata var. leucostachys. I found it several times in marshy parts of the mountain meadows at 5,000+ foot elevation. It has a fragrant spike of pure white flowers that really stands out among the green rushes. My book says it's pollinated by a Noctuid moth which gets nectar from the spur along with a pollinarium. I got this photo near Twin Lakes. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 July 2006)
ScientificName: P. leucostachys; Platanthera dilata; leucostachys; Noctuid
associatedTaxa: P. leucostachys; Platanthera dilata; leucostachys; Noctuid

File.Name: 6805e_6723-019.jpg
Notes: Rattlesnake Orchid aka Rattlesnake Plantain (Goodyera oblongifolia) still flowering in the Oregon woods near Susan Creek despite the heat. This is by far the most common summer orchid in the woods. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 July 2006)

File.Name: 6805f_6724-085.jpg
Notes: Scorpio rising, vampire thoughts... but this pretty Lepidoptera is a daytime moth. I believe this is a Red-shouldered Ctenucha (Ctenucha rubroscapus ) moth, a kind of Tiger Moth in the family Arctiidae. It's working a flower head of Canada Thistle (Cirsium arvense). Note how it's guiding its long double-barrelled-barrelled proboscis between the labial palps into the flowers. Compare my [Previous] photo. I got this photo in a clearing along the dirt road to Twin Lakes. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 July 2006)
CommonNames: Red-shouldered Ctenucha; Tiger Moth; Canada Thistle
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Ctenucha rubroscapus ; Arctiidae; Cirsium arvense
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Ctenucha rubroscapus ; Arctiidae; Cirsium arvense

File.Name: 6806a_6804-003.jpg
Notes:
Fluffed-up little hummingbird - I guess a female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) - perched on a branch on the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree above the garden. It must be wondering what happened to summer! (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2006)

We were in Oregon two weeks ago just in time for the hottest week of the year - 100+ degrees on the Umpqua River and at home. We came back home last week, but it's been cloudy and drizzly every morning and then windy when it finally clears. The cool weather feels good, but the wind is bad for photos. I think all our birds and butterflies have gone down the canyon looking for the sun - I'll chase them tomorrow to get some new photos!

File.Name: 6806b_6726-121.jpg
Notes: Perfect Grasshopper (family Acrididae) perched on a flower head of Western Coneflower (Rudbeckia occidentalis) near the Reynolds Shelter high on the mountain divide between Steamboat Creek and the Boulder Creek Wilderness. Strange flower - like a Black-Eyed Susan without petals. It's a composite in the Asteraceae) family that lacks ray flowers. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 July 2006)
CommonNames: Grasshopper; Western Coneflower; Black-Eyed Susan; composite

File.Name: 6806c_6720-001.jpg
Notes: Big green Grasshopper (family Acrididae) perched on leaves near the Klamath River. I love those striped eyes! (Klamath River, 20 July 2006)

File.Name: 6806d_6723-054.jpg
Notes: Look close - here's a pair of Greater Frilillary (genus Speyeria) butterflies on a flower head of Nettleleaf Giant Hyssop (Agastache urticifolia) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family in a mountain meadow near Twin Lakes. They are obviously interested in more than food! A moment later, they dropped down into the privacy of the stems and leaves for some privacy. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 July 2006)
CommonNames: Greater Frilillary; Nettleleaf Giant Hyssop; Mint

File.Name: 6806e_6726-161.jpg
Notes: Dusty-looking Checkerspot butterfly - maybe the Snowberry Checkerspot (Euphydryas colon aka Euphydryas chalcedona var. colon) - working a flower along the dirt road near the Wild Rose trailhead high on the mountain divide between Steamboat Creek and the Boulder Creek Wilderness. Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies really are variable and have many confusing varieties. I should know this plant, but I don't. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 July 2006)
ScientificName: Euphydryas colon; Euphydryas chalcedona; Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas colon; Euphydryas chalcedona; Euphydryas chalcedona

File.Name: 6806f_6725-101.jpg
Notes: Big Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider on its web with the sun behind in the Oregon woods near Susan Creek. I had my tripod as high as it gets and had to stand on tiptoes to frame the shot. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 July 2006)

File.Name: 6807a_6806-011.jpg
Notes:
Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on the Zinnias in the garden. Life is good. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2006)

It's been cloudy and windy all week, but the sun came out today and the wind dropped from gusty to breezy. The caterpillars of this native butterfly mostly work non-native plants of Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) - also some other native plants in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family. We pull most of these weeds, and the caterpillars are doing a good job on the rest of them.
Locality: Apiaceae) family. We pull most of these weeds

File.Name: 6807b_6807-026.jpg
Notes:
Remarkable fasciated Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri) growing along the highway. You can see a normal flower stalk on the left side of the photo, but when a plant becomes fasciated, it grows wide and weird like this with flowers everywhere. The main stalk is as wide as my extended hand - 7 inches, almost 20 cm! (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2006)

Many plants become fasciated and some gardners collect them. I wish I could just collect seeds, but it's not that simple. The normal flower stalk in the photo is growing from the base of the same plant with the fasciated stalk. I reckon it's one of those "genotype" vs. "phenotype" things. I'll collect some seeds anyway!

I have another photo of a fasciated plant in the archive:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=8029530

File.Name: 6807c_6807-060.jpg
Notes: Remarkable summer flower of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) growing in the chaparrel on the dry side of the canyon. These uncommon lilies appear in our desert-like summer when wild flowers should be done. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2006)

File.Name: 6807d_6807-030.jpg
Notes: Tiny mosquito-sized humpbacked fly (order Diptera) working summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the canyon. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2006)

File.Name: 6807e_6807-046.jpg
Notes: I'm pretty sure this is the same kind of butterfly as the [Previous] photo - a "Blue" (subfamily Polyommatinae) working native flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the woods. I guess this is a Lupine Blue (Plebejus lupinus) which depends on buckwheat rather than legumes - but I wouldn't bet the farm on it. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2006)

File.Name: 6807f_6807-043.jpg
Notes: A butterfly's world inside a flower cluster of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the woods. If I had to guess, I'd say Lupine Blue (Plebejus lupinus). I have more conventional shot of this butterfly in my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2006)

File.Name: 6808a_6808-081.jpg
Notes:
Strange Fruit - it took me a moment to realize what I saw hanging from the flowering Vinegar Weed (Trichostema lanceolatum) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family on a dry flat near the river - look close! This Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens) has captured a Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis). It's a tough end for this beautiful creature, but I guess nature is like that. (Santa Ynez River, 8 August 2006)

Now look at the remarkable summer flowers. The flower on the bottom-left has its stamens and pistil set like a scorpion tail waiting for an insect visitor to land and bring the goodies down on its back. See my [Previous] two photos to see how this works. The flower above-left has its goodies off to the side. I reckon it's already been pollinated and doesn't care.

I have many photos of living Snowberry Clearwing Moths (Hemaris diffinis) in the archive. I can't resist trying to get photos of these diurnal moths that fly like hummingbirds! Here's a recent photo:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=14616160
CommonNames: Vinegar Weed; Mint; Green Lynx Spider; Snowberry Clearwing Moth; Snowberry Clearwing Moths
ScientificName: Trichostema lanceolatum; Lamiaceae; Peucetia viridens; Hemaris diffinis; Hemaris diffinis
associatedTaxa: Trichostema lanceolatum; Lamiaceae; Peucetia viridens; Hemaris diffinis; Hemaris diffinis

File.Name: 6808b_6808-089.jpg
Notes:
I know as a photographer this is not quite sharp. It was windy and the bees are fast. But as a naturalist - what a perfect photo of how pollination happens! This Bumblebee (family Bombus, order Hymenoptera) is working a summer flower of native Vinegar Weed (Trichostema lanceolatum) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family on a dry flat near the river. The bee is heavy enough to tip the flower to bring its stamens and pistil down on the back of its thorax. Flower and bee seem made for each other - and maybe they are. Other insects work these flowers, but they aren't heavy enough to work the lever arm. Compare my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (Santa Ynez River, 8 August 2006)

This well-named plant has an intense vinegar smell. It was a challenge to get on the ground for these photos!

File.Name: 6808c_6808-090.jpg
Notes: Little Grass-Skipper (order Lepidoptera - subfamily Hesperiinae - genus Hesperia) - butterfly working a flower of native Vinegar Weed (Trichostema lanceolatum) on a dry flat near the river. Note how it's clasping the flower, but it's not heavy enough to bring the stamens and pistil down to its back. Compare my [Next] photo of a bunblebee on the same flower. (Santa Ynez River, 8 August 2006)

File.Name: 6808d_6808-112.jpg
Notes: I guess this is a male Northern White Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working a flowerhead of a Peppermint (Mentha sp. in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family) in the garden. Skippers are moth-like butterflies that always have that curved "apiculus" extension on the tip of their antennae. There are many black and orange skippers that look alike, but this one is different. It's not common here. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2006)

File.Name: 6808e_6808-073.jpg
Notes: I'm not sure if this is an Orange Sulfur (Colias eurytheme) or a male Harford's Sulpher (C. harfordii) butterfly. It only has a single HW eyespot. Either way, it's not a California Dogface (Zerene eurydice). This pretty butterfly is working composite flowers of the horrible weed Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis) on the dry wash near the river. (Santa Ynez River, 8 August 2006)
CommonNames: Orange Sulfur; Harford's Sulpher; California Dogface; Yellow Star Thistle
ScientificName: Colias eurytheme; C. harfordii; Zerene eurydice; Centaurea solstitialis
associatedTaxa: Colias eurytheme; C. harfordii; Zerene eurydice; Centaurea solstitialis

File.Name: 6808f_6806-009.jpg
Notes: This is the same Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly as my [Previous] photo - perched on a Zinnia flower in the garden. I don't think it's feeding. It's been cloudy and drippy this week. This butterfly is spreading its wings like a solar collector to warm up! This is our only swallowtail with black running sideways along the front edge of the forewings. (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2006)

File.Name: 6809a_6809-151.jpg
Notes:
Frisky pair of Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies resting for a moment on native summer flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum). Not quite sharp, but it's an action shot. They're not resting at all, but pursuing and avoiding! My rule in the woods is "First get a photo - any photo - then do it right." One shot is all I got with this pair of butterflies. I'm sure that's the smaller male on the left making a move on the larger female to the right. Note that the pattern of "eyes" and markings on the wings is not quite the same, especially towards the apex of the forewings. I'll be looking for the difference on tomorrow's hike! (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2006)

I have another photo of mating - cavorting at least - Buckeyes from last summer at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=10626058

File.Name: 6809b_6809-159.jpg
Notes:
American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working a flower head of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. This lady has two large eyespots on the underside of her hindwings while other "Ladies" have four spots. We had all the "Painted Ladies" (genus Vanessa) last year, but this year they are scarce and shy. I love the patterns of the wings - like the swirls inside the cover of an antique book. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2006)

Every year is different in the woods. One benefit of a daily photo collection like this is that I can document some of the changes year after year. This will become a database if I can figure out how.
CommonNames: American Lady; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 6809c_6809-143.jpg
Notes:
Great Basin Wood-Nymph (Cercyonis sthenele ssp. silvestris) perched on a new acrorn of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). (Looks like a good acorn crop - good for wildlife!) I didn't know we had this butterfly in our mountains, but once I spotted this one, I saw several more. I saw it chase away a Swallowtail and return to its perch. Usually they stay in the dry grasses and flutter away like moths when distuurbed. I'm sure I've walked right past them many times. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2006)

This Wood-Nymph butterfly is interesting because it has a double eye on the forewing underside. My books don't show that.

File.Name: 6809d_6809-149.jpg
Notes: Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly perched on blade of grass in the woods with the sun behind. Our local population of these butterflies has California summer colors - golden dry grass, not poppies - part of the "golden rolling hills" that Kate Wolf sang about so eloquently. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2006)

File.Name: 6809e_6809-158.jpg
Notes:
Pretty little "Blue" butterfly - one of the Gossamer-wings (family Lycaenidea, subfamily Polyommatinae) working summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the canyon. I guess this is an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon). Hard to see in this photo, but they have metalic irdescent blue spots around the black spots on the hind wing. This photo does show the blue upper wings. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2006)

These butterflies were common last year, but this year is different. This butterfly seems too small - just the size of my fingernail. Every year is different.
ScientificName: Lycaenidea; Polyommatinae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Plebejus acmon
associatedTaxa: Lycaenidea; Polyommatinae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Plebejus acmon

File.Name: 6809f_6808-078.jpg
Notes:
More Strange Fruit - I was busy getting the [Previous] photo when I looked over and noticed another Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) hanging as prey to a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens). Sad sight - these daytime moths that fly like hummingbirds are one of my favorite critters. The plant is summer-flowering native Vinegar Weed (Trichostema lanceolatum) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family growing on a dry flat the size of a tennis court near the river. I looked close and found these two moths down and six more working the flowers. I guess this is why insects start getting scarce towards the end of summer. The spiders love it! (Santa Ynez River, 8 August 2006)

See my [Previous] photo and many more in the archive:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=15072094

File.Name: 6810a_6809-120.jpg
Notes:
New feather-like seed plumes of our native shrub Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family with the sun behind. The plumes look dull brown when the sun is the other way. I guess that's a lesson for life - find the right way to look at the world or *everything* is dingy! (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2006)

Check my last (or is it first?) photo today. It's one of those crime story - CSI - forensic photos that I need help with!
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=15105211

File.Name: 6810b_6809-117.jpg
Notes:
Tiny flowers of native parasitic Dodder (Cuscuta sp.) plant in the Cuscutaceae family. The host-plant in the background is the well-known Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum). That tiny black weevil (family Curculionidae) beetle with elephant-like snout disappeared into the little flower soon after I got this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2006)

It's an interesting year - aren't they all? We had a cool wet spring, but the summer woods are unusually dry. The dodder is shriveling up early with just a few flowers. Flowers and insects are both getting scarce.

File.Name: 6810c_6809-127.jpg
Notes: This native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the woods looks like a scrub oak, but it has brilliant red berries rather than acorns. The berries look delicious, but they taste bitter. I guess that's not surprising since they're cousins of Cascara Sagrada (Rhamnus purshiana), a powerful laxative. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2006)

File.Name: 6810d_6809-145.jpg
Notes: Strange fuzzy gall - like cotton candy - on a leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the woods. Tiny Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) make these galls so their eggs can develop inside. So many mysteries! (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2006)

File.Name: 6810e_6809-135.jpg
Notes: Fat new acorns on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). Flowers are getting scarce in the mountains, but it looks like a good season for autumn acorns. Our scrub oaks (Q. berberidifolia and (Q. wislizenii var. frutescens) and Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees are doing well. The Goldencup Oaks (Quercus chrysolepis) seem barren. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2006)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Coast Live Oak; Goldencup Oaks
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Q. berberidifolia; Q. wislizenii; frutescens; Quercus agrifolia; Quercus chrysolepis
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Q. berberidifolia; Q. wislizenii; frutescens; Quercus agrifolia; Quercus chrysolepis

File.Name: 6810f_6808-068.jpg
Notes:
This is one of those crime story - CSI - forensic photos. I took the photo and didn't disturb anything. Here's a deer leg with a few extra clean bones. There's another limb with a hoof (nothing else) about 40 feet (12 meters) away. I looked for the rest of the carcass and didn't find any more bones nearby. The scene is a rise in the wide mostly dry river wash with large oak trees. There's a campground and ranger station 1/2 mile away. This photo is a stumper. Is this lion-kill, or something the coyote brought home, or evidence of recent hunting or poaching? (Santa Ynez River, 8 August 2006)

I believe deer hunting season starts on Saturday, August 12 - or was it last Saturday? The regulations are really confusing, and there are special seasons for bows and black powder. I hiked through this same place 3 weeks ago before our Oregon trip. These bones are something new.

Notes:
Female California Dogface (Colias eurydice aka Zerene eurydice) butterfly working flowers of healing Purple Coneflower (Echinaceae purpurea) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family in the garden. I have a photo of the male in my [Previous] photo.

File.Name: 6811b_6808-094.jpg
Notes: Male California Dogface (Colias eurydice aka Zerene eurydice) butterfly working a composite Zinnia flower in the garden. This butterfly is the official California State Butterfly. It always folds its wings when it lands on a flower. I was trying to get a photo showing the iridescent dog face on the upper wings. Even at 3 frames per second, it was there in one photo and gone in the next. Maybe tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2006)

File.Name: 6811c_6811-188.jpg
Notes: Yipes - be careful walking through the summer-dry meadows in the mountains wearing shorts! I'm sure this is an Argiope Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae - genus Argiope) perched upside-down on it's web, but I'm not sure which species. It's about the size of a quarter. Insects are getting scarce in the mountains. These spiders are part of the reason why! (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2006)

File.Name: 6811d_6807-067.jpg
Notes: Snakefly (order Neuroptera - family Raphidiidae) insect perched on the canvas cover of our classic tent trailor. This critter is a preditor that seems aware of me. I like the geometry of the shadow. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2006)

File.Name: 6811e_6811-184.jpg
Notes:
Summer flowers of Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata). This is a local endemic species in our mountains with narrow leaves rather than the wider leaves of the more common Chaparral Honeysuckle (L. subspicata var. denudata) that is more common inland. Botanist/explorer David Douglas collected the type specimen in 1831. The inland variety is more common, but the first named collection is official. This species is rare - the variety is common. It's the little differences that make the local flora so interesting year after year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2006)

Summer flowers are scarce this year, so I'm collecting every one I can.
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; L. subspicata; denudata
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; L. subspicata; denudata

File.Name: 6811f_6811-197.jpg
Notes: Native Mimulus floribundus in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. It's related to penstemon and paintbrush. It's growing in a wet place in the chaparral. The leaves are distinctly slimy when you grasp them. I'm looking for summer flowers in our summer-dry mountains. I'm amazed that there still is a wet place in the chaparral in the month of August! (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2006)

File.Name: 6812a_6812-015.jpg
Notes:
Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) perched high in the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden with the sun behind. It made a perfect shadow on the leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2006)

Windy today, so I chased the big swallowtail butterflies in the garden. They're easier to follow when everything is moving. I took 180 photos today, kept 20, and I'm showing 6 here in the f'log. I counted six Pale Swallowtails (Papilio eurymedon), three Anise Swallowtails (P. zelicaon), and two Western Tiger Swallowtails (P. rutulus) . Despite appearances, butterflies - all insects, flowers, and birds - are getting scarce in our summer-dry woods. I go back to school (teaching) in two weeks, and I'm almost glad. It's getting hard to find new photos every day.
CommonNames: Western Tiger Swallowtail; Butterfly Bush; Pale Swallowtails; Anise Swallowtails; Western Tiger Swallowtails

File.Name: 6812b_6812-033.jpg
Notes:
Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a composite Zinnia flower in the garden with a yellow Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) behind. The gleam in its eye is a hexagon with six sides. I thought this was a camera lens diaphram thing like the 7-sided highlights in my [Previous] photo, but Nano gave me the answer - it's got to do with the 6-sided "ommatidia" simple-eyes that make up the compound eyes of these insects. Thanks Nano! (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2006)

I still don't understand the optics, and I'm really curious about dragonfly eyeballs that look at me in every photo:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=13578775
   http://www.fotolog.com/nano_el_enano/?pid=14483203
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Zinnia; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 6812c_6812-026.jpg
Notes: Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) working a flower head of the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. Nice butterfly, but look at the geometrical shape in the upper left. It has 7 sides that match the 7 petals on the diaphram of my favorite Nikon 105mm micro lens. It's a refraction thing. Now compare the butterfly's eye in my [Next] photo that shows a 6-sided highlight. Que pasa? (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2006)

File.Name: 6812d_6812-044.jpg
Notes: Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly holding on to a composite Zinnia flower head. Maybe it let me get so close because I was blocking the wind. Notice how it's catching some color from the red flower petals. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2006)

File.Name: 6812e_6812-050.jpg
Notes:
Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) working a Dahlia flower in the garden. Did you spot the little Wasp (order Hymenoptera) in the air in the upper right? (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2006)

Lucky shot for sure. You know that kind of photo luck - I took 180 photos today, kept 20, and I'm showing 6 here in the f'log today. Gotta love digital!

File.Name: 6812f_6812-008.jpg
Notes:
A two-fer butterfly photo in the garden today - Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) (upper side) on the left and a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) (lower side) on the right sharing flowers of the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) flowers. Close as they look in this photo, they have different habits. The Pale Swallowtails like to move around, but the Western Tiger Swallowtails stay put on one flower cluster for 20 minutes or more. The Pale Swallowtails like Zinnias, but the Tigers like Dahlias. Those are the little differences that make the world go 'round! (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2006)

There were also Anise Swallowtails (Papilio zelicaon) working the same flowers in the morning, but they left when I started taking photos.
Locality: Papilio zelicaon) working the same flowers in the morning
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Western Tiger Swallowtail; Butterfly Bush; Zinnias; Anise Swallowtails

associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Papilio rutulus; Buddleia davidii; Dahlias; Papilio zelicaon

File.Name: 6813a_6808-106.jpg
Notes:
Tiny Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working a new Dahlia flower in the garden. The flower is a natural light tent with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2006)

Look close at the center of this flower. It's like one of those optical illusion cubes that go in or out depending how you look at it. In the flower head, you can see curves turning counter-clockwise - then if you blink you can see different curves turning clockwise. But you can't see both at once! I printed a few copies and counted the curves - 21 turning counter-clockwise and 13 going clockwise. (Actually I got 22 the first time - it's easy to get lost on the bottom. But I did it again, and I'm sure the number is 21.) These are both Fibonacci Numbers in the series 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, 34 ... where each new number is the sum of the two previous numbers. The ratio of any two successive numbers in the series approaches the Golden Number Phi = 1.61803399... Flower math??

It was foggy this morning, then windy all afternoon, and I'm preoccupied with other stuff. Not a good photo day, but I have these good photos in the vault to post. You can tell I'm thinking about school that starts in two weeks!
Locality: Actually I got 22 the first time - it's easy to get lost on the bottom.  But I did it again
ScientificName: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Dahlia; Fibonacci Numbers; Phi
associatedTaxa: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Dahlia; Fibonacci Numbers; Phi

File.Name: 6813b_6808-097.jpg
Notes: I'm pretty sure this is a Katydid (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) nymph a molt or two behind the almost-adult green Katydid in my [Previous] photo. I got both photos on the same day last week in the garden. It's perched (and nibbling) on a new Dahlia flower. This a stumper that the nymphs are so colorful while the adults are so well camouflaged. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2006)

File.Name: 6813c_6808-092.jpg
Notes: Green Katydid (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) "hiding" on a green Basil (Ocimum basilicum) leaf in the garden. It's not ducking behind the leaf or anything, but green on green is hard to notice as you walk by. The best camouflage is sometimes subtle. This is the first adult-looking katydid I've seen this summer, but I think it's still one-molt short of mature because of the short wings. I still see a few of the more-colorful younger nymphs in the garden. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2006)

File.Name: 6813d_6712-116.jpg
Notes:
Flowering stem with both male and female flowers of native Durango Root (Datisca glomerata) in it's own Datisca (Datiscaceae) family growing along a small almost-dry creek near the river. This plant is "androdioecious" - it has both male and male/female hermaphroditic individuals like this one that grow together. See my [Previous] photo for an all male plant. (Santa Ynez River, 8 August 2006)

This native plant is a joke for local naturalists. The flower stems sure look like "colas" of a certain notorious introduced non-native plant in the Cannabaceae family that is sometimes found in our mountains, though the leaves are pinnate rather than palmate. Sometimes I find these plants cut down and presumably harvested. The irony is that the plant was widely used as a fish poison by native Americans to stun fish in creeks. I haven't seen any reports of human ingestion, but cattle have died after eating it. Know your plants!

File.Name: 6813e_6712-121.jpg
Notes: Male plant of native Durango Weed (Datisca glomerata) in it's own Datisca) (Datiscaceae) family. Heh - the stamens look like little clusters of bananas! See my [Next] photo for more info. (Santa Ynez River, 8 August 2006)

File.Name: 6813f_6809-137.jpg
Notes: Fat new acorn of a native Scrub Oak (Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens). We have several different "scrub oaks" in our mountains that are difficult to tell apart even when you know what to look for. This scrub oak has leaves that vary from smooth to toothed, but they are always shiny green underneath. According to my books, the acorns take two years to mature, so maybe those little acorns are next year's crop - but I also see "acorn babies" on the Q. berberidifolia scrub oaks that are said to mature in just one year. Despite the Jepson Flora treatment, the acorn scales are clearly tubercled. I don't think the critters care! It looks like a good year for acorns from most local species. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2006)

File.Name: 6814a_6814-074.jpg
Notes:
Awesome Tarantula Hawk (order Hymenoptera, genus Pepsis) wasp working native flowers of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis) in the Asclepiadaceae family growing along the highway near San Lucas Ranch. Note the Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) for scale! (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 August 2006)

This is our largest wasp - dragonfly size - one of the biggest in the world. They "sting" and paralyze California Tarantula (Aphonopelma reversum) spiders and drag (or carry?) them to a burrow for the wasp larvae to consume. Their growing kids need a protein meal, but the adults get their nectar energy boost from flowers like these. I usually find these wasps working yellow composite flowers like Lepidospartum squamatum along the dry river wash, but the summer composites aren't flowering yet. I haven't seen any tarantulas either.
Locality: or carry?) them to a burrow for the wasp larvae to consume.  Their growing kids need a protein meal
CommonNames: Tarantula Hawk; Narrow-leaved Milkweed; Honey Bee; California Tarantula
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Pepsis; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae; Apis mellifera; Aphonopelma reversum; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Pepsis; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae; Apis mellifera; Aphonopelma reversum; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 6814b_6814-072.jpg
Notes: A trio of Large Milkweed Bugs (order Hemiptera, family Lygaeidae, species Oncopeltus fasciatus or kin) on a new seed pod of native Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis) in the Asclepiadaceae family growing along the highway. It's been windy enough today without cars driving by way too fast. This native summer-flowering perennial mostly grows along the highway. Maybe the cars and trucks whizzing by create just the right gusty breeze to disperse the dandelion-like seeds along the road - and frustrate photographers like me! (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 August 2006)
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Lygaeidae; Oncopeltus fasciatus; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Lygaeidae; Oncopeltus fasciatus; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae

File.Name: 6814c_6814-089.jpg
Notes:
Some kind of Cicada (family Homoptera, family Cicadidae) perched on a branch of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) on the edge of the garden at home. Our early summer cicadas just click, but this one sings a continuous chorus. I was lucky to find this critter - they stop singing whenever you get close. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2006)

Compare our early summer cicada in my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=14330765

File.Name: 6814d_6814-059.jpg
Notes: Native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrubs in the Mallow (Malvaceae) family are just coming into full bloom when we need new flowers in the woods to keep the insects busy. Notice the Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) burrowed inside the flower going round and round to collect nectar and pollen. I'm interested in these summer flowers in our summer-dry mountains. I got this photo near Manzanita Lane in Paradise. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 August 2006)

File.Name: 6814e_6814-064.jpg
Notes: Little Short-Horned Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Acrididae) hanging on maybe the last flower of native Red Thistle (Cirsium occidentale - maybe var. venustum?) in the woods. Summer flowers are getting scarce - this is the only native thistle I've seen in bloom for weeks. The brilliant red flower is striking among the mostly dull late summer dry plants. I got this photo near Manzanita Lane in Paradise. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 August 2006)

File.Name: 6814f_6814-084.jpg
Notes: Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working a Dahlia flower in the garden. These butterflies favor the Dahlia and Cosmos flowers, but the Pale Swallowtails (P. eurymedon) prefer the Zinnias. Nice photo, but I posted it because of the six-sided gleam in it's eye. Nano helped me understand that it's got to do with the 6-sided "ommatidia" simple-eyes that make up the compound eyes of these butterflies. (NOT the seven-sided diaphram in my camera lens.) This photo shows that the gleam in the butterfly's eye is complicated - there are layers, like a diffraction pattern. (San Marcos Pass, 14 August 2006)
CommonNames: Western Tiger Swallowtail; Pale Swallowtails; Zinnias

File.Name: 6815a_6815-116.jpg
Notes:
Here's a remarkable pair of insects in one photo. There's a Leafcutter Bee or kin (order Hymenoptera, family Megachilidae) on top, and a wasp-mimic Clearwinged Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Aegeriidae aka Sesiidae) right behind. The bee collects pollen on its abdomen rather than its legs, and you can see it dragging its tail over the tiny disk flowers of this Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. The daytime-shift diurnal butterfly/wasp-looking critter works the flowers with its long proboscis. Moth and bee seem to be getting along. See my [Previous] photo for a close view of this surprising moth. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2006)

This Clearwinged Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Aegeriidae aka Sesiidae) is completely different from the Snowberry Clearwing Moths (Hemaris diffinis) in the family Sphingidae.
CommonNames: Leafcutter Bee; Clearwinged Moth; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy; Clearwinged Moth; Snowberry Clearwing Moths
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Megachilidae; Lepidoptera; Aegeriidae; Sesiidae; Rudbeckia hirta; Lepidoptera; Aegeriidae; Sesiidae; Hemaris diffinis; Sphingidae

Families: Megachilidae; Aegeriidae; Sesiidae; Aegeriidae; Sesiidae; Sphingidae

File.Name: 6815b_6815-128.jpg
Notes: This is not a butterfly or a wasp - it's a remarkable Clearwinged Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Aegeriidae aka Sesiidae) working Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. This little diurnal moth works the day-shift. It has perfect wasp colors, but the fat antennae without clubs and the long proboscis give it away. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2006)
CommonNames: Clearwinged Moth; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 6815c_6815-106.jpg
Notes:
Tiny Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of non-native Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. The bee seems to have something stuck on its extended proboscis. It's a sticky pollen sack or "pollinarurm" from the flower. There's another dark one waiting to be snagged between the cups to the left. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2006)

Milkweed seems commonplace, but the flowers are remarkable. They have another ring of unusual structures above the usual petals and sepals called the corona. See my photo and explantaion at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=8108247

File.Name: 6815d_6815-124.jpg
Notes: Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly working a Zinnia flower in the garden. This swallowtail is different for having a dark band along the front of the forewing rather than across it. For some reason, swallowtails are our most common butterflies in the garden right now, along with Duskywing Skippers (genus Erynnis). Other butterflies are scarce. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2006)

File.Name: 6815e_6815-099.jpg
Notes: Large Argiope Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae - genus Argiope) perched upside-down on its web in the woods waiting for prey. It might be a long wait - insects are getting scarce in the woods as we move from summer into autumn. These spiders are part of the reason why! You can see some of the spider's "stabilimentum" - that zigzag structure below the spider's head (on the bottom) that looks like cigarette smoke in an old film noir movie. As far as I know, all Argiope spiders produce this distinctive structure, and it's different for each species. Maybe it's a lure for prey, or a marker to warn birds away from the web. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2006)

File.Name: 6815f_6814-068.jpg
Notes: This large spider in the woods is not green enough to be a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens), but it has that Lynx Spider (family Oxyopidae) look. It's perched under a dry seed head of of native Red Thistle (Cirsium occidentale - maybe var. venustum?) in the woods. I wonder what prey it hopes to find here? (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 August 2006)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Lynx Spider; Red Thistle
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae) look. It's perched under a dry seed head of of native Red Thistle (Cirsium occidentale; venustum

File.Name: 6816a_6816-146.jpg
Notes: This tiny treefrog (family Hylidae) has such an enigmatic smile! It really is tiny - it could just about sit on my thumbnail. I guess it's either a Pacific Treefrog (Pseudacris regilla) or California Treefrog (P. cadaverina). Looking at my books, I suspect it's the latter species beacuse the dark eye stripes are so weak. See my [Previous] two photos. (Santa Ynez River, 16 August 2006)

File.Name: 6816b_6816-144.jpg
Notes: I found tiny treefrog (family Hylidae) by a pool that is starting to dry up on Oso Creek not too far from the river. It's either a Pacific Treefrog (Pseudacris regilla) or California Treefrog (P. cadaverina). Looking at my books, I suspect it's the latter species beacuse the eye stripes are so weak, though I don't notice any webbing on the hind feet. Notice the adhesive toepads that help it climb! See my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (Santa Ynez River, 16 August 2006)

File.Name: 6816c_6816-140.jpg
Notes: You looking at me? This is a tiny treefrog (family Hylidae) that I found by a pool that is starting to dry up along Oso Creek near the first river crossing in Paradise. It's either a Pacific Treefrog (Pseudacris regilla) or California Treefrog (P. cadaverina). See my [Next] two photos. (Santa Ynez River, 16 August 2006)

File.Name: 6816d_6816-139.jpg
Notes: This is a beautiful red Skimmer Dragonfly (order Odonata, suborder Anisoptera, family Libellulidae) - maybe a Red Rock Simmer (Paltothemis lineatipes? It's perched on an alkali-encrusted rock near a small creek near the river that is going dry. I love the shadows on the rock - I see a superhero dude standing with mutant wings spread wide! (Santa Ynez River, 16 August 2006)

File.Name: 6816e_6816-138.jpg
Notes: Little Damselfly (Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) resting on a rock. It's so blue that it almost looks black. What eyes! (Santa Ynez River, 16 August 2006)

File.Name: 6816f_6815-129.jpg
Notes: I can't resist posting one more photo of this beautiful critter that is neither butterfly nor wasp. This is a remarkable daytime Clearwinged Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Aegeriidae aka Sesiidae) working Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. See my [Previous] two photos for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2006)
CommonNames: Clearwinged Moth; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 6817a_6817-153.jpg
Notes:
Male Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working new sunner flowers of native California Goldenrod aka Oreja de Liebre (Solidago californica) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family that is just starting to flower in our desert-dry summer. Usually I skip the dark Duskywings and orange Grass-skippers - "skip her" - but this pretty skipper stands out. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 August 2006)

Compare the Common Checkered Skipper (Pyrgus communis) butterfly in this photo:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=14983867
CommonNames: Northern White-Skipper; California Goldenrod; Oreja de Liebre; Composite; Common Checkered Skipper

associatedTaxa: Heliopetes ericetorum; Solidago californica; Asteraceae; Pyrgus communis

File.Name: 6817b_6815-094.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful new summer flowers of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. I don't care what the new flora says - this will always be Zauschneria californica) for me! This photo shows the ancient dance of life for all plants - move pollen from stamens to stigma. It's a classic hummingbird flower - long flower tube, nectar deep inside, no place for any other insect to land. These flowers aren't doing very well this year in the canyon. We finally put up a hummingbird feeder with sugar water, though I don't really approve. The hummers love it and buzz in my face when it's empty! (San Marcos Pass, 15 August 2006)

We have several similar late-summer plants with long red flowers in very different plant families - like the Keckiella ternata in my [Previous] photo. I'm working to collect photos of all our late-summer plants in the summer-dry mountains. It's an interesting group of plants that keep the pollinators alive for next year.
ScientificName: Epilobium canum; Onagraceae; Zauschneria californica; Keckiella ternata
associatedTaxa: Epilobium canum; Onagraceae; Zauschneria californica; Keckiella ternata

File.Name: 6817c_6817-158.jpg
Notes: New summer flowers of Scarlet Beardtongue (Keckiella ternata) growing near the top of of La Cumbre Peak, near 4,000 feet, one of the high points of our mountains. That's an ugly common name for this remakable summer-flowering shrub that only grows on the very top of our Santa Ynez Mountains. This plant needs a better common name! It's a classic "hummingbird flower" -though I didn't see any hummers visiting today. This straggly shrub has long flowering stalks with leaves in threes, and shorter more congested non-flowering stems with bright red alternate leaves. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 August 2006)

File.Name: 6817d_6817-148.jpg
Notes: New flowers of Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. The small white flowers are hard to find in the green leafy bracts, and they never open much more than this. Summer flowers are all remarkable in our summer-dry mountains. It's remarkable that this cryptic flower is related to such brilliant native flowers as paint brush, owl's clover, monkey flower, snapdragon, mullein, and penstemons like the [Next] photo! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 August 2006)

File.Name: 6817e_6817-151.jpg
Notes:
I believe this photo shows new flowers of Miss Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. Notice the Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera) just sitting and waiting for prey! This plant is a local endemic species between Monterey and Ventura, but it's pretty common in our mountains. This plant is growing along the road near the 4,000 foot high point of our mountains. You would hardly notice the flowers walking or driving by, but the insects notice during our summer-dry season! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 August 2006)

I'm curious about Miss Sara Allen Plummer (1836-1923) whose name appears on several of our native plants.
CommonNames: Miss Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Composite; Ambush Bug

File.Name: 6817f_6817-169.jpg
Notes: These are not squid tentacles or starfish roe - this is a close shot under the pinnae of a native Narrowleaf Sword Fern (Polystichum imbricans) fern in the Wood Fern (Dryopteridaceae) family growing near the top of La Cumbre Peak, near 4,000 feet, one of the high points of our mountains. I've been looking for summer flowers, but I don't think this counts. Ferns and fern-allies are vascular plants but not quite flowering plants. I'm still impressed that they do their stuff during the harshest part of our summer-dry season when most plants - especially fens - are done. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 August 2006)

File.Name: 6818a_6818-183.jpg
Notes:
Little Leafcutter Bee or kin (order Hymenoptera, family Megachilidae) working the tiny disk flowers of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden with the ray flowers behind, like a rising sun. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2006)

It's usually easy to pick one top photo for the day, but every one of my photos today is gold!
CommonNames: Leafcutter Bee; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 6818b_6818-197.jpg
Notes:
Courting Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies on the native summer flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the canyon. I'm pretty sure that's the male fluttering above the famale pretending to feed on the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2006)

   "Had we but world enough, and time,
   This coyness, Lady, were no crime...

File.Name: 6818c_6818-191.jpg
Notes: I took a dozen photos today of this late-summer flower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) still flowering in the chaparrel on the dry side of the canyon - trying to get one decent photo in-between the wind gusts. I didn't notice the little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) until I looked at this photo on my computer screen. First look for the spider-shadow in the lower-left. Then notice the two white legs poking up on top left of center. Then maybe you can see the other two white legs crossing the gap between the petals on the right. I hope someone sees this remarkable scene besides me! (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2006)

File.Name: 6818d_6818-190.jpg
Notes: A "two-fer" pair of Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies working native summer flowers of native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family growing near the creek. Same species, but note the ccolor difference. The butterflies steal the show, but I'm more interested in these native flowers that wait until the end of our dry desert-like summer to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2006)

File.Name: 6818e_6818-177.jpg
Notes: Too many flies! I don't know what this bee-size fly is, but she's oviposting - laying eggs - on a leaf of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. Push lady, push! Please help me if you know. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2006)

File.Name: 6818f_6818-201.jpg
Notes: This impressive wasp flicked its wings like a Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae in the order Hymenoptera) on this flowerhead of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. It's proboscis is buried deep in a little disk flower. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2006)
CommonNames: Spider Wasp; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 6819a_6819-013.jpg
Notes:
I saw the shadow of a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on this native summer flower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) in the canyon yesterday. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=15248238

I went back today. The spider was still there holding a Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) as prey inside the flower. I tried to climb up the steep bank to get a photo looking down into the flower - little sword-like yuccas all around, and shaky footing - that didn't work. But at least I spooked the spider to carry its prey to the outside of the flower. What can I say? Life is beautiful. Life is harsh. (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2006)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Weed's Mariposa Lily; Wild Bee
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Calochortus weedii; vestus; Apoidea; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Calochortus weedii; vestus; Apoidea; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 6819b_6819-020.jpg
Notes:
A moth in the hand is worth... a photo at least! I believe this is a Chersis Sphinx (Sphinx chersis) moth (order Lepidoptera) , but compare the Elegant Sphinx (S. perelegans) if you care about those details. This elegant moth decided to perch on the upstairs screen door in the shade - easy to spot! I carefully nudged it onto my hand and moved it to the oak tree in front of our house. I paused in the sun for this photo in my hand. (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2006)

This moth is probably working the Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri) flowers in the garden right now as I write this caption. I have a nice sequence of this moth (kin anyway) working the flowers at night - click [Forward] from here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=8040448
CommonNames: Chersis Sphinx; Elegant Sphinx; Evening Primrose
ScientificName: Sphinx chersis; Lepidoptera; S. perelegans; Oenothera elata; hookeri
associatedTaxa: Sphinx chersis; Lepidoptera; S. perelegans; Oenothera elata; hookeri

File.Name: 6819c_6819-024.jpg
Notes:
Now this is camouflage! I believe this is a Chersis Sphinx (Sphinx chersis) moth, but compare the Elegant Sphinx (S. perelegans). I would never notice it resting on gray Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) bark like this, but this moth decided to perch on the upstairs screen door - easy to spot! I carefully nudged it onto my hand and moved it to the oak tree in front of our house for this photo. Wonderful how the black lines on its wings match the cracks in the oak bark. (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2006)

By the way, this IS a color photo!
CommonNames: Chersis Sphinx; Elegant Sphinx; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Sphinx chersis; S. perelegans; Quercus agrifolia
