File.Name: 6819d_6819-003.jpg
Notes: I'm not sure what kind of flies (order Diptera) these are, but I know what they're doing! I caught this handsome pair on the metal railing of the little bridge over the creek in our canyon. These pretty flies with patterned wings are twice the size of houseflies. I saw another one yesterday on the same metal railing. They fly with a loud buzzy sound. (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2006)

File.Name: 6819e_6818-195.jpg
Notes:
Courting Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies on native summer flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the canyon. I believe that's the frisky male below and the coy female above pretending to think about food. The female seems to have a larger "eye" and an extra white band on the front of the forewing. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2006)

I posted another photo of these frisky Buckeyes yesterday:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=15248416

File.Name: 6820a_6810-173.jpg
Notes: Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) resting on an antique rag rug that's airing out on a wall by the garden. Looking close at the original photo, I think I can spot two other parasitic critters hitching a ride on this lizard. There's something burrowed into its left leg at screen lower-right, and a dark spot I think is a tick at 10:00 o'clock up from that. (They may be impossible to see in this 500 pixel photo.) Every critter is a community - including you and me! (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2006)

File.Name: 6820b_6818-205.jpg
Notes: Male Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly working Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis) flowers in the garden. This common butterfly is an introduced European species that has made itself at home in California just like I have. I won't throw any stones. (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2006)

File.Name: 6820c_6818-182.jpg
Notes:
I believe this unusual fly (order Diptera) is a Soldier Fly in the Stratiomyidae family - maybe it's a Spotted Soldier Fly (Stratiomys maculosa), at least it's related. Look close at the "scutellum" - that dark crescent-moon-shaped structure on the back behind the thorax and between the rear legs - it has four conspicuous "scutellar spines" pointing hindward to photo-right. I don't think the Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illudens) has those spines. I don't know - it's some darn fly in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2006)

This fly is perched on a leaf of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. I didn't realize when I took the photo, but she's oviposting - laying eggs. I have another photo of the same fly showing the action at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=15247632
Locality: Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. I didn't realize when I took the photo
CommonNames: Soldier Fly; Spotted Soldier Fly; Black Soldier Fly; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy
ScientificName: Diptera; Stratiomyidae; Stratiomys maculosa; Rudbeckia hirta
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Stratiomyidae; Stratiomys maculosa; Rudbeckia hirta

File.Name: 6820d_6820-030.jpg
Notes: I'm collecting photos of the summer flowers in our Mediterranean climate summer-dry mountains. These are new flowers and seed heads of native Yellow-flowered Hawkweed (Hiericium argutum) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family growing on a rock ledge in the canyon. It's hard to get a meaningful photo of the long diffuse flower stalks of this plant - but I like this one. The flowers look like dandelion weeds, but they are special because they wait until late-summer to flower. These late-season flowers are important to keep our insects alive for next year. (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2006)

File.Name: 6820e_6811-185.jpg
Notes: Ripening tri-colored berries of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. Birds love these berries, though they are a powerful natural laxative that tastes bitter to me. No thanks. Birds are scarce in the mountains right now, but I hope they'll come home for the harvest. (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2006)

File.Name: 6820f_6815-102.jpg
Notes:
Some bird has been eating these spiny berries of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) on top of this wood post in the canyon. I guess it's a Rufous-sided Towhee (Pipilo erythrophthalmus) bird. Yesterday I watched a towhee very carfully nip the stem above a fruit and carry it away. I'll try to get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2006)

I can vouch that the spiny ripe berries are edible and tasty. The secret is to crunch them open with your front teeth, and then squeeze and suck the good stuff inside.

File.Name: 6821a_6821-074.jpg
Notes: Looking up to the sky at an American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly getting ready to work flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) flowers in the garden. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2006)
ScientificName: Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly getting ready to work flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly getting ready to work flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii

File.Name: 6821b_6821-070.jpg
Notes: Like Icarus, I shot too close to the sun - but sometimes you gotta go for it! This is a big Fly (order Diptera) - I guess a Horse Fly (Tabanus punctifer or kin) - working Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii or kin) flowers in the garden. The prisms in the sky all have seven sides - something to do with the seven-sided diaphram of my lens. Getting sharp photos isn't everything! (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2006)

File.Name: 6821c_6821-047.jpg
Notes: You gotta feel joyous looking at this photo! These are real native wildflowers of Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca) in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family. It's a fire-follower plant growing in a place in the mountains that burned last year. I don't think of this as a summer flower - most of the plants are done - but here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2006)

File.Name: 6821d_6821-063.jpg
Notes:
Pubescent Katydid (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) insect on a leaf in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2006)

This beautiful katydid has its training wings, but it has (at least) two more molts to go. It's a step beyond the pretty nymphs:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=15157527

But next it will become an almost-adolescent with longer wings and less color:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=15157384

And finally a real adult:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=8580831

It's fun watching the changes every summer. The katydids start so beautiful like jewelled ponies and end up as big green mature bugs. I guess that's how it goes! ;=)

File.Name: 6821e_6821-039.jpg
Notes: The name of this beautiful native summer flower is a mouthful: Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea). (I prefer the old name that is rich with local history.) It's a local endemic species, but common in our mountains. I don't know when these morning flowers open, but they melt away before noon. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2006)

File.Name: 6821f_6811-181.jpg
Notes: Elegant summer flower of native Cliff Desert Dandelion (Malacothrix saxatilis var. tenuifolia). The plants look kind of weedy, but the composite flowers really are elegant up close like this. All our native summer flowers are all remarkable in our summer-desert mountains! (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2006)

File.Name: 6822a_6822-079.jpg
Notes: A yin-yang pair of Honey Bees (Apis mellifera in the Hymenoptera order) working a composite Dahlia flower in the garden. Look close and you might see that they both have full loads of sticky orange pollen on their hind legs to take back to the hive. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2006)

File.Name: 6822b_6822-115.jpg
Notes:
Pretty little Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo or kin) - I guess Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti - butterfly working a flower of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. I saw another of these butterflies on my morning hike a few miles away, but I missed the photo. Then I found this one in the garden when I got home. I guess it's their time! (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2006)

This butterfly went 'round and 'round the cone of the composite flower for at least 1/2 hour working the tiny disk flowers. Other butterflies skip around from flower to flower. It was breezy and the butterfly kept moving and flicking its wings - hard to capture - I took 100 photos and kept four. I hope this butterfly is still here tomorrow before the wind kicks up. I've been seeing the same kinds of butterflies every day, so it's a treat to see something new.

The Metalmark (family Riodonidae) butterflies have a certain shape with angular wings that I recognize right off in our mountains at least. These are the butterflies that author (and lepidopterist) Vladimir Nabokov ("Lolita" "Pale Fire" and lots more) studied and revised - not our two species, but the remarkable variety in South America that I hope to see.
Locality: Lolita" "Pale Fire" and lots more) studied and revised - not our two species
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy; Metalmark
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Rudbeckia hirta; Riodonidae
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Rudbeckia hirta; Riodonidae

File.Name: 6822c_6822-085.jpg
Notes:
Adult Katydid (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) insect on a leaf in the garden. This critter has earned its wings! I guess it's a Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata). The wings sure look like fresh green leaves - great camouflage - but that's the stumper. This is the summer-dry season in our mountains. There are native flowers with flowers, but no plants with "fresh green leaves" that I can think of, except in our garden. Why does this insect mature NOW? (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2006)

I posted a photo of another not-quite mature Katydid yesterday. It's another stumper that the immature insects are more colorful than the adults. Shouldn't the kids have better camouflage?
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=15300052

File.Name: 6822d_6822-107.jpg
Notes: Female California Dogface (Colias eurydice aka Zerene eurydice) butterfly pulling a scam on this non-natiive Fuschia flower in the garden. Instead of going up through the throat of the flower, it's working its proboscis "under the skirts" behind the petals to go right to the good stuff. Many insects know this trick for many flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2006)

File.Name: 6822e_6818-187.jpg
Notes: Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working summer flowers of native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family growing near the creek in our canyon. These mint-relative flowers grow in a head with an cup-like "involucre" that holds the flowers like a composite flower. (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2006)

File.Name: 6822f_6821-036.jpg
Notes: Tiny Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) on an early morning flower of Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea). These native summer-flowers have just started flowering when our summer-dry mountains are about as dry as they get. This little bee seems to be exploring the stamens, but it was really interested in the nectar at the base of the flowers. I also saw a Grass-Skipper (order Lepidoptera - subfamily Hesperiinae - genus Hesperia) - butterfly visit these flowers. I don't usually get out early enough to catch these flowers in bloom. (San Marcos Pass, 21 August 2006)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Grass-Skipper
ScientificName: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Lepidoptera; Hesperiinae; Hesperia
associatedTaxa: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Lepidoptera; Hesperiinae; Hesperia

File.Name: 6823a_6823-141.jpg
Notes:
Looking close at a pretty little Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - I guess Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti - working tiny disk-flowers on the cone of this Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. This uncommon late-season butterfly is catching a gleam in its eyes from the yellow ray-flowers reflecting the afternoon sun. Just another everyday wonder in the garden right ouitside the front door! (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2006)

*Sigh* We are usually so busy we walk right past...
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Rudbeckia hirta
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Rudbeckia hirta

File.Name: 6823b_6823-136.jpg
Notes: Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens) with a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) prey hanging under summer flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) on the dry wash along the river. Harsh for the bee, but good for the spider. I've been noticing more and more preditors in the woods as summer moves on. Insects get scarce at the end of summer - it's no mystery. I show a few more preditors in today's photos. (Santa Ynez River, 23 August 2006)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Honey Bee; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Apis mellifera; Eriogonum fasciculatum
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; Apis mellifera; Eriogonum fasciculatum

File.Name: 6823c_6823-127.jpg
Notes: Male Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working new sunner flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) that is just starting to flower along the dry river bed. The dry wash along the not-quite-dry river is our most desert-like habitat. There's a group of yellow-flowered Composites in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family that are just starting to flower on the dry wash along the river. These late-summer plants are important to keep our summer insects alive. It was windy this morning, but I'll be back. My photos near the river today are all near Sage Hill. (Santa Ynez River, 23 August 2006)
CommonNames: Northern White-Skipper; Scale Broom; Composites; Sunflower

File.Name: 6823d_6823-147.jpg
Notes: Once you notice these tiny Ambush Bugs in the family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera, you start seeing them everywhere! This little preditor is perched on the cone of a composite Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) flower in the garden. I think of them sitting still like this and waiting for hours, but this preditor kept moving around looking for the right position and finally flew off. I also saw them on the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) flowers in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2006)
CommonNames: Ambush Bugs; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy; Butterfly Bush
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Rudbeckia hirta; Buddleia davidii
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Rudbeckia hirta; Buddleia davidii

File.Name: 6823e_6823-132.jpg
Notes: I'm sure this insect preditor is an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera hunting on summer flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) on the dry wash along the river. Notice its mighty sword-like proboscis folded under it's not-much-thicker head. D'oh - it's anatomy, not politics! (Santa Ynez River, 23 August 2006)

File.Name: 6823f_6823-126.jpg
Notes: Pretty Wasp (order Hymenoptera) - not one of the usual wasps - working new native flowers of composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) starting to flower along the dry river bed. There's no mystery how pollination happens - the wasp is dusted with yellow pollen. It seems painted with faces! (Santa Ynez River, 23 August 2006)

File.Name: 6824a_6824-167.jpg
Notes:
Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) at the feeder in the garden with the sun behind. I reckon she's a "she" because of the small dark patch on her chin - compare my [Previous] photo to see a male with a large patch. These dark patches show brilliant iridescent red color when the sun is just right. Is there a perfect angle for a photo? (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2006)

Look close at the feeder - there's a reflection of the woods upside down, like a jar full of nature! But it's just an illusion - nothing but a 25% sugar solution. That's why I don't approve of feeding hummingbirds - or any wild critters. The hummers love the feeder and fight over it all day long - instead of exploring the woods like they should be doing. They buzz my face when it's empty! But this is a harsh summer, and the usual summer hummingbird flowers like California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) aren't doing well this year.

The hummingbird feeder is great fun to watch. The male hummer in the [Previous] photos seems to have claimed this feeder and surrounding garden. But there are two (or three?) females testing his authority. One flies in and the male chases it away - but then the other comes in for a sip of sugar water. I wonder if the ladies take turns? I wish they wore little hummer-jerseies with numbers so I could keep score!

File.Name: 6824b_6824-168.jpg
Notes: Male Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) at the feeder with the sun behind. You can see some red color on its throat, but not the brilliant iridescent colors I see as they fly around. I show the same butterfly in my [Previous] photo from the opposite side. Sun in front or behind isn't enough to show the amazing colors. Is there a perfect angle? (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2006)

File.Name: 6824c_6824-164.jpg
Notes: Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) at the feeder near the garden with the sun on the far side. I was sitting in the sun across this photo, and then moved to the shady side. I forgot to reset my camera exposure, but I like this silhouette. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2006)

File.Name: 6824d_6824-162.jpg
Notes:
Male Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) at the feeder near the garden. I don't really approve of feeding hummingbirds - or any wild critters - at least not here in the woods far from the city. But it's a harsh summer and the usual summer hummingbird flowers like California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) aren't doing well this year. I got a few photos like this with the sun behind me. It was too hot, so I moved to the other side of the feeder for the next few photos. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2006)

I don't really approve, but our hummingbird feeder has been great fun to watch. This male hummer has claimed the feeder and nearby garden. But there are two (or three?) females testing his authority. One flies in and the male chases it away - but then the other comes in for a sip of sugar water. I wonder if they take turns?

File.Name: 6824e_6824-161.jpg
Notes: Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) flowers in the garden, with some Zinnias and Dahlias mixed in. These old-fashioned flowers are a perfect mix for butterflies. Avoid all "double flowers" - the butterflies can't reach the goodies! (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2006)

File.Name: 6824f_6823-142.jpg
Notes: One more photo of the pretty little Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - I guess Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti - working tiny disk-flowers on the cone of this Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. I got this photo yesterday - it shows the underside of the wings. I didn't see this butterfly in the garden today. There are so many perils for these critters in the woods - interesting hungry preditors! - this may be the only one I'll see this summer. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2006)
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Rudbeckia hirta
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Rudbeckia hirta

File.Name: 6825a_6825-177.jpg
Notes:
Sometimes you attract more butterflies with mud than flowers!

This is looking very close at a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly "puddling" on wet ground near the garden where the sprinkler went too far. Look how the butterfly's proboscis is bent under at the end for better contact with the earth. In another photo, I can see that it is actually pressing into the soil to bring up the water, like you make little puddles standing on wet sand at the beach. These butterflies have been common all summer, but they rarely visit flowers. Usually the butterflies are camera shy, but this one let me get close. See my [Previous] photo to view the upper wings. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2006)

Busy day. I had a break at noon to get a few photos in the garden. Nothing new, but I got some nice photos of beautiful critter-friends that I will photograph again and again. School starts next week. Then I get really busy!

File.Name: 6825b_6825-186.jpg
Notes:
California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly "puddling" on wet ground near the garden. I reckon it's getting moisture or salts from the damp soil. (See my [Next] photo.) These pretty butterflies rarely visit flowers. They mostly fly back and forth through the oak woods chasing away other butterflies. How do they fuel all that activity? (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2006)

We also have the Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly with very similar colors and habits. I usually see it near the creek in the canyon, though it sometimes visits the garden. You can see in this photo that the Sister has a dark band around the end of the forewing. The Admiral's orange spot reaches the edge. The Sister especially looks almost black in the air. I'm surprised by her rich chocolate-brown colors up close.
Locality: Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly with very similar colors and habits. I usually see it near the creek in the canyon

File.Name: 6825c_6825-171.jpg
Notes: I couldn't resist cropping tight into my [Previous] photo. This is an adult Katydid (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) - maybe a Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata) - on a leaf in the garden. Everything is strange when you get this close to another species/genus/family/order/class/phylum... further in the tree is stranger! I love the katydid's two-toned eyes. They seem to have dark "eyeballs" that look at me from any angle in every photo, but that can't be right with insect compound eyes. The mouthparts are really strange - like little articulated arms. (Like Bender playing washboard if you know Futurama on TV.) Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2006)

File.Name: 6825d_6825-171.jpg
Notes: Face to face with an adult Katydid (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) - maybe a Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata) - on a leaf in the garden. It's fun to photograph these critters because they're big and don't move much (or attack!) so I can get up close and personal like this. See my [Next] photo for an even closer look! (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2006)

File.Name: 6825e_6825-187.jpg
Notes: Cactus leaves aren't quite as scarce as hen's teeth - I only see those in cartoons, and here are real cactus leaves! All (or many?) cacti have leaves, but they usually fall away early leaving just the spines behind. I have no idea what species this Cactus (family Cactaceae) in the garden is or where it came from. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2006)

File.Name: 6825f_6818-200.jpg
Notes: Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) resting on sandy ground near the garden. I boosted the exposure a stop to bring out the shadows. I love all the unexpected color on these lizards - like the surprising little brush strokes of color you see looking close at impressionist paintings. I don't see those turquoise spots from a distance, but they enrich the colors I do see. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2006)

File.Name: 6826b_6826-010.jpg
Notes: Pretty little Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae) working a composite Zinnia flower in the garden. What a way to make a living! (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2006)

File.Name: 6826c_6826-004.jpg
Notes: Nasty-looking little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a Dahlia flower in the garden. Pollinator insects start getting scarce at the end of summer. Here's why! (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2006)

File.Name: 6826d_6826-011.jpg
Notes: With those two eye-spots on the hindwing, this must be an American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly perched on this native summer flower head of native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family growing near the creek. But this pretty lady has no color on her underwigs, and she doesn't flex her wings while perched - unlike the usual American Ladies at our flowers. It's hard to notice something different. But I've been walking the canyon almost every day this summer getting photos - especially butterflies - and this pretty lady is different in looks and behavior! (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2006)
ScientificName: Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly perched on this native summer flower head of native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly perched on this native summer flower head of native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata; Lamiaceae

File.Name: 6826e_6826-013.jpg
Notes: Pretty little Damselfly (Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) perched parallel to a stem in the woods. Good camouflage for critters who see double! (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2006)

File.Name: 6826f_6826-001.jpg
Notes: Butterfly battle with shadows! Usually I see male and female butterflies in one species go at it, but this is a tussle between a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly (left) and an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly (right). Hard to see in this 500 pixel photo, but the butterflies are facing each other - underside to underside, mating position. Note the color differences - black along the wing or across it. A moment later, they fluttered away to different parts of the garden. Que pasa? (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2006)

File.Name: 6827a_6827-023.jpg
Notes:
Not-quite-ripe acorns of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). Acorns? There's just one acorn left, and even that has a hole where some little critter burrowed in (or out). (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2006)

I go back to school tomorrow - a week of getting ready, and then the real thing. Just as well, it's getting harder and harder to get new photos everyday in the canyon as summer heads to autumn.


   Butterflies flutter
   in my garden and stomach
   flutter back to work

File.Name: 6827b_6827-031.jpg
Notes: Beautiful chrysalis - the shell of a butterfly pupa - on a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) shrub in the mountains. This chrysalis is empty. I wonder what butterfly fluttered away from this? (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2006)

File.Name: 6827c_6827-024.jpg
Notes: Gall Wasp (family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) stem gall on a scrub oak form of Canyon Live Oak aka Goldencup Oak (Quercus chrysolepis). You can pull away some oak galls like "oak apples" - see my [Previous] photo - but this stem gall is non-detachable. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2006)
CommonNames: Gall Wasp; Canyon Live Oak; Goldencup Oak

File.Name: 6827d_6827-020.jpg
Notes: New "oak apple" gall on a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) shrub in the mountains. Oaks make acorns of course. These galls are really tumors created by tiny Gall Wasps in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2006)

File.Name: 6827e_6827-025.jpg
Notes: Brilliant ripe berries of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. The berries look so brilliant, but they taste btter to me - maybe the birds think so too. Now that's a stumper - why would any plant produce a berry that no critter wants to eat? (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2006)

File.Name: 6827f_6827-039.jpg
Notes: You never know what you'll find in the woods! I found this power pole adorned with bottle caps in the mountains today. Usually I go back on the low trail, but today I hiked up the high trail looking for something different, and here it is. Every cap is nailed in. Whoever created this monument has good taste in beer and stamina to get here again and again and again... (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2006)

File.Name: 6828a_6828-047.jpg
Notes:
A "two-fer" pair of brilliant male California Dogface (Colias eurydice aka Zerene eurydice) butterflies working a composite Zinnia flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2006)

School is starting, but I got to work at home today and managed to get a few photos in the garden.

File.Name: 6828b_6828-050.jpg
Notes: Perfect little Leafcutter Bee or kin (order Hymenoptera, family Megachilidae) working the tiny disk flowers of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. It's dragging it's butt over the little disk flowers to collect pollen under its abdomen. But look closer - it's also sucking one of the tiny flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2006)
CommonNames: Leafcutter Bee; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 6828c_6828-054.jpg
Notes: Little Short-Horned Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Acrididae) munching some flower part of the cone of a Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) flower in the garden. The yellow-orange ray flowers make such a brilliant background for insect photos! (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2006)
CommonNames: Short-Horned Grasshopper; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 6828d_6828-063.jpg
Notes: I guess this is an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) flowers near our mailbox in the canyon. You can see some glitter from the metallic spots on its hindwing. These blues were common last year, but I've only captured a few photos this summer. Every year is different, and I love fotolog for motivating me to keep this day-by-day photo diary of what I see in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2006)

File.Name: 6828e_6828-064.jpg
Notes: I'm not sure what this impressive large wasp is - I see them now and then in the woods and the garden, but they don't pose for photos. Maybe it's a Digger Wasp (family Sphecidae, in the order Hymenoptera)? (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2006)

File.Name: 6828f_6828-067.jpg
Notes: The remains of a Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly caught in a spider web in the garden. Look at all the life in your garden and think about winter to come. All of those beautiful critters will die in the hands of one preditor or another. And then the preditors will disappear until next year. Nature is a harsh mother, and every year is born new. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2006)

File.Name: 6829a_6829-070.jpg
Notes:
Remarkable wasp-mimic fly! I believe this is a Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae in the insect order Diptera). Compare my [Previous] photo to see what an amazing mimic this fly is. It's working new summer flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family growing on the dry wash near the river. Ain't nature grand! (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2006)

The late-summer composite flowers like Scale Broom keep our summer insects alive during the hardest time of our summer-dry season. I got today's photos along the dry river wash near Sage Hill in Paradise - I stopped by on the way to school this morning. Tomorrow is a full school day and classes start next week.
CommonNames: Thick-headed Fly; Scale Broom; Sunflower; Scale Broom

File.Name: 6829b_6829-078.jpg
Notes:
Pretty wasp, but I kept my distance! This wasp has the size and stature of a Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera), but the colors are more interesting. I guess this is another Vespid wasp, but I don't know anything about it except that it doesn't build nests under our porch! The wasp is working new summer flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family that are just starting to appear on the dry wash near the river. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2006)

Compare the wasp-mimic fly in my [Next] photo. Did you notice the Leafcutter Bee (Megachilidae) in the background?
CommonNames: Golden Paper Wasp; Scale Broom; Sunflower; Leafcutter Bee
ScientificName: Polistes fuscatus; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Polistes fuscatus; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Megachilidae

File.Name: 6829c_6829-074.jpg
Notes: Little Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - I guess Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti working new summer flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family on the dry wash near the river. This tattered butterfly is a veteran with scars to prove its duty. I firsat noticed these butterflies a few weeks ago, already tattered. They must be migrating from somewhere else, bringing their scars with them. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2006)
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: 6829d_6829-076.jpg
Notes:
Tiny needle-like Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae in the order Hymenoptera) working new flowers of the native composite shrub Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) that are just starting to flower along the river. The wasp is an awesome preditor that uses its long ovipositor "stinger" to lay eggs in critters hidden in wood or under ground to nourish its babies. The wasp's eggs need protein to develop, but the adults need sweet nectar from flowers for energy. This is a common pattern in most wasps. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2006)

Look close - do you notice something unusual about this unusual wasp? I'll post another photo tomorrow to reveal this stumper!

The dry wash along the not-quite-dry river is our most desert-like habitat at the end of summer, but these late-summer yellow-flowered Composites in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family are just starting to flower. They keep our summer insects alive during the hard time. My photos today are all along the river near Sage Hill in Paradise.
CommonNames: Ichneumon Wasp; Scale Broom; Composites; Sunflower

File.Name: 6829e_6829-086.jpg
Notes: Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens) with its prey hanging in the native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) flowers near the river. I guess the unfortunate prey is a Leafcutter Bee or kin (order Hymenoptera, family Megachilidae). Can you see the scary "face" on the abdomen of the spider? See my [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2006)

File.Name: 6829f_6829-089.jpg
Notes:
Yipes - this is looking close at a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens) hanging in the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) flowers near the river. Spiders have two body parts - head below with eyes at the bottom, and abdomen above in this photo. Look at that astounding "face" on the abdomen. It looks like a demon wolf to me - it even has eyelashes! I don't know if there's a name for this kind of mimicry, but there should be. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2006)

What an image. That face would work as a tattoo or a heavy metal cover! Isn't it remarkable that the markings of this spider resonate with us humans so many branches away on the tree of life!

If I didn't find something surprising like this in the woods almost every day that blows me away, I wouldn't work so hard getting these photos. But the closer I look, the more I find!

File.Name: 6830a_6829-082.jpg
Notes:
Eye to eye with a little Damselfly (Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) near the river. With eyes like that, you can't help but be eye to eye! You can't sneak up on these critters, but sometimes you can get close if you move slow and don't surprise them. (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2006)

Damselflies and dragonflies have compound eyes with up to 30,000 individual units called ommatidia. Something happens as insect eyes become this complicated - they look deep and mysterious, and that dark spot like an eyeball that always seems to look right at me. I'm sure it's a matter of optics. I wonder if I could duplicate the effect with a trimmed bundle of hair-like optical fibers?

File.Name: 6830b_6828-056.jpg
Notes:
Bright yellow Ambush Bug in the family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera waiting for prey on a dark flower cone of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden.

D'oh, that is not camouflage! I often find rusty-white Ambush Bugs on the rusty-white buckwheat flowers, and bright yellow bugs on the bright yellow on the yellow goldenrod flowers. I'm not sure if these preditors are real color-shifters or just choose their flowers to match. Either way, this Ambush Bug isn't doing it right!
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 6830c_6829-077.jpg
Notes:
Looking closer at a Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae in the order Hymenoptera) working new flowers of the native composite shrub Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) that are just starting to flower along the river. Check my photo yesterday to see its long ovipositor "stinger":
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=15450804

But this close shot is interesting too. Note how it's missing middle and hind legs on the left side. These late summer insects are veterans with many stories to tell. I love the way the abdomen sticks up like Dizzy Gillespie's horn! (Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2006)

Little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a beautiful Zinnia flower in the garden opposite a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice aka Zerene eurydice) butterfly. Even in our sweet garden just outside the door, life is dangerous - for spider and butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2006)

File.Name: 6902f_6901-107.jpg
Notes:
I didn't expect to stump the smart fotolog naturalists like CHiS and N@no and everyone else - like Justmouse - who thought it "looks like a spider's BUTT" - see my [Previous] photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=15506625

The spider's abdomen is remarkable - this photo shows the whole spider. It's a large Argiope Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae - genus Argiope) on its web in the native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) flowers by our mailbox. The horns on the abdomen and the cartoon "face" on its tail are surprising. Many spiders who sit motiomless and wait - the best camouflage - have "faces" on their abdomens. But why do so many spiders have "faces" the we humans recognize? (Think about spider preditors!)

File.Name: 6903a_6903-084.jpg
Notes: Cute Fly are words we don't usually put together - a real oxymoron - but this golden Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae) really is a cute fly. I know this photo is not quite sharp - but some of it is! This golden fly is working new flowers of the native composite shrub Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash by the river, just a few miles away from home as the birds fly. The dry river wash is almost desert at the end of summer, but the late-summer flowering composite shrubs in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family attract many interesting insects. + (Santa Ynez River, 3 September 2006)

File.Name: 6903b_6903-097.jpg
Notes: Strange Treehopper (family Membracidae in the order Homoptera) on a flower cone of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. This critter is camera shy - I only got this one shot. Odd shape. The top of this insect is mostly the pronotum of the thorax that covers the head and much of the abdomen. The brown spot that looks like an eye and eyebrow is just a spot. The real eye is down towards the insect's left-front knee. I see a dark "eyeball" on this treehopper's eye, so it must have well-developed eyes with many facets. It sure saw me coming and got away fast! (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2006)

File.Name: 6903c_6903-088.jpg
Notes: Pretty little Vespid Wasp (order Hymenoptera, family Vespidae) working new flowers of the native composite shrub Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash by the river. This might be the same kind of wasp that fell prey to the spider in my [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez River, 3 September 2006)

File.Name: 6903d_6903-091.jpg
Notes:
Another Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens) with wasp (Hymenoptera) prey in a native composite shrub of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash by the river. Notice the demon wolf-like "face" on its abdomen - then notice the spooky white "face" with real eyes in the center of the photo! Why do these spiders have designs that we recognize as faces and emblems? (Santa Ynez River, 3 September 2006)

I just noticed - look how the "wolf face" pattern on the rear abdomen continues onto the front cephalothorax. This must be an illusion - these are separate body parts!

File.Name: 6903e_6903-078.jpg
Notes: This photo is disturbing in many ways. It's an Argiope Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae - genus Argiope) on a native composite shrub of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash by the river. The "face" on the spider's abdomen is disturbing. See my [Previous] photo to get more disturbed! The spider is wrapping its Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) prey - probably just paralyzed and still alive to provide food for its young. Now look close at the bottom of the photo - there's a tiny Fly (order Diptera) oviposting - laying eggs - that will feed on the bee and maybe the baby spiders within. (Santa Ynez River, 3 September 2006)

File.Name: 6903f_6903-076.jpg
Notes: I've noticed a few Argiope Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae - genus Argiope) in the woods - now I see them everywhere, many different kinds. This is the amazing "face" on the underside of an Argiope spider on a native composite shrub Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash by the river a few miles away from home as the birds fly. Look close to see the white "eyes". I understand why natural selection would favor spiders who sit on their webs in the open with a scary face on their back - but why such sad eyes? Why should anything about spiders look familiar to us humans? (Santa Ynez River, 3 September 2006)

We moved school to Sanddollar Beach on the wild Big Sur coast for our first week back to school. It's a perfect way to get to know each other before we hit the books. I was busy with my classes on the beach and other teacher stuff. I took hundreds of school photos, but I managed to get a few "Treebeard Photos" as well.

File.Name: 6909a_6909-008.jpg
Notes:
Like a spider rodeo! A moment ago, this Crab Spider (Thomisidae) was perched on a Composite flower in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family - maybe a horticultural Sneezeweed (Helenium)? The spider grabbed the butterfly and jumped - or was pulled - off the flower, but the spider has a "safety line" web tied to the flower. They fluttered around for a few seconds until the spider subdued its prey. The spider is still holding the web-line with its screen-left top leg - maybe you can see the single web-strand above. The faux "face" on the spider's abdomen makes this look even more like a rodeo! (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2006)

I guess the unfortunate butterfly is a Duskywing Skipper (genus Erynnis) - maybe a Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius). All the butterflies and other summer insects start disappearing at the end of summer. These spiders are part of the reason why.

I have more Big Sur photos, but I'd rather post today's photos today. I'll catch up next week when I'm back to school.

File.Name: 6915a_6908-413.jpg
Notes:
Pretty new composite flowers of Common Aster (Aster chilensis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family, still wet with morning dew near our Plaskett Creek camp last week. (Big Sur, 8 September 2006)

The late-summer flowers are pretty, but the scientific name is a stumper. This native wildflower only grows in California and Oregon, but the name "Aster Chilensis" surely means "Chilean Aster" - the aster from Chile. Que pasa?

It's a fascinating story. The plant was first collected by the Bohemian (Czech) naturalist Tade·ö Haenke aka Tadeo or Thaddeus Haenke who served as the botanist on a Spanish naval scientific voyage under the command of Captain Alejandro Malaspina that sailed up the Pacific coast of the Americas from Santiago de Chile to Alaska between 1790 and 1792. Haenke collected thousands of plant specimens on this voyage - including Redwood (Sequoia sempervirens). He never made it back to Europe - he died in Bolivia in 1817 - but his plant collections did make it back to Spain for study.

Haenke's herbarium specimen of this aster was finally named "Aster chilensis" because it was thought to come from Chile. According to the "The International Code of Botanical Nomenclature, The St. Louis Code of 1999" the earliest name takes precedence. And so the "Chilean Aster" is a California native. It's a mistake we must live with!

I want to know more about Tade·ö Haenke. He's one of the great naturalists like David Douglas and Joseph Banks and Alexander von Humboldt. His life sounds like fiction. There's a facinating introduction at:
   http://www.ou.edu/cas/botany-micro/ben/ben288.html
Locality:

File.Name: 6915b_6908-419.jpg
Notes: Ripe berries of Red Flowering Currant (Ribes sanguineum var. glutinosum) in the woods along Plaskett Creek near our campground last week. The glandular berries don`t taste very good. (Big Sur, 8 September 2006)

File.Name: 6916a_6916-056.jpg
Notes:
Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens) converging on native plants of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) near the creek. These Lady Beetles aka Lady Bugs are in the Coccinellidae family of the order Coleoptera. I usually find them converging in winter after Christmas, but here they are in the canyon today. Is this a sign of an early winter? (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2006)

With school back in session, it's a pleasure to hike our canyon with my camera after a week or two away! The seasons are changing as we get closer the equinox, and the woods are changing. Every photo today is gold!

File.Name: 6916b_6916-061.jpg
Notes: Perfect Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the canyon near the creek. I missed my first photo-op, but the butterfly came back to the same roost for take-2 a few minutes later. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2006)

File.Name: 6916c_6916-019.jpg
Notes: Common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) dusted with pollen working new flowers of native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) that's just starting to flower in the canyon. It's a dirty job, but someone has to do it! Note the bee's pollen-dusted face and full pollen baskets. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2006)

File.Name: 6916d_6916-038.jpg
Notes: Tiny Fly (order Diptera) perched on a flower of native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family growing in the canyon. The fly has long legs and an amazing long proboscis - notice the "high tide" pollen marks on its proboscis and antennae to see how far it delves into these flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2006)

File.Name: 6916e_6916-083.jpg
Notes: The circle of life - a Leafcutter Bee (Megachilidae) on the left and a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) preditor - its upraised claws at least - on the right. The flowers are a non-native composite Goldenrod (Solidago) in the garden. We're close to the equinox next week. The seasons really are changing, summer insects are geting scarce. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2006)

File.Name: 6916f_6916-066.jpg
Notes: Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly working male flowers of the native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) that's just starting to flower in the canyon. These late-summer flowers are important to keep our insects alive during our dry season. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2006)

File.Name: 6917a_6917-107.jpg
Notes:
Astounding Great Purple Hairstreak (Atlides halesus) butterfly working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. It rubs its hind wings together like other hairstreaks - offering a glimpse of the brilliant iridescent blue upper wings when I get the timing right. Why is any creature so beautiful? I got most of today's photos near Sage Hill in Paradise today. (Santa Ynez River, 17 September 2006)

This is an uncommon butterfly in our mountains. I got another photo last year on October 1, close to today:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=11131623

The late-summer season must be their time. The equinox is next week. The wooods are changing.

File.Name: 6917b_6917-097.jpg
Notes: Huge dragonfly-size Tarantula Hawk (order Hymenoptera, genus Pepsis) wasp sprawled over many new flowers of a native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river, like an awkward NBA basketball player in a child's room! This is our largest wasp - one of the biggest in the world. Compare [Next] and [Previous] photos to get a sence of scale. They "sting" and paralyze California Tarantula (Aphonopelma reversum) spiders and drag them to a burrow for the wasp larvae to consume. The growing wasp kids need a protein meal, but the adults get their nectar energy boost from these flowers. (Santa Ynez River, 17 September 2006)

File.Name: 6917d_6917-099.jpg
Notes: Mating True Bugs (order Hemiptera) on native flowers of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family on the dry river wash near Sage Hill campground. (Santa Ynez River, 17 September 2006)

File.Name: 6917e_6917-091.jpg
Notes: Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - I guess Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti. The angular wings are distinctive. It's working the new late-summer flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family on the dry wash of the river near Sage Hill campground. The dry river wash is not far from the river, but it's our most desert-like habitat at the end of summer. I got these photos near Sage Hill in Paradise today. (Santa Ynez River, 17 September 2006)

File.Name: 6917f_6917-133.jpg
Notes: Adult Katydid (order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) - probably a Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata) - munching a flower of Canna Lily in the garden. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2006)

File.Name: 6918a_6916-048.jpg
Notes:
Little Grass-Skipper (order Lepidoptera - subfamily Hesperiinae - genus Hesperia) - butterfly working a new composite flower of native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family growing in the canyon. I love these late-summer plants that start to flower at the end of our desert-dry summer season. They keep the insects alive. There's a stumper - and maybe a science fair project - here! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2006)

Busy school week and weekend ahead. I'll do my best to post new photos!

Notes: I guess this is a white-fringed Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis, but cp. E. tristis) skipper butterfly visiting late-summer flowers of native California Goldenrod aka Oreja de Liebre (Solidago californica) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family that are just starting to flower in our canyon. Notes: Little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family still flowering in the canyon. I love these late summer flowers in our summer-dry mountains! The spider doesn't have camouflage colors, but maybe the long legs and long stamens count as cryptic camouflage, like a zebra in the sun-striped grassland. I almost walked past. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2006)

File.Name: 6919b_6916-080.jpg
Notes: Impressive little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a flower stalk of a non-native composite Goldenrod (Solidago) in the garden. Most of these little crab spiders are color-shifters that match their flower to the flower, but the black crab-legs of this spider really stand out. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2006)

File.Name: 6919c_6917-122.jpg
Notes: Tiny spiderlings on a web in the woods. I reckon they have an Orb-Weaver (family Araneidae) look. Hard to photograph so many little spiders at one time. Hard to photograph so many little spiders at one time. I took many photos, but I like this one that shows a few and hints of many more. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2006)

File.Name: 6920a_6918-153.jpg
Notes: Finger-sized Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) resting on the fingers of one of my students at school. The little lizard seems to like all the attention! (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 September 2006)

File.Name: 6921a_6921-211.jpg
Notes:
It's sleepy time for this little Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna).

I got home from school near 6:00 p.m. tonight as the evening shadows were moving over the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the canyon. I spotted something in the tree as the sun was setting. No time to get my tripod, but I managed to capture this photo with my big lens propped up with a couple of books on our porch railing. I came back 20 minutes later, and the hummer was gone. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2006)

File.Name: 6922a_6922-221.jpg
Notes:
Looking very close at a fluffed-up Band-Tailed Pigeon (Columba fasciata) under the oaks along our road this morning. It let me get so close that I wondered if it was injured or sick, but then it flew away into the woods. This is a wary native woodland pigeon, not a city pigeon. The yellow bill and white collar on the back of its head are distinctive. A flock of these native birds are living in our canyon waiting for the fall acorns to ripen. It's our largest pigeon - they weigh nearly a pound. In different times, this bird would be tonight's dinner instead of tonight's photo! (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2006)

Compare my photo of a non-native Rock Dove aka City Pigeon (Columba livia) at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=13668066
CommonNames: Band-Tailed Pigeon; Rock Dove; City Pigeon

File.Name: 6922b_6922-218.jpg
Notes: I spotted this fluffed-up Band-Tailed Pigeon (Columba fasciata) under the oaks along our road this morning. It let me get so close that I wondered if it was injured or sick, but then it flew away into the woods. This is a wary native woodland pigeon, not a city pigeon. See my [Next photo]. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2006)

File.Name: 6922c_6921-212.jpg
Notes: Little lady Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) nodding off on a branch of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near the porch last night as evening shadows growon the last day of summer. Compare my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2006)

File.Name: 6923a_6923-111.jpg
Notes:
Fire in the mountains, and fire weather over the ocean - not weather that makes fire, but weather made by fire. Here is the sunset tonight looking towards the Santa Barbara Channel as the sun sinks through a plume of wildfire smoke from the "Day Fire" in the backcountry behind. There's a ring around the sun - but it's more of an egg-shaped oval. (Or is it bigger and fish-shaped?) Scary weather. I like the offshore oil rigs and the railroad beacon that add to the effect. I cropped and resized this photo, but did nothing else. It is a bit underexposed to saturate the colors. I tried playing with color and contrast, but the original is best. (Santa Barbara Channel, 23 September 2006)

We spent the day hiking the trail between Prisoner's Harbor and Pelican Bay on Santa Cruz Island, the other side of the channel. The offshore Santa Ana winds - aka Santana or Devil winds - kicked up overnight, and they kicked up the 100,000+ acre wildfire in the backcountry. But the winds were good for our island trip because they blew the smoke west towards Santa Barbara away from our island. I got this photo as we drove back home in the evening. It was worth a stop.

I got some photos on the island despite the wind. But I'm tired and sore tonight. More tomorrow.

There's a MODIS satellite image that shows how the smoke missed us on the islands today at:
   http://activefiremaps.fs.fed.us/imagery.php?op=fire&day=2006266&fireID=ca-lpf-2023
Click the [Imagery] tab to see more.

File.Name: 6924a_6923-107.jpg
Notes:
We were hiking on Santa Cruz Island yesterday. On the way home across the channel, our commercial ferry boat encountered a pod of maybe 1000's of Common Dolphins (Delphinus delphis) that extended for a mile or more. I've never seen so many dolphins at one time. Our boat reduced speed, but it was still hard to get photos with so many dolphins in the water and so many people crowding for position along the rail, and the wrong lens. This is a tight crop, not as sharp as I like, but a fabulous moment! The dolphin looks like it's walking on the water. (Santa Barbara Channel, 23 September 2006)

Busy weekend. No time in the canyon.

File.Name: 6925a_6923-045.jpg
Notes: This Island Monkey Flower (Mimulus flemingii) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family is endemic to a few of the Channel Islands across the Santa Barbara Channel. I found this little shrub still flowering at the end of summer along the trail between Prisoner's Harbor and Pelican Bay on Santa Cruz Island. It was hidden deep in the canyon in my [Previous] photo. It's remarkable that this native plant only grows wild in a few canyons of a few islands across the ocean channel 20+ miles from the mainland. Does it matter? What stories can it tell? Is it worth protecting this "plant of special concern" that grows nowhere else? (Santa Cruz Island, 23 September 2006)

File.Name: 6925b_6923-035.jpg
Notes: Looking up an unnamed canyon last weekend as we hiked the trail between Prisoner's Harbor and Pelican Bay on Santa Cruz Island. This was a Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society hike. (I'm involved.) Our trip leader and botanist Michael Benedict (in white) described this as a Pleistocene flora of plants known from the fossil record on the mainland, but now only found on the islands - island pines and oaks and ironwood and many more taxa. I've been dreaming of mammoths and sabre-tooth tigers and horses and camels in this beautiful "lost world" that is still here 20+ miles across the Santa Barbara channel! (Santa Cruz Island, 23 September 2006)

File.Name: 6925c_6923-088.jpg
Notes: Strange mixed forest of Bishop Pine (Pinus muricata) - mostly the Santa Cruz Island Pine (P. muricata forma remortata) - and the almost-tree-like Island Scrub Oak (Quercus pacifica) on Santa Cruz Island last weekend. There are related pines and oaks on the mainland, but you can't get a view of them together like this. I got this photo as we hiked the trail between Prisoner's Harbor and Pelican Bay. (Santa Cruz Island, 23 September 2006)
CommonNames: Bishop Pine; Santa Cruz Island Pine; Island Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Pinus muricata; P. muricata;  remortata; Quercus pacifica
associatedTaxa: Pinus muricata; P. muricata;  remortata; Quercus pacifica

File.Name: 6925d_6923-019.jpg
Notes:
Santa Cruz Island lies 20+ miles off the Santa Barbara coast across the channel. It's like going back in time. This is looking east as we approach Prisoner's Harbor on our trip last Saturday. So many canyons to explore! (Santa Cruz Island, 23 September 2006)

These canyons and waters are part of the Channel Islands National Park. I asked my kids at school what is the closest National Park, and only a few guessed right:
   http://www.nps.gov/chis/

File.Name: 6926a_6923-077.jpg
Notes: Pretty native succulent Dudleya in the Stonecrop (Crassulaceae) family growing with lichens on an igneous rock cliff near the beach at Tinker's Cove on Santa Cruz Island in the Santa Barbara Channel. I guess this is the island endemic "Candleholder Dudleya" (Dudleya candelabrum), but compare D. greenei. See my [Next] photo (tomorrow). My island flora book describes this as a "species of special environmental concern" and "common". That's a good combination! (Santa Cruz Island, 23 September 2006)
ScientificName: Dudleya; Crassulaceae; Dudleya candelabrum; D. greenei

File.Name: 6926b_6923-081.jpg
Notes:
Native Northern Island Hazardia (Hazardia detonsa) in the Composite (Asteraceae) plant family growing on a rock cliff at Tinker's Cove on Santa Cruz Island in the Santa Barbara Channel. This is another island endemic shrub that only grows here and on two other Channel Islands. This furry white plant look like a milkweed. Since the domestic sheep were removed from the island in the 1980s, the seacliff flora is moving into the canyons and woodlands. There has been a remarkable recovery of the native flora - in this part of the island at least. (Santa Cruz Island, 23 September 2006)

Back home on San Marcos Pass, we have Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa), a related species in the same genus:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=1027236

You have to be a botanist to see any similarity - or maybe a photographer. Looking at both photos, I see that the flowers are quite similar, though the plants look very different.
CommonNames: Northern Island Hazardia; Composite; Sawtooth Goldenbush

File.Name: 6927a_5b23-265.jpg
Notes:
This is a photo from last year that I never posted, but it should follow my [Previous] two photos from last weekend.

This is a native succulent Dudleya in the Stonecrop (Crassulaceae) family on Santa Cruz Island in the Santa Barbara Channel. I believe this is the island endemic "Candleholder Dudleya" (Dudleya candelabrum). It's growing as an epiphyte on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Canada del Puerto. It developes a thick "trunk" shrouded by old leaves, and it sends out runners like a Spider Plant to colonize new territory. (Santa Cruz Island, 23 November 2005)

In my [Previous] photos, I captured this plant growing on a rock cliff near the beach. Maybe there's not much difference between a rock cliff and an oak branch for this Dudleya!

Notes:
Another native succulent Dudleya in the Stonecrop (Crassulaceae) family growing with lichens on an igneous rock cliff near the beach at Tinker's Cove on Santa Cruz Island in the Santa Barbara Channel. I believe this is the island endemic "Candleholder Dudleya" (Dudleya candelabrum), but compare D. greenei. It developes a thick "trunk" shrouded by old leaves, and it seems to send out runners like a Spider Plant to colonize new territory. (Santa Cruz Island, 23 September 2006)

Compare my [Next] and [Previous] photos, and my photo at:

File.Name: 6928a_6924-130.jpg
Notes:
A pair of California Dogface (Colias eurydice aka Zerene eurydice) butterflies sharing a composite Zinnia flower in the garden. That's the female on the left, and the male on the right - with slightly different colors and patterns. The big gender difference (for us at least) is the brilliant iridescent upper side of the male's wings, but they always hide that at rest. Look close and see that both butterflies have extended probosces. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2006)

I had to look that up. One proboscis, two probosces - you know, those long sucking things!

File.Name: 6928b_6923-091.jpg
Notes:
Feather-like seed plumes of native Island Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides var. blancheae) growing on Santa Cruz Island along the trail between Prisoner's Harbor and Pelican Bay on Santa Cruz Island. The species shrub grows near home. The endemic island variety has larger leaves with more lateral veins, but I'm not sure I'd recognize it if they were growing side by side. Maybe both forms are in our mountains, but I'll never remember to measure and count lateral leaf veins! (Santa Cruz Island, 23 September 2006)

The seed plumes are pretty with the sun behind, but kind of dingy otherwise. I have many photos of the mainland species in the archive, e.g.
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=15106556
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=8014717

File.Name: 6928c_6923-093.jpg
Notes: Ripe fruit of Summer Holly (Comarostaphylis diversifolia ssp. planifolia) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family. This plant is growing on Santa Cruz Island, but it also grows in our mountains on the mainland. I think of it as uncommon because it doesn't grow on San Marcos Pass near home, but it is common along the Gaviota Peak trail. It has shredding bark and Toyon-like leaves. The berries have a beautiful jewel-like texture, but they are astringent and not very tasty. (Santa Cruz Island, 23 September 2006)

File.Name: 6929a_6924-117.jpg
Notes: American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working a Zinnia flower in the garden with the sun behind. Life is good. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2006)

File.Name: 6929b_6924-113.jpg
Notes: Bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a Zinnia flower in the garden with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2006)

File.Name: 6929c_6924-121.jpg
Notes: Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working a Composite flower in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in the garden - maybe a horticultural Sneezeweed (Helenium). Last year, these little butterflies were common, but this year they are scarce. These population swings are interesting. I would never notice if I didn't post these photos (almost) every day! (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2006)

File.Name: 6930a_6924-128.jpg
Notes:
Little Short-Horned Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Acrididae) munching a Zinnia flower in the garden. The grasshopper's maxillary and labial palps on its mouth are like little hands designed to shovel in food. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2006)

*Sigh* another busy weekend. Our local Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society has a native plant sale, and I've been working since dawn. Tomorrow is day 2 of the plant sale, but I hope to get away early to get a few photos. Maybe I didn't miss much. It was cool and cloudy today, with a chance of rain all week. The seasons really are turning!

File.Name: 6930b_6924-123.jpg
Notes:
Another photo of the little Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working a Composite flower in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in the garden - maybe a horticultural Sneezeweed (Helenium). (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2006)

Compare my photo of this same butterfly posted yesterday that really does look gray:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=16069239

The same butterfly in today's photo doesn't look gray at all in the natural light tent of this yellow flower. I'm sure the "real color" of the butterfly (or anything else) is an illusion - it depends on the time of day, the season of the year, the location of trees and flower petals, clouds and haze, much more. Our eyes are smart - "all sight is insight" - so we don't usually notice the color changes between indoors and outdoors, or a butterfly inside and outside the flower. Every photo is different - unless you shoot flash or color correct and take the life from it. I like the differences.
Locality: or anything else) is an illusion - it depends on the time of day

File.Name: 6a01a_6a01-024.jpg
Notes:
Raindrops on the flowers, and raindrops on my lens... it's a natural softening filter. These are new flowers of the native shrub Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Honeysuckle (Caprifoliaceae) family growing near the river. This is a real shrub, not a vine like California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula), though it does clamber through other plants when it can. It mostly flowers in early summer, but here it is during our first autumn rain. (Santa Ynez Valley, 1 October 2006)

I took today's photos in the rain near Sage Hill along the river in Paradise with a plastic bag to cover my camera. This is our first real rain since May, and I hope it's an omen for a wet year to come. This is a sea change in our seasons. Few insects today, but I did find spiders and a mushroom - more photos tomorrow!
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae; Lonicera hispidula
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae; Lonicera hispidula

File.Name: 6a01b_6a01-011.jpg
Notes: I saw a few other insects today, but this is the only one I captured in a photo during our first real rain since May. It's a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) on flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub by the river. It wasn't moving much in the rain. Note the raindrops on its wings! (Santa Ynez Valley, 1 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a01c_6a01-012.jpg
Notes: The seasons really are changing... autumn leaves on a native Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) tree by the river. Even better, I took this photo in the rain today - our first real rain since May! (Santa Ynez Valley, 1 October 2006)

Notes: Bristly Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) tending her nest on a stalk of composite Hairy Golden Aster (Heterotheca sessiliflora ssp. echoides) on the dry wash along the river. Look close to see some of her baby spiderlings! I got this photo in the rain yesterday with a plastic bag over my camera and lens. Not recommended, but this was my only chance for photos this weekend, and it's a natural "rain filter" to soften the image, like it or not. Compare my [Previous] photo of a different not-so-green Lynx Spider. I got both of today's spider photos near the Sage Hill campground in Paradise. (Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a02b_6a01-017.jpg
Notes: This is obviously not a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens) like my [Next] photo, but it must be some kind of kindred not-so-green Lynx Spider (family Oxyopidae). It has the same shape and the same habit, tending its nest of new spiderlings. Look close to see the babies! This spider has her nest on a stalk of Hairy Golden Aster (Heterotheca sessiliflora ssp. echoides), one of our many fall-flowering composite shrubs on the dry wash along the river. I got this photo in the rain yesterday - our first rain since May in our summer-dry climate. The seasons are turning. (Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2006)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Lynx Spider; Hairy Golden Aster
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Heterotheca sessiliflora; echoides
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Heterotheca sessiliflora; echoides

File.Name: 6a03a_6a01-029.jpg
Notes: This is not a big gnarly spider like my [Previous] photos today, but a tiny adult Whitefly (family Aleyrodidae in the order Homoptera) insect resting on a ray flower of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. It looks like a bit of ash or fluff in the air. I don't usually even try to photograph anything this small, but this whtefly landed on the flower right in front of me, so I couldn't resist. It's only about 2 mm long. The larvae can be garden pests. I love the little black eye - a tiny candlelight of intelligence in its vast insensate world. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a03b_6a03-032.jpg
Notes:
My kids at school noticed this large nickle-sized spider at school resting on the bottom of a blue recycling container. We pushed the container into the sun on the grass for a photo. I guess it's a Wolf Spider (family Lycosidae). It's hanging on the plastic like a rock climber on a granite mountain wall! (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 October 2006)

My DMS photo kids got some photos too:
   http://www.fotolog.com/dms/?pid=17341772

File.Name: 6a03c_6929-266.jpg
Notes:
Bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) in the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub by our mailbox. I'm sure the spider is genus Argiope - maybe a Banded Argiope (Argiope trifasciata)? These large spiders hang head down and belly up on their webs, so I guess it's not surprising that their undersides have the more complicated patterns to scare away preditors. I see faces here - lots of faces! (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2006)

I have more photos of this same spider at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=15676205
Click [Next] and [Previous] from there to see the upper side and a male.
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; California Buckwheat; Banded Argiope
ScientificName: Araneidae) in the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum; Argiope; Argiope trifasciata
associatedTaxa: Araneidae) in the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum; Argiope; Argiope trifasciata

File.Name: 6a04a_6a01-020.jpg
Notes: Colorful foliose and fruticose Lichens growing on a branch of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near the river. I got this photo in the rain last Sunday - it brings out the colors. (Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a05a_6a05-137.jpg
Notes: The sun sinks early after the equinox here on the north side of the mountains. I caught these ripe red berries of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Honeysuckle (Caprifoliaceae) family catching the last light in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a06a_6a06-142.jpg
Notes: Looking close at a Lynx Spider (family Oxyopidae) guarding her egg sack in the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub by our mailbox this morning. Insects have three body parts - head, thorax, and abdomen. Spiders only have two body parts - a "cephalothorax" that combines mouth and legs, and a separate abdomen. This spider seems to have another little furry "control center" of eyes on the front of its cephalothorax. It reminds me of the "transformer" toys my kids used to play with a decade ago! See my [Previous] photo for another view of the same spider. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a06b_6a06-141.jpg
Notes:
Some kind of not-green Lynx Spider (family Oxyopidae) guarding her egg sack in the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub by our mailbox this morning. What remarkable spines ("macrosetae") and that furry little "control center" of eyes on the fron of its "head" aka cephalothorax. See my [Next] photo for a closer look if you dare! (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2006)

I looked at this photo again after uploading. All those spines on the legs are so confusing. Maybe that's the point, to confuse preditors?

File.Name: 6a06c_6a05-127.jpg
Notes: Like a living mousetrap - this little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is wound up and ready to strike on a male "staminate" flower of composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the canyon. This late-season native shrub is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants that attract different insects. The spider is totally focused on this single flower! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a07a_6a07-044.jpg
Notes: Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens - family Coccinellidae in the order Coleoptera) converging on a native composite Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) plant near the creek. The weight of the beetles is bringing the plant down! I'm sure it's the place, not the plant. The "Ladybugs" return year after year, every winter, but this is the largest convergence I've seen in our canyon. I hope it's a sign for a wet "El Nino" winter to come! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2006)
ScientificName: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera) converging on a native composite Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana
associatedTaxa: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera) converging on a native composite Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana

File.Name: 6a07b_6a07-032.jpg
Notes: Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens, order Coleoptera) converging on native plants of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) near the creek in the canyon - like candysticks! These pretty beetles are converging on other plants as well. I'm sure it's the place, not the plant. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2006)
ScientificName: Hippodamia convergens; Coleoptera) converging on native plants of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana
associatedTaxa: Hippodamia convergens; Coleoptera) converging on native plants of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana

File.Name: 6a07c_6a07-053.jpg
Notes:
Little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) with its butterfly prey on a ray flower of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. The unfortunate butterfly is a Cabbage White (Pieris rapae or maybe P. napi). Butterflies are getting scarce in the canyon as the seasons change. Here's why. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2006)

   3:2 A time to be born, and a time to die; a time to plant, and a time to pluck up that which is planted;
   3:3 A time to kill, and a time to heal; a time to break down, and a time to build up;
   3:4 A time to weep, and a time to laugh; a time to mourn, and a time to dance;
   3:5 A time to cast away stones, and a time to gather stones together; a time to embrace, and a time to refrain from embracing;
   3:6 A time to get, and a time to lose; a time to keep, and a time to cast away;

   3:8 A time to love, and a time to hate; a time of war, and a time of peace.

The unfortunate butterfly is a Cabbage White (Pieris rapae or maybe P. napi). Butterflies are getting scarce in the canyon as the seasons change. Here's why. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2006)
