File.Name: 6a07e_6a07-003.jpg
Notes:
Another landmark - these new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family are the first flowers of the new rain year rather than the last of the old. We had our first early rain last weekend. Our Mediterranean climate is summer-dry and winter-wet, so it's exciting when we get the first real rain after months of summer drought. The seasons really are changing! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2006)

Last year I posted my first Ribes malvaceum photo on October 4. I guess these flowers are right on track.
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=11157210

File.Name: 6a07f_6a07-047.jpg
Notes:
This is a landmark - our first mushroom of the year! It came up with our first early rain of the year last weekend, but there was better light today. Our Mediterranean climate is summer-dry and winter-wet, so it's exciting when get the first real rain after months of summer drought. This mushroom is some kind of Russula growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2006)

I have to remember how to photograph mushrooms. Insects are fast, but mushrooms are slow. I got this photo at 1/4 sec, f/22, ISO 400.

File.Name: 6a08a_6a08-059.jpg
Notes:
Flirting Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies. I guess that's the female working the composite flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) as the male flutters by in the air again and again. She flutters too - see my [Next] photo tomorrow! (Santa Ynez River, 8 October 2006)

I got most of today's photos near the Sage Hill campground in Paradise on the dry wash along the river.

File.Name: 6a08b_6a08-067.jpg
Notes: Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - I guess Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti - working flowers of a native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub by the river today. (Santa Ynez River, 8 October 2006)
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 6a08c_6a08-071.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working composite flowers of a Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub by the river today. It looks so punk! (Santa Ynez River, 8 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a08d_6a08-070.jpg
Notes: Beautiful Wasp (order Hymenoptera - family Vespidae?) working native composite flowers on this Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub by the river today. (Santa Ynez River, 8 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a08e_6a08-057.jpg
Notes:
Handsome Assassin Bug preditor in the Reduviidae family in the order Hemiptera perched on a flower head of a native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub by the river. Note the sharp proboscis folded under its head. (Santa Ynez River, 8 October 2006)

I was lucky to spot this critter before it flew away. I know nothing about how this insect lives from egg to adult. Amateur naturalists - even kids - can advance our knowledge by recording what they see!

File.Name: 6a08f_6a07-042.jpg
Notes: Lots of ladybugs in the canyon! These are Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens - family Coccinellidae in the order Coleoptera) converging in the canyon by the creek. Don't try counting spots - they go from none to many, but they all seem to have those two white lines on their carapice. They're not feeding on the plants - I'm sure it's the place not the plants that attracts them. Maybe reproduction is happening, but their coupling is so fast that I don't think it's for real. I reckon it's a ladybug rave! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a09a_6a08-080.jpg
Notes:
Hi, how's it going, wanna buy a used car?

Sorry, I couldn't resist, but this little Short-Horned Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Acrididae) on the dry river wash yesterday needs a caption! (Santa Ynez River, 8 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a09b_6a08-062.jpg
Notes:
Flirting Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies on composite flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash by the river. I guess that's the female working the flowers - she flutters whenever the male dives in close like this. I had no time to adjust my camera settings - this is 1/125 sec @ f/16 @ ISO 200. I love the combination of blur and (almost) focus! See my [Previous] two photos. (Santa Ynez River, 8 October 2006)

Now that I think of it, I'm not sure if these butterflies are flirting or fighting - maybe they're not sure either. As humans, I guess we all know something about that!

File.Name: 6a09c_6a08-064.jpg
Notes: Flirting fluttering Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies on composite flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash by the river. I guess that's the female working the flowers and the male attending. I had no time to adjust camera settings - this is 1/125 sec @ f/16 @ ISO 200 - I love the blur! See my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (Santa Ynez River, 8 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a10a_6a07-008.jpg
Notes: Little Western Spotted Cucumber Beetle (Diabrotica undecimpunctata, order Coleoptera) hanging under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf in the woods with the sun behind. It's autumn, and light is everything as the sun gets low! These pretty beetles are said to be a garden pest, but not in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2006)
CommonNames: Western Spotted Cucumber Beetle; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Diabrotica undecimpunctata; Coleoptera) hanging under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Diabrotica undecimpunctata; Coleoptera) hanging under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 6a10b_6a07-007.jpg
Notes: It's autumn, and light is everything as the sun gets low. This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is working one of the last perfect composite flowers of the Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) on the edge of our garden, with the sun behind. This native shrub in the in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family has flowers all summer long if it gets some water. Gotta be careful - summer watering kills many of our summer-dry natives. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a10c_6a08-069.jpg
Notes: Fat little Beetle (order Coleoptera) snuggling into new flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. It's munching the petals and pollen. I have golden memories of times like that when everything is right! (Santa Ynez River, 8 October 2006)
ScientificName: Coleoptera) snuggling into new flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera) snuggling into new flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 6a10d_6a08-083.jpg
Notes: I thought this Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly was feeding on the male composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) flowers. I took a few photos from a distance so I wouldn't disturb it. Then I moved in closer and saw what was really happening. Look close, left of the butterfly's head. Do you see the little Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) with it's prey? Spiders aren't the only autumn preditors. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2006)
CommonNames: Common Ringlet; Coyote Bush; Ambush Bug
ScientificName: Coenonympha tullia; Baccharis pilularis; Phymatidae; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Coenonympha tullia; Baccharis pilularis; Phymatidae; Hemiptera

File.Name: 6a11a_6a08-088.jpg
Notes: New flowers of Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family starting to flower in the mountains. We're past the autumnal equinox, but there are still late-season flowers starting to bloom in our summer-dry mountains. The sun is getting low, which makes for perfect lighting. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a12a_6a07-025.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of the native composite shrub Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family growing in our woodland canyon. I guess this is the species, but a different smaller variety grows on sandstone outcrops in the mountains. They both have these distinctive overlapping recurved phyllaries below the flowers, like little cones. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2006)

We're on the cusp between our summer-dry and winter-wet seasons. I saw lightning tonight, and there's a forcast for rain tomorrow! I'm collecting photos of our interesting late dry-season flowers for a my school science fair project next spring.

File.Name: 6a12b_6a08-078.jpg
Notes: I think these are new flowers of the native composite shrub Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) growing on the summer-dry flood plain of the river. This is a shrub that grows head-high or more. The most conspicuous native flowers at the end of our dry summer are all yellow composites, many confusing different kinds of them. Why are they yellow? (Santa Ynez River, 8 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a13a_6a13-183.jpg
Notes: Rain in the mountains - with thunder and lightning! This is the view at the upper end of Lake Cachuma on my way home from school this afternoon. After our long summer dry season, these first winter rains are exhilarating! (Santa Ynez Valley, 13 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a14a_6a14-018.jpg
Notes: Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae in the order Hymenoptera) tenderly holding a female flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family in the canyon. The sharp bristles on her forelegs are a sign that she's not always so tender! These wasps prey on the spiders that are preying on the pollinator insects that are working the last flowers as summer turns to winter. Who gets the Spider Wasp? Probably some tiny internal parasite I can't even see. Nature is like that. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a14b_6a14-003.jpg
Notes:
I didn't realize this is a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly when I took the photo on a Zinnia flower in the garden this morning. I thought it was just another American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly.

Compare http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=16069334

But the color patterns are different. This is the first Painted Lady I've recognized this year. They stream north from Mexico in the spring - sometimes in mass migrations like two years ago. Maybe this veteran is heading home after traveling abroad? (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2006)
CommonNames: Painted Lady; Zinnia; American Lady; Painted Lady

File.Name: 6a14c_6a14-008.jpg
Notes: Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working a Zinnia flower in the garden this morning. I almost missed the photo, mistaking it for another American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly. When I Looked at the image on my computer screen, I realized it has four eyespots below rather than two. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a14d_6a14-010.jpg
Notes:
Male Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus sp.) folded head-down and butts-up on the cone of another Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden this morning. I show a female cricket in the same pose in my [Previous] photo. These crickets are nocturnal, and they "hide" like this during the day.

The males have broad wings and a narrow body while the famales have narrow wings and a wider body. The males make all the noise and "sing" - really they stridulate - by rubbing their wings together. It's a continuous pulsating shrill trill that is disturbingly loud in the house. They are called "thermometer crickets" because the modulated frequency is dependent on the temperature. The folklore is to count the chirps in 15 seconds and add 40 for degrees Fahrenheit. It's been cold with a bit of rain this week. I haven't heard them at all. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2006)
CommonNames: Snowy Tree Cricket; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 6a14e_6a14-004.jpg
Notes: Female Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus sp.) folded butts-up on the cone of a Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden this morning. These crickets are nocturnal, and they "hide' like this during the day. For some reason, I find them every day on the daisy flowers. The females have narrow wings and a cylindrical body. See my [Next] photo for the male. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2006)
CommonNames: Snowy Tree Cricket; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 6a14f_6a14-013.jpg
Notes: Our late-season flowers are not all yellow, but other colors are the exceptions. These brilliant red California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) flowers in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family are still open for the humingbirds. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a15a_6a15-028.jpg
Notes: Tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the flower cone of a Zinnia flower in the garden. Looks like something I'd see snorkeling at the Channel Islands! This spider is smaller than a pencil erasor - more like a match head. After I noticed this tiny spider, I noticed more even smaller ones. Our weather has turned cold, and insects are scarce. I wonder what prey these late spiders will find? (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a15b_6a14-009.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a Zinnia flower in the garden. Pollinator insects are scarce in the garden as the weather turns cold and wet, but the Honey Bees are still active. These bees are not native. Maybe this is part of their advantage? (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a15c_6a14-022.jpg
Notes: Little Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) resting in the garden. I took a dozen photos to get this one with open eyes. It's looking for the critters in the rest of my photos today! (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a15e_6a08-084.jpg
Notes: Little Short-Horned Grasshopper (order Orthoptera, family Acrididae) hiding in the late-season male "staminate" flowers of a composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a15f_6a14-025.jpg
Notes: Translucent Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata, order Orthoptera, family Tettigoniidae) in the garden. This katydid is missing something important - I bet it goes left when it jumps! (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a16a_6a08-098.jpg
Notes: Perfect fat acorn on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the canyon. Acorns are the bottom of the food web for many critters in the woods, but they are fickle with good years and bad years. This year looks pretty good for wildlife and future photos! (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a16b_6a14-020.jpg
Notes: New acorns on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. These trees are the base of the food web for many critters in the woods, but they are fickle with good years and bad years. This year looks pretty good so far, though many trees in the canyon dropped their acorns early. I hope there's enough to go around! (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a16c_6a08-089.jpg
Notes: Autumn is harvest time in our mountains. Here are ripening fruit of California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) in the canyon. They are related to avocado and cinnamon in the Laurel (Lauraceae) plant family. The fruit are REALLY intense, like chewing on the bay leaf in the stew, only more so. The large nuts inside were an important food for native Americans after leaching with water, and the squirrels love them. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a17a_6a17-041.jpg
Notes: A handful of spider! This palm-size California Tarantula (Aphonopelma reversum or kin) spider feels like a walking feather. They have fangs and can hurt, but we're not afraid, and they are not threatening. Thanks Kevin and Michael for sharing at school today! I've never found these spiders in the mountains on the pass. (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a17b_6a17-034.jpg
Notes: The Traveling Man! This California Tarantula (Aphonopelma reversum or kin) spider didn't want to pose for a photo. It's our largest native spider with a palm-size leg spread - see my [Next] photo. I believe this traveling spider is a male looking for a mate. The ladies seem to all be on the other side of the highway! I've been seeing these spiders slowly crossing the road almost every day. "I brake for tarantulas!" What are their odds on the busy highway? (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a18a_6a08-073.jpg
Notes: We have many kinds of Buckwheat (genus Eriogonum in the Polygonaceae family) in our mountains. I believe these are flowers of a Longstem Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum). It's easy to walk right past hese little flowers, but they are beautiful up close. (Santa Ynez River, 8 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a18b_6a08-099.jpg
Notes: I believe this little wildflower is Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. Curious plant. Most of its relaties grow in swampy places, but this late-summer flowers one likes to grow along dry trails and roads in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a19a_6a06-147.jpg
Notes: Sleepy little lady Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) resting in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near the porch as the sun gets low. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2006) I'll be gone for a while too. I'm heading to Oregon tomorrow for a long weekend to visit my Dad and fix the broken water line. I'll find time for a few photos. Gotta pack!

File.Name: 6a24a_6a22-054.jpg
Notes:
Time and the river flowing...

Brilliant Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) leaves turning yellow as winter approaches in the Oregon woods, with the river beyond. The leaves will soon fall - the measure of time - but the river will keep flowing. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 October 2006)

I went to Oregon last weekend to help my Dad fix his broken water line. Problem solved. We had some fishing time together with perfect weather and fall colors. I found time for a few photos. Now I'm stumbling to get caught up with school and other commitments back home!

File.Name: 6a24b_6a22-068.jpg
Notes: Brilliant red autumn leaves of native Vine Maple (Acer circinatum) in the Oregon woods last weekend. The red autumn leaves contrast so nice with the yellow Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in my [Next] photo. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a24c_6a22-063.jpg
Notes: Looking up into a tree of Pacific Dogwood (Cornus nuttallii) with the sun above. They show characteristic "dogwood veins" converging parallel to the leaf edge. The leaves are starting to develop red autumn colors as the seasons change. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a25a_6a21-003.jpg
Notes:
The brilliant Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) autumn leaves in my [Previous] photos look different up close - more like a microbiology agar plate. I'm not sure what I'm seeing here, but the black spots and color separations sure are interesting. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 October 2006)

I made this my school stumper for the week:

Marc's stumper for the week: I visited my Dad in
Oregon last weekend, and the woods were at the peak
of fall color with brilliant yellow and red maples and
dogwood along the river. Our local trees are mostly
evergreen, though we do have deciduous trees that are
just starting to show autumn color before they drop
their leaves for winter. My stumper is not why some
trees drop their leaves and others don't. That's just one of
nature's many successful strategies. But why do some
deciduous trees make a show of their autumn leaves?
The leaves are dying. Why are they are so beautiful?

File.Name: 6a25b_6a22-047.jpg
Notes:
Maybe this tree is the real Treebeard patiently waiting for the entwives in the Oregon woods...

This is a deciduous Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) with golden autumn leaves looking over the North Umpqua River. The branches are covered with a hatch of mosses and lichens that grow during the wet winter when the banches are bare after the leaves fall. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a26a_4725-033.jpg
Notes: Our dog Mojo in the garden a couple of years ago. He had a yarrow bed that was his favorite place to rest after our hikes. Mojo was my friend and the best trail dog. After 14+ years, Mojo is at rest. We had a good visit together this afternoon after school. Then the mobile vet arrived to put him down. Fast and easy. Mojo died in our arms. I couldn't stop the tears, but that was love not pain. A procedure like this - at home, with loved ones - sure would alleviate a lot of human suffering. Happy trails, good Mojo.

File.Name: 6a27a_6a27-140.jpg
Notes: We had a dozen of these pretty Underwing Moths (genus Catocala of the family Noctuidae in order Lepidoptera) on the screen door tonight. They usually fold up their wings with cryptic colors - see my [Previous] photo. I poked this one and it opened up - reminds me of anime ninja-samurai armor! I used flash for this almost-night photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a27b_6a27-141.jpg
Notes: Underwing Moth (genus Catocala of the family Noctuidae in order Lepidoptera) on the screen door tonight in camouflage mode. It should be resting on oak bark. I poked it and it opened up to show its stuff. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a27c_6a27-121.jpg
Notes: Little Western Pygmy-Blue (Brephidium exile) butterfly working late-season male "staminate" flowers of the composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the field at school. This tiny butterfly is no bigger than my little fingernail - it's the smallest butterfly in North America. Small, but pretty - with iridescent highlights on the hindwing eyespots. (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a27d_6a20-079.jpg
Notes: California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly in the Oregon woods last weekend. I chased this pretty butterfly for a while and gave up. Then I spotted it resting on a dead branch right in front of me. I had time for just one shot and got it. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a27e_6a20-084.jpg
Notes: Some kind of Crescent (genus Phycodes) butterfly visiting a composite flower in a clearing in the Oregon woods last weekend. It doesn't quite match any of the photos in my books. I only saw this butterfly and the [Next] and only had one chance for a photo with each. Lucky shots! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a28a_6a28-025.jpg
Notes: Pretty lady - an American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working a Zinnia flower in the garden today with the sun behind. Life is good. Butterflies - and all insects - are getting scarce as we move into winter here in the Santa Barbara mountains. That makes every last butterfly look even more beautiful - like this. Note the pink glow on its underside reflected from the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a28b_6a28-007.jpg
Notes: Butterflies - and all insects - are getting scarce as we move into winter, so all of our late-season butterflies are interesting. This Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly - I love the name - is working late-season flowers of the native composite shrub Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family growing in our woodland canyon. I recognized this butterfly by the shape of its wings. Author Vladimir Nabokov made his mark studying tropical metalmark butterflies as well as writing books like Lolita and Pale Fire. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a28c_6a28-003.jpg
Notes: Little Grass-Skipper (order Lepidoptera - subfamily Hesperiinae - genus Hesperia) - butterfly working a new composite flower of native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family growing in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a28d_6a28-033.jpg
Notes: Pale green Moth (order Lepidoptera) resting on a golden autumn apple leaf with the sun behind. That's camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a28e_6a28-016.jpg
Notes:
It takes a moment to understand this photo - look close. This is a lady not-green Lynx Spider (family Oxyopidae) guarding her egg sack in the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub by our mailbox. All those legs and spines make great cryptic camouflage. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2006)

I posted a photo of this same spider guarding on her egg sack on October 6:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=16213090
I hope all is well. I guess that yellow ball under her second leg on the left is a predator egg waiting for the hatch. I'm waiting too. I want photos of the baby spiderlings!

File.Name: 6a28f_6a28-034.jpg
Notes: Bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) with its Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) prey wrapped in a glowing shroud with the sun behind. It's no mystery why insects are getting scarce in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a29a_6a29-046.jpg
Notes: It's apple picking time, and the house smells like apple sauce today! Like Johnny Appleseed - aka John Chapman - we started this tree from a seed, maybe be a spit seed or a tossed core, when we built our house 25 years ago. The fruit are beautiful and tasty when the birds let us harvest a few. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a29b_6a28-043.jpg
Notes: Coin-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) on the porch at home. We have to be careful walking out at night! You know that feeling when you walk into a web - where's the spider! These big spiders and their silk webs are so contrasty in the sun - it's hard to show both in one photo. I prefer backlight, but I shot this photo with the sun behind me. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a29c_6a28-014.jpg
Notes: A different Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) in the woods. These large coin-size spiders are everywhere in the woods right now picking off the summer pollinator insects. Spiders and their webs are so contrasty - it's hard to get both in one photo. This works. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a29d_6a28-037.jpg
Notes: Bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) in the apple tree with its Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) prey wrapped and ready for an egg to carry the seed into another year. It's temarkable that these spiders and many insects bet everything on their eggs to carry one next year after winter. Mother Nature's seed is so precious and precarious every year. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a29e_6a28-029.jpg
Notes: Bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) in the apple tree with its Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) prey. I reckon that bee with tongue extended is saying "blah" - substitute your expletive of choice - but this is their fate as we approach winter in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a29f_6a27-125.jpg
Notes: Ripe seedheads of female composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) shrub in the field at school. The baby dandelion-like seeds are leaving home. (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a30a_6a21-025.jpg
Notes:
Ping Pong Gall on a leaf of deciduous Oregon White Oak (Quercus garryana) in the Oregon woods last week. Oaks make acorns, so these galls are somthing else. These galls are maybe made by the tiny Gall Wasp Cynips maculipennis, family Cynipidae in the order Hymenoptera. Galls are fascinating tumor-like plant growths caused by specialized insects and other organisms who manipulate the DNA of their host to provide for their young housed inside. These galls are drying out because they fall with the autumn leaves. At some time, new wasps will emerge to do it all again and produce a new generation. There's so much to learn in nature! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 October 2006)

Interesting trivia: Alfred Kinsey did his PhD work on gall wasps, and in 1930 published a monograph entitled "The Gall Wasp Genus Cynips: A Study in the Origin of Species." Kinsey went on to study human sexual behavior. He wrote the notorious "Kinsey Reports" on Sexual Behavior in the Human Male (1948) and Female (1953) that still make people nervous!

It all started with wasp galls! For more info:
   http://www.pbs.org/wgbh/amex/kinsey/filmmore/index.html
   http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alfred_C._Kinsey
   http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kinsey_Reports
ScientificName: Quercus garryana; Cynips maculipennis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus garryana; Cynips maculipennis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

I'm not sure of the ID, but maybe it's made by the Gall Wasp Cynips maculipennis, family Cynipidae in the order Hymenoptera. Oaks make acorns, so this strange but pretty thing in the woods must be something else. See my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a30c_6a21-028.jpg
Notes: This looks like a mushroom growing on an oak leaf! It's a wasp gall. Maybe It's a "Pimg Pong" gall from the Gall Wasp Cynips maculipennis, family Cynipidae in the order Hymenoptera. Oaks make acorns, but this pretty gall is growing on a leaf of Oregon White Oak (Quercus garryana) in the Oregon woods. I'm not sure of the ID. I show more galls on the same tree in my [next] two photos, but this gall is above the leaf rather than below, and succulent rather than dry. Galls are fascinating tumor-like plant growths caused by specialized insects and other organisms who manipulate the DNA of their host to provide for their young. This is advanced bio-engineering! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a31a_6a20-092.jpg
Notes:
I guess this big spider is scary enough for Halloween night!

But it's also a love story. This is a bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) in the Oregon woods last week. It's plenty scary if you walk into her web at night - where's the spider?! One end of her web attaches to a native grass plant in the woods, and I think I photographed a much smaller male spider of the same kind making advances by plucking her web to send the right vibrations for loving. See my [Previous] two photos. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a31b_6a20-094.jpg
Notes: This is the same little pencil eraser-size spider on a native grass (maybe genus Leymus?) that I show in my [Previous] photo in the Oregon woods last week. I believe it's a male on the prowl! Look how it's carefully plucking the web strands that lead to the MUCH LARGER female Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) in my [Next] photo! I think they are a potential couple, though they look very different. The little male has to send just the right vibes over the web to calm and excite the female. Mating is hard for these preditors. It's usually hard for us humans too! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a31c_6a20-093.jpg
Notes: I noticed this little pencil eraser-size spider on a native grass (maybe genus Leymus?) in the Oregon woods last week. It has swollen spermaphores on the palps on his head. I believe this is a little male spider on the prowl! See my [Next] photo. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 October 2006)

File.Name: 6a31d_6a23-080.jpg
Notes: Spider web in the Oregon woods last week - surely a large Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae). Autumn is the spiders' time. It's spooky walking into a web in the dark woods - like Bilbo in Mirkwood - "Where's the spider?!" (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 October 2006)

File.Name: 6b01a_6a20-086.jpg
Notes: Tiny mushrooms growing from a fallen cone of Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) in the Oregon woods last week. I believe these pretty little mushrooms are Baeospora myosura, but Strobilurus trullisatus is similar. Both mushrooms live on conifer cones. A single fallen cone can be the start of much new life - maybe even a new tree! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 October 2006)
ScientificName: Pseudotsuga menziesii; Baeospora myosura; Strobilurus trullisatus
associatedTaxa: Pseudotsuga menziesii; Baeospora myosura; Strobilurus trullisatus

File.Name: 6b02a_6b02-238.jpg
Notes: My after-school Hike Club kids at "The Castle" on the stone ruins of this mountain mansion that was destroyed by wildfire in the 1930s. Only stone walls and chimneys and perfect views remain. This is looking into the afternoon sun across San Marcos Pass. I get most of these photos between here and there. *Sigh* Life is good. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b02b_6b02-253.jpg
Notes: I took my Hike Club kids to "The Castle" after school today. It's fun to climb on the ruin of this mountain mansion near San Marcos Pass that was destroyed by wildfire in the 1930s. Only stone walls and chimneys and perfect views remain. This is looking into the backcountry across the Santa Ynez River - with Little Pine Mountain and glimpses of Mission Pine Basin and Big Pine Mountain beyond. Much of the backcountry is protected in the San Rafael and Dick Smith Wilderness areas. It's an inspiring view. So many canyons to explore! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b03a_6a28-018.jpg
Notes:
Today's photos show the first and last flowers as our climate tips from dry summer to wet winter.

I reckon these delicate native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) flowers in the Grossulariaceae family are the first flowers of the new rain year rather than the last of the old like my [Previous] photo. We have a summer-dry and winter-wet Mediterranean climate that is harsh for wildlife, but the native flowers close the circle so there is always something flowering in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2006)

Autumn is the hard time for wildlife - and photos! - as our weather swings between early rains and hot dry Santa Ana winds. We've had sprinkles, but I'm still waiting for a real rain to settle the dust and wake the seeds and bring up the mushrooms to start a new year in the woods.

File.Name: 6b03b_6a28-011.jpg
Notes:
Today's photos show first and last flowers as our climate tips from dry summer to wet winter.

The brilliant red California Fuchsia (Epilobium canum) flowers are still flowering in the canyon for the hummingbirds as winter approaches. They're also making dandelion-like seeds. Our garden fuchsias still have a few flowers too. The fuchsias share a shape and a common name, and both are in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) plant family. Otherwise they are very different. The garden plants have berries and the natives have dry seeds that blow in the wind like composite (Asteraceae) seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2006)

File.Name: 6b04a_6b04-042.jpg
Notes: Pretty Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa in the Asclepiadaceae family) milkweed flowers in the garden with the sun behind. Look at the bottom of the photo to see the golden Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) that are working the plant like cattle on the hills. This is a garden milkweed that is not native here. I've also seen these golden aphids on native Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis). I wonder how the aphids survive from one year to the next? (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2006)
CommonNames: Butterfly Weed; Milkweed Aphids; Narrow-leaved Milkweed
ScientificName: Asclepias tuberosa; Asclepiadaceae; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias fascicularis

File.Name: 6b04b_6b04-040.jpg
Notes: Golden Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on a seed pod of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. Look closer to see the smaller-than-aphid preditor in the top-center of the photo. I'm sure it's a fly (order Diptera) about to lay eggs that will hatch into larvae to consume the vulnerable aphids. Nature is like that. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2006)
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa; Diptera

File.Name: 6b04c_6b04-028.jpg
Notes:
Yellow Jacket Wasp (Vespula pensylvanica or kin) trapped in a Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed flower in the garden. I had to study this photo to understang what I saw. I think the wasp's left front leg is trapped in the flower, and it's missing both front and middle legs on its right - photo front - side. These nasty wasps have a nest in the ground by the door. We've learned the hard way to walk around! (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2006)

Milkweed flowers are traps for pollinators. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=8108247

File.Name: 6b04d_6b04-046.jpg
Notes: Little Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus sp. in order Orthoptera, family Gryllidae) trying to hide head-down on a Salvia in the garden. These are "thermometer crickets" because the modulated frequency is dependent on the temperature. The folklore is to count the chirps in 15 seconds and add 40 for degrees Fahrenheit. Nights have been cold, but I here them tonight. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b04e_6b04-008.jpg
Notes: Little nocturnal Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus sp. in order Orthoptera, family Gryllidae) hiding head-down and butts-up in leaves of scruib Canyon Live Oak aka Goldencup Oak (Quercus chrysolepis). You can see some of the rusty hairs under the leaves that give this oak its name. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2006)
CommonNames: Snowy Tree Cricket; Canyon Live Oak; Goldencup Oak

File.Name: 6b04f_6a21-010.jpg
Notes: Little nocturnal Cricket (order Orthoptera, family Gryllidae) trying to hide during the day on a leaf of deciduous Oregon White Oak (Quercus garryana) in the Oregon woods. Compare my [Next] two photos. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 October 2006)

File.Name: 6b05a_6b05-054.jpg
Notes:
Male Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) perched in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our porch. He's not resting on the spider web! The brilliant red feathers on its head and neck are dull in my [Previous] photo. This color is not a pigment, but iridescent - like the rainbows on a CD or spilled gasoline. A slight turn of the head sometimes makes all the difference between dull and brilliant! (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2006)

Today was a perfect "Indian Summer" autumn day with no wind. *Sigh* My Dad visited and I had school work to catch up on. At least I got to work outside on the porch, and I managed to capture these birds between lab books. A cricket is singing in my room right now a few feet away from my computer - almost painfully loud. Life is good!

File.Name: 6b05b_6b05-052.jpg
Notes: Male Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) perched in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our porch. It's not resting on the spider web! The feathers on its head and neck are dull in this photo, but brilliant in my [Next] photo. The color is not a pigment, but iridescent - like the rainbows on a CD or spilled gasoline. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b05c_6b04-001.jpg
Notes: The tiny Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula) birds - not much bigger than hummingbirds - are back in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The seasons really have changed! We are "South" for these winter-visitor birds, and they disappear for the summer. The "incomplete" white eye-ring is distinctive. They have this distinctive way of fluttering in front of the oak leaves looking for insect prey. They never stand still! (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b06a_6b04-043.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the garden. Hummingbirds fight over these flowers. With long bill and tongue, the hummers go in through the throat of the flower to reach the sweet nectar in the back. But the bees know a shortcut. Instead of going in through the throat of the flower - and helping pollination happen by moving pollen from anther to stigma - this bee is working its proboscis "under the skirts" of the flower behind the petals and into the calyx to go right to the good stuff. Many insects know this trick for many flowers. I reckon this is nature not nurture. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b07a_6b04-014.jpg
Notes: A herd of golden Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) butts up on a stem of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. The aphids appear every year about this time on the milkweeds. They are so pretty, we let them be, and the plants do fine. I wonder how the aphids propagate from year to year only to appear now at the end of the growing season! (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b08a_6b08-113.jpg
Notes: This is not a spring poppy wildflower, but a late-autumn flower of the still-flowering Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) shrubs in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family growing in the chaparral near home. These shrubs are fire-followers in our mountains, but they keep flowering almost year-round for decades after the fire, so it's easy to forget the history. I'm fascinated by these late-season flowers in our summer-dry woods. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b09a_6b09-243.jpg
Notes: Shooting into the setting sun at a secret beach along the Gaviota Coast between Refugio and Gaviota. We climbed down to the beach below the railroad trestle on the right. I've been trying to "fix" the colors on my computer, but everything I do makes it worse. This is what my camera captured. (Gaviota Coast, 9 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b09b_6b09-206.jpg
Notes:
Not my usual photo, but I like it. In the forground is a steel railroad trestle. Then a concete arch bridge from the original two-lane Highway 101. The hill in the background is the new Highway 101 "bridge" - a dirt-fill embankment that fills the canyon. It's interesting to discover these bits of the old highway that are still intact. (Gaviota Coast, 9 November 2006)

I took my after-school Hike Club kids to the old bridge to test school egg-drop projects - build a structure to protect an egg from a great fall. That old bridge is a perfect place to launch eggs, and there's a pefect beach to explore in the canyon below.

File.Name: 6b10a_6917-090.jpg
Notes:
Impressive Spiny Tachina Fly (order Diptera, family Tachinidae, species Paradejeania rutiloides or kin) working flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. I believe that these bumble bee-size bristly flies lay eggs that become internal parasites of the equally bristly Tiger Moth (order Lepitoptera, family Arctiidae) "woolly bear" caterpillars. (Santa Ynez River, 8 October 2006)

No time tonight, we're stepping out to hear the Robert Cray Band! Here's a couple I found in my "Ready" folder from last month.

File.Name: 6b10b_6a20-088.jpg
Notes: New fruit of native California Spikenard (Aralia californica) in the Ginseng (Araliaceae) family starting to ripen in southern Oregon a few weeks ago. This odd perennial is common along mountain roads in Oregon, but I have to hunt for it in our canyons at home. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 October 2006)

File.Name: 6b11a_6b11-016.jpg
Notes:
Another one of those tiny birds in the woods - maybe an Empidonax flycatcher or is it a Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula)? This pretty little bird is poking out of a natural frame of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) twigs down by the creek today. No camera tricks except capturing the moment! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2006)

I've been waiting all week to get new photos, but today was a terrible photo day - cold, dark, drizzly, and windy. The woods were already moving with the wind - no chance for close shots - so I looked for birds also moving through the woods. Birds have been scarce this summer, but they're coming home to our woods for winter.

File.Name: 6b11b_6b11-017.jpg
Notes: One of those tiny birds in the woods - maybe an Empidonax flycatcher or another Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula)? This little bird is looking right at me through a natural frame of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) twigs down by the creek today. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b11c_6b11-008.jpg
Notes: Shy little Hermit Thrush (Catharus guttatus - family Turdidae) bird on the deck in the drizzle this morning. A moment later, it dropped a bird-turd and disappeared into the woods. So much for you! I like these shy birds that keep to themselves and sing the sweet song of the woods in the summer. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b11d_6b11-004.jpg
Notes: Fluffed-up little male Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) taking a break in this Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree overlooking our garden on this cold drizzly day. It was dark and wet today, but the hummers seemed more active and feisty than usual. I wonder if they really break even defending their flower patch - calories spent vs. calories gained! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b11e_6b11-024.jpg
Notes: Black Phoebe (Syornis nigricans) bird trying to hide in the native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) tree down by the creek with the sun behind. These birds usually perch high on the powerline or on our roof at home - despite what the Sibley Guide says about always perching low! They are not a common bird in our canyon, but here they are. It's fun to watch these flycatchers dart out from their perch to catch flies! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b11f_6b11-020.jpg
Notes: Black Phoebe (Syornis nigricans) bird in the native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) tree down by the creek with the sun behind. It's a Tyrant Flycatcher bird in the family Tyrannidae. It's fun to watch these flycatchers dart out to catch their prey in the air and return to their perch. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2006)
CommonNames: Black Phoebe; Arroyo Willow; Tyrant Flycatcher

File.Name: 6b12a_6b11-033.jpg
Notes: Bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) in the woods, with the sun behind. These big spiders sit exposed on their webs all day. Many of them have a "scary face" pattern on their front or back. Look close - this spider has one of the scariest "faces" ever if you look close. It's remarkable that these spiders (phylum Arthropoda) have evolved characteristics that we humans (phylum Chordata) can relate to! (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b12b_6b12-063.jpg
Notes: Little incoming Fly (order Diptera) approaching a late-season native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) flower in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family growing in the canyon. This little fly hovered at the flowers and never actually landed. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b12c_6b11-026.jpg
Notes: This American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) is the only butterfly I saw this weekend. It's resting on native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) leaves near the creek. The seasons have turned, and the butterflies seem done until next year - though I expect to be surprised by other late-season stragglers! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b12d_6b12-054.jpg
Notes: We had sprinkles yesterday, but no real rain - I still count these new Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) flowers in the Grossulariaceae family as the first native flowers of the new year rather than the last of the old year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b12e_6b12-057.jpg
Notes: A herd of golden Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on a seed pod of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. Some of these aphids will go for a ride when the seed pod opens and releases its dandelion-like seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2006)
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 6b12f_6b11-005.jpg
Notes: Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) working the late-season Sage flowers in the garden - maybe Salvia guaranitica? Note the yellow pollen on her bill that she's passing around from flower to flower. Being sharp isn't everything in a photo! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b13a_6b12-061.jpg
Notes: Bent leaves of Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) in the woods. We're losing the sun as we move into winter here on the north side of the mountains, but the low angle sunbeams and shadows provide perfect lighting. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b13b_6b04-038.jpg
Notes:
Little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) oviposting - laying eggs - on a calyx of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the garden. I think I inadvertently broke the "Hipocratic Oath for Naure Photographers" - try to get great photos, but don't mess with Mother Nature. These pretty flies lay eggs that hatch into larvae that prey on aphids and other insect pests. This fly was interested in the golden Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on the Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed nearby in the garden. (See my photo yesterday.) I got too close with my camera, and the fly moved over to the Salvia to lay eggs - where there are no aphids. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2006)

Sure we're part of nature too. Of course we interfere in big ways and small. It's our nature.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Waverly Sage; Milkweed Aphids; Butterfly Weed
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Salvia "Waverly"; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Salvia "Waverly"; Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 6b14a_6b14-080.jpg
Notes:
Little male Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) stretching its tail feathers on his favorite perch in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree overlooking our garden. We have school conferences all week, but I got to start late today. I captured this photo just as the sun was breaking through the morning drizzle to show the iridescent colors of the hummer's head feathers. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2006)

I've photographed this same hummingbird many times on the same perch, see:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=16900632
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=16788783

File.Name: 6b14b_6b11-042.jpg
Notes:
Here's a real odd couple that I found at home last weekend. Our cat brought in the little immature California Mole aka Broad-footed Mole (Scapanus latimanus) and carefully left the by the front door. I found the dead Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) in the garden. I moved them both to a sandstone rock for a photo. It's so strange to see these different critters - almost the same size - lying together. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2006)

I hope our cat "Narcissa" didn't kill the hummer. She's so fat, it hardly seems possible, and she wears a bell to give the birds warning, but she is a good hunter. We need her to help control the mice and wood rats that move in to our house!
CommonNames: California Mole; Broad-footed Mole; Anna's Hummingbird

File.Name: 6b14c_6b11-037.jpg
Notes:
Look what the cat brought in...

Our cat carefully left this dead critter by the front door for me to photograph. I went to my books, and I reckon this is a young California Mole aka Broad-footed Mole (Scapanus latimanus) even though it has no sign of the broad feet that characterize the adults. Compare my adult photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=7715028

This mole has perfect adaptations for burrowing: slender pointed face, flat ears, and real "moleskin" fur - like the pads for blisters - that can lay flat forwards or backwards. They have tiny eyes because there's not much to see underground in the dark.

Moles are mammals in the order Insectivora. They don't drag plants down like gophers (order Rodentia), but they tunnel through the garden looking for invertebrates and leave our plants with roots hanging in their tunnels. So that's why some garden plants suddenly wilt and my tripod unexpectantly sinks into the ground! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b15a_6b15-098.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful white berries of Common Snowberry (Symphoricarpos albus var. laevigatus - aka S. rivularis) in the Honeysuckle (Caprifoliaceae) family growing in the woodland along Alisal Creek between Solvang and Nojoqui Falls. (Santa Ynez Valley, 15 November 2006)

These plants are taller than I am with lots of blueberry-size white berries. They are surely different from the low Nuttall's Snowberry aka Tripweed (Symphoricarpos mollis) that grows near home and has already lost its smaller fruit. The pretty white berries don't taste good and may be slightly toxic. Even the birds don't like them.
CommonNames: Common Snowberry; Honeysuckle; Nuttall's Snowberry; Tripweed
ScientificName: Symphoricarpos albus var. laevigatus; S. rivularis; Caprifoliaceae; Symphoricarpos mollis
associatedTaxa: Symphoricarpos albus var. laevigatus; S. rivularis; Caprifoliaceae; Symphoricarpos mollis

File.Name: 6b15b_6b15-116.jpg
Notes:
Brilliant plumose seed heads of Creek Clematis aka Yerba de Chivato (Clematis ligusticifolia) in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family, with the sun behind. I got this photo in the woodland today along Alisal Road between Solvang and Nojoqui Falls. (Santa Ynez Valley, 15 November 2006)

I have a photo of the mid-summer flowers at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=14661451
CommonNames: Creek Clematis; Yerba de Chivato; Buttercup

File.Name: 6b15c_6b15-117.jpg
Notes:
Late flower of Fish's Milkwort (Polygala cornuta var. fishiae). This is an uncommon native shrub with odd flowers in it's own Milkwort (Polygalaceae) family. I've been collecting photos of all our late-season flowers, and this one is interesting. I found it in the woodland along Alisal Road between Solvang and Nojoqui Falls. That's typical. I usually find this shrub growing in shady north-slope canyons not too far from the river. (Santa Ynez Valley, 15 November 2006)

We have school conferences all week. We had a break this morning, so I went exploring.

File.Name: 6b16a_6b15-089.jpg
Notes:
Seed pods and pinnate foliage of an ornamental tree at school with the sun behind. I'm better with natives than garden plants, but I guess this is a Silktree Mimosa (Albizia julibrissin or kin) in the Mimosoideae group of the Pea (Fabaceae) family. Some plants are made for backlight! (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 November 2006)

We have school conferences all this week. I got out with my camera during a break this morning. We're finally done with conferences tomorrow, and then a week of vacation!

File.Name: 6b16b_6b16-127.jpg
Notes:
Halloween pumpkins in the school compost pile - two weeks later. (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 November 2006)

We have school conferences all week. I got out with my camera during a break this morning.

File.Name: 6b17a_6b17-133.jpg
Notes:
Ladybird Beetle aka "Ladybug" (Coleoptera, family Coccinellidae) on late-season flowers of a native composite shrub Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) growing on the dry flood plain of the river. This is one of the last of our summer flowers that overlaps the new Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrubs starting to flower at home. There is always something flowering in our muntains, but we really need rain to start the new generation! (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 November 2006)

We finished a long week of school conferences today. I stopped by the Sage Hill campground in Paradise near the Santa Ynez River on my way home to get a few photos despite the wind. Now I have a well-earned vacation week to get a few more photos!
CommonNames: Ladybird Beetle; Ladybug; Palmer's Goldenbush; Chaparral Currant
ScientificName: Coleoptera, family Coccinellidae; Ericameria palmeri; pachylepis; Ribes malvaceum
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera, family Coccinellidae; Ericameria palmeri; pachylepis; Ribes malvaceum

File.Name: 6b17b_6b17-139.jpg
Notes: Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly working late flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family. The late-season flowers are almost done, and so are our butterflies. I was suprised to find these flowers and butterflies today near the river. (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b17c_6b17-137.jpg
Notes: Almost-ripe acorns on a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree by the river. It looked like a good acorn crop last summer, but many trees dropped their acorns early. These acorns are unusually long and skinny. (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b17d_6b17-143.jpg
Notes: Ripe fruit of Calabazilla (Cucurbita foetidissima) in the Gourd (Cucurbitaceae family . The species name tells how it tastes, but the apple-size gourds were used by Chumash native Americans for soap, tools, and rattles. These native gourds are not common. This colony near Paradise Camp has been here as long as I can remember. I wonder if it dates back to the old days. (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b18a_6b18-012.jpg
Notes:
Bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) huddled under a one-size-too-small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf in the woods. The spder has tied this fallen leaf into the outer edge of her web for a refuge, but she needs a bigger leaf! See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2006)

This photo is not "photoshopped" beyond my usual color and contrast adjustments - no layers or masks. The spider was too contrasty, so I held up my hand to put a shadow on the spider and keep the bright background. That's the low-tech solution for a common lighting problem!
ScientificName: Araneidae) huddled under a one-size-too-small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Araneidae) huddled under a one-size-too-small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 6b18b_6b18-011.jpg
Notes: I saw the spider web and followed it to find the spider. This bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) is huddled under a fallen one-size-too-small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf in the woods - like a very large person trying to squeeze into a too-small amusement park car. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2006)
ScientificName: Araneidae) is huddled under a fallen one-size-too-small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Araneidae) is huddled under a fallen one-size-too-small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 6b18c_6b18-038.jpg
Notes:
Female not-green Lynx Spider (family Oxyopidae) in the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub by our mailbox. This lady spider has been guarding her egg sack for more than a month, but she finally gave up and moved one branch away to be single again. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2006)

I first noticed this spider with egg sack on October 6. I''ve been waiting to photograph the spiderlings. I wonder what's growing inside the egg sack now!
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=16213090

File.Name: 6b18d_6b18-003.jpg
Notes: Dandelion-like composite seeds of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) along our road in the canyon. Leaving home is not as easy for seeds or kids! (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b18e_6b18-007.jpg
Notes: Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family in the canyon. These are not sperm going in, but seeds somehow working their way out of the slender pod. It's a stumper - how do these seeds work their way out against the grain of their plumose "wings"? (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b18f_6b18-032.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) snuggling into a non-native Salvia (Labiatae) flower in the garden. Flowers and pollinators are getting scarce as we head towards the winter solstice, but our garden is a refuge for both. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b19a_6b19-073.jpg
Notes:
Oh my - you have to look close at this photo. I didn't know what I had when I took the photo of on interesting pattern in a sap drip on a non-native pine tree at an old homestead a few miles from home. I only saw it when I got home and put the photo up on my computer screen. Can you see it now?

This is an insect entombed in pine resin framed between golden columns of dried sap. From the pattern of the abdomen at the bottom, I guess it's a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera). It must have struggled - you can see its final kicks frozen in the resin, like a Chihuly glass sculpture. Now can you see the whole insect - head above, abdomen below, legs to the sides? This is amber in the making. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2006)

I know where this is. I'll go back for more photos this week!

File.Name: 6b19b_6b19-050.jpg
Notes: Beautiful early morning flowers of composite Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. We're at the end of our summer-dry season waiting for the winter-wet season to begin. There are still late-season flowers in the mountains even though the last real rain was many months ago and new-season flowers are already starting to appear. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2006)
CommonNames: Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Sunflower

File.Name: 6b19c_6b19-059.jpg
Notes:
All the flower stages of the native late-season composite shrub Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family - aborted buds, new flowers, developing seeds, ripe seeds ready to fly away, and the final empty calyx-like papery involucre. The species grows in the woods in our canyon, but I believe this is the variety Grindelioides with more congested and less resinous leaves growing in the rocks at Lizard Mouth in the mountains nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2006)

As you learn the natural history of one place very well, these little differences in plants between canyon and canyon become more important. This is evolution in action.

File.Name: 6b19d_6b19-080.jpg
Notes: We've had unseasonably warm sunny weather, but somehow these native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrubs know it's time to flower for the new season, even though it feels like a beach day. Every year is different, but the schedule stays the same. These are the second native shrub to flower in the woods after the early Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum). It gets more complicated when the rains finally come. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b19e_6b19-052.jpg
Notes: Fully-formed flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) family, but they will wait a few months before opening for business next spring. It's an interesting strategy. Several plants form their buds early, but many don't. I wonder if there's an advantage, or is this just how it's always been done? (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b19f_6b19-083.jpg
Notes: Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) stretching her tail feathers while perched in her usual spot in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree above the garden. I got this photo from the upstairs porch. Note the perfect Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) web behind her. The spiders are everywhere! (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2006)
CommonNames: Anna's Hummingbird; Coast Live Oak; Orb-Weaver Spider

File.Name: 6b20a_6b20-107.jpg
Notes:
This late-season Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) shrub in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family still has a few flowers, but look close! Instead of a female "style" in the center, it has another flower bud trying to make a flower in a flower! These Bush Poppies often get thick "fasciated" growth at the end of our summer dry-season, and the flowers get weird. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 November 2006)

I drove up the potholed East Camino Cielo road to the summits of our mountains today looking for late-season flowers, and I wasn't disappointed. It's t-shirt and beach weather - warm and dry. We haven't had any real rain in our summer-dry mountains for 6+ months. It's remarkable that there are any new flowers! I saw few insects today. The flowers have outlasted the pollinators.

File.Name: 6b20b_6b20-112.jpg
Notes: There are still pretty new composite flowers of Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in the mountains, though the flowers are getting scarce as we move towards winter. We haven't had real rain in the mountains for 6+ months. It's remarkable that any plants are still flowering! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b20c_6b20-084.jpg
Notes: These composite flowers of native Bush Sunflower (Encelia californica in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family) are unexpected in the mountains, but here they are in full bloom along Camino Cielo on the top of the mountains. Maybe a highway crew dumped waste soil here? It's hard to tell the native range of any native plant these days. I guess time will tell. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b20d_6b20-098.jpg
Notes: I went up the potholed road to the summits of our mountains today looking for late-season flowers, and I wasn't disappointed. This is a new flower of native Red Thistle (Cirsium occidentale - maybe var. venustum?) still flowering after months of summer drought. The plants and mushrooms and critters are all waiting for real rain to start the new wet-season. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b20e_6b20-104.jpg
Notes: Tomato-like fruit of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum Xanti) still flowering in a disturbed place in the mountains. All parts of the plant is said to be toxic. Sounds good to me, but didn't they used to say that about tomatoes? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b20f_6b19-073.jpg
Notes: This is part of my [Previous] photo q.v. On the right is a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) entombed in pine resin on a non-native pine tree at an old homestead a few miles from home. It must have struggled - these are its final kicks frozen in resin and drawn to capillary fineness. A spider has added its web to the perfect curve and caught a small fly as prey. That's life and death in the woods... (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b21a_6b21-144.jpg
Notes:
Dancing "Armillaria Mushroom Fly" kicking up the white-spores on the cap of a Honey Mushroom (Armillariella mellea or kin) in the garden today. I made up that name, but I see these flies every year on the same mushrooms. It's in the order Diptera for sure, but I don't know what family. These are the flies that lay the eggs that become the maggots that usually consume the Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea or kin) in the woods before I can! See my [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2006)

I've seen these honey-colored flies on the honey-colored Honey Mushrooms in our woods before. See my photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=11722301
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=11785559

I was surprised to find any mushrooms today. We're still waiting for our first real winter rain after 6+ months of drought in our summer-dry "Mediterranean climate" California mountains. But there are still new flowers and new mushrooms in the woods! Even dry weather can't stop the seasons from turning, just like you can't stop a slow coasting truck by pushing against it - too much momentum!

File.Name: 6b21b_6b21-139.jpg
Notes: I call this pretty fly an "Armillaria Mushroom Fly" - order Diptera for sure, but I don't know what family. These are the flies that lay the eggs that become the maggots that consume the Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea or kin) in the woods before I can! See my [Previous] two photos. Here it is perched on a Honey Mushroom cap in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b21c_6b21-135.jpg
Notes: Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea or kin) growing from a stump in the garden! These mushrooms are edible and tasty if you find them before the flies do. (Too late for these - see my [Next] photos.) It's also called "Oak Root Fungus" in California because it can attack living trees and shrubs. I find these mushrooms every year in the garden, and we've never had a problem. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b21d_6b21-133.jpg
Notes:
This caught me by surprise. It was overcast and windy today, so I was looking down on the ground for anything not moving. I noticed this new cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from a stump on the dry side of the garden hidden in the native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2006)

We're still waiting for our first real winter rain after 6+ months of drought in our summer-dry "Mediterranean climate" California mountains. But there are still new flowers and new mushrooms in the woods! Even dry weather can't stop the seasons from turning, just like you can't stop a slow coasting truck by pushing against it - too much momentum!

File.Name: 6b21e_6b21-130.jpg
Notes:
Webslinger Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) resting in a perfect web-woven hammock in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2006)

I reckon this is a stumper too - why don't spiders get stuck in their own webs?

File.Name: 6b21f_6b20-103.jpg
Notes: Mating Short-Horned Grasshoppers (order Orthoptera, family Acrididae) slung over a fallen needle of native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) near La Cumbre Peak, the high point of our mountains. This is a tender moment, but the grasshoppers look carved from stone. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b22a_6b22-147.jpg
Notes: Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) working flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. These are our first (and so far only) flowers of the new rain year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b22b_6b22-149.jpg
Notes: Female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) taking a break from working the Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) flowers in the Grossulariaceae family. These flowers have a shallow tube, and the hummer only has pollen on the tip of her beak. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b22c_6b21-142.jpg
Notes: It looks like this "Armillaria Mushroom Fly" is walking through snow, but it's resting on the white-spores that cover this cap of Honey Mushroom (Armillariella mellea or kin) in the garden. What looks like a path is really just the narrow depth of field at this near 1:1 magnification. See yesterday's photos. Several people suggested that this fly (order Diptera) is a Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae. I believe that's right. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2006)
CommonNames: Honey Mushroom; Pomace Fly; Small Fruit Fly
ScientificName: Armillariella mellea; Diptera) is a Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae
associatedTaxa: Armillariella mellea; Diptera) is a Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae
Families: Diptera) is a Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae

File.Name: 6b23a_6b23-160.jpg
Notes: Native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata in the Mint (Labiatae) family) flowering on the dry side of our garden. I don't count this as a new flower since it flowers all summer long with just a bit of water. There is much to be thankful for today - life in the woods, and that wonderful homemade dinner with family and friends we just finished. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b23b_6b23-167.jpg
Notes: Little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a flower of non-native Salvia greggii) in the garden today. I like the shadows. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b23c_6b23-156.jpg
Notes: Male Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) on his favorite perch in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree looking over the garden. The sun-bird-camera angles are just right to show his amazing iridescent colors. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b24a_6b24-180.jpg
Notes: Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula) bird - not much bigger than a hummingbird - in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees this morning. She makes it look so easy to stand on a leaf! (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b24b_6b23-174.jpg
Notes: Perfect apple-cranberry pie to finish Thanksgiving dinner last night. Thanks Roz! (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b25a_6b18-034.jpg
Notes: The unpicked grapes hanging over our porch are trying to become raisins. They have a yeasty taste like fresh bread dough. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b26a_6b26-016.jpg
Notes:
There were a least a dozen of these Townsend's Warblers (Dendroica townsendi) in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees at home this morning. Look close - this male is holding a green "Stink Bug" (order Hemiptera, maybe family Pentatomidae) that it found in the oak leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2006)

More warbler photos tomorrow. I'm getting a cold or flu - early to bed.
CommonNames: Townsend's Warblers; Coast Live Oak; Stink Bug
ScientificName: Dendroica townsendi; Quercus agrifolia; Hemiptera; Pentatomidae
associatedTaxa: Dendroica townsendi; Quercus agrifolia; Hemiptera; Pentatomidae

File.Name: 6b27a_6b26-015.jpg
Notes: Male Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) hovering under a branch of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree looking for insect or spider prey. See my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b27b_6b26-033.jpg
Notes:
Look close! This Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) has plucked a large Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) from its web in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our porch. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2006)

This photo isn't as sharp as I try for, but it answers a stumper. The autumn spiders pick off the summer insects, but who gets the spiders? The warblers do! I can't see a single spider in the oaks near home since this flock of a dozen or more warblers passed through our trees last weekend!
CommonNames: Townsend's Warbler; Orb-Weaver Spider; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Dendroica townsendi; Araneidae) from its web in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Dendroica townsendi; Araneidae) from its web in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 6b28a_6b26-032.jpg
Notes: Little bird hiding in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our porch, probably another little Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula) like my [Previous] photo. I'm sure these tiny birds are aware of my every move. They don't like my long telephoto lens, and they vanish if I bump my tripod. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b28b_6b23-157.jpg
Notes: Little Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula) bird - not much larger than a hummingbird - in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our porch. These fast little birds flutter constantly through the trees looking for prey. They are a challenge to photograph, but also great fun to chase with my morning cup of coffee. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b29a_6b26-045.jpg
Notes:
Pretty little crested Oak Titmouse (Baeolophus inoratus) - aka "Plain Titmouse" - bird on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near the porch. I usually find these birds flying in mixed flocks with other little birds. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2006)

Busy week - Thanksgiving, my kids visiting, school stuff, our first cold rain, we're between flowers and mushrooms in the woods - but the new birds have been a treat. There's always something happening in the woods!

File.Name: 6b29b_6b26-012.jpg
Notes: Male Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) on a branch of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near the porch. Males and females have similar colors, but the males are more black than gray. Compare the female in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b29c_6b26-041.jpg
Notes: Female Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) on a branch of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near the porch. Males and females have similar colors, but the females are more gray than black. Compare the male in my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 November 2006)

File.Name: 6b30a_6b30-082.jpg
Notes:
Sometimes I have to laugh at what I find in the woods! This is a new melon-size Bear's Head aka "Lion's Mane Hericium" or "Old Man's Beard" (Hericium erinaceus) mushroom growing from the scar of a fallen branch ten feet up a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. The fungus will turn white and develop icicle-like spines. Look close and notice that a squirrel has nibbled it on the lower-right. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 November 2006)

I have a photo of the mature mushroom at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/mykologos/?pid=5195879

File.Name: 6b30b_6b30-074.jpg
Notes:
Pretty fronds of native California Maiden-Hair (Adiantum jordanii) fern growing on sandstone above the entrance to a secret cave in the Santa Barbara mountains. The ferns look lush, but the woods are dry despite a 1/4 inch of rain last weekend. We are officially under a Red Flag fire warning today. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 November 2006)

See more photos of the cave at http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=11510088 and [Previous] to that.

File.Name: 6b30c_6b30-061.jpg
Notes:
Real pipestem stalactites in a secret cave in the Santa Barbara mountains. Look close to see a little spider husk. It's hard to take photos in a dark cave, even with flash. I used a little flashlight to get focus. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 November 2006)

See more photos of the cave at http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=11510088 and [Previous] to that.

File.Name: 6c01a_6b30-059.jpg
Notes:
Two of my after-school Hike Club kids inside the secret "Paradise Cave" in the Santa Barbara mountains yesterday. The mouth of the cave has lips of ferns above and brambles and bracken below. See my [Previous] photo for more info. There was a moment of perfect light. The sun is getting low over the mountains behind the north-facing cave, but the glow is a reflection from the south-facing mountains behind me on the other side of the valley. The remarkable light only lasted for a few minutes. A minute later and the cave would be all black.

I told the kids to stand still. The exposure is 1 second @ f/10 @ ISO 200. I did my usual adjustments on this photo, nothing special and no fancy layers. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 November 2006)

File.Name: 6c01b_6b30-078.jpg
Notes:
I took my after-school Hike Club kids to the secret "Paradise Cave" in the Santa Barbara mountains yesterday. Here we are posed at the opening of the cave. I set my camera on my tripod and used the self-timer so I could run over and be in the photo. The mouth of the cave really does look like a mouth - with a California Maiden-Hair (Adiantum jordanii) fern moustache and a beard of brambles and bracken! (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 November 2006)

I posted some photos yesterday, and a link to more photos from last year. As I said, this cave is big enough to walk around in - and crawl through to another opening. An ancient native American Chumash village site is nearby. It`s a secret place, so don`t bother asking. Find your own secret places!

File.Name: 6c02a_6c02-016.jpg
Notes: I was surprised to find this delicious new Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom in the woods today, but here it is! Looks like breaking waves meeting a backwash. I reckon the stem is split because it is so dry. We had 1/4 inch of rain last week, but the woods feel dry today. We're close to winter by the calendar, but there was a red-flag fire alert this week. We're still waiting for real rain in our summer-dry mountains to bring on new life for the new year. (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c02b_6c02-011.jpg
Notes:
Bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) guarding her nest in a native composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the canyon. I reckon the eggs will hatch soon, since these finished plant stems disappear over winter. (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2006)
