This bristly spider was lucky. Most of our big spiders disappeared last Sunday when a flock of Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) birds moved through the canyon. I have't seen any warblers since. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=17231423
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Telegraph Weed; Townsend's Warbler
ScientificName: Araneidae) guarding her nest in a native composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora; Dendroica townsendi
associatedTaxa: Araneidae) guarding her nest in a native composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora; Dendroica townsendi

File.Name: 6c02c_6b18-042.jpg
Notes: Little cricket (family Gryllidae in the order Orthoptera) on the last flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the canyon. I guess this ia a female with that long ovipositor. Compare my [Previous] photo of a male cricket. Insects are getting scarce after the hard frost this week. I had to scrape my car windows! (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c02d_6b18-039.jpg
Notes: Little cricket (family Gryllidae in the order Orthoptera) on a native grass (genus Leymus?) in the woods. I guess this is a male showing his stuff. Compare my [Next] photo of (I guess) a female showing her ovipositor. (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c02e_6c02-004.jpg
Notes: Ripe nuts of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivivus) in the Storax (Styracaceae) family. Sunlight is scarce in the canyon at this time of year. I do what I can. (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c02f_6c01-117.jpg
Notes: Strange flowers of Oak Mistletoe (Phoradendron villosum) growing as a parasite on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) near Lake Cachuma. The sun is setting and the light is just right. I didn't see any berries. (Santa Ynez Valley, 1 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c03a_6c03-018.jpg
Notes:
Fall colors and reflections in the river a few miles (as the vultures fly) from home. That's Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) on the left and Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) on the right. I've been mastering my music tape recordings from last summer, and this photo sure looks like a waveform! I wonder what it sounds like? (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 December 2006)

It's hard to show that many golden leaves in just 500 pixels.

File.Name: 6c03b_6c03-025.jpg
Notes: Shadow of White Sweet Clover (Melilotus albus) flowers and leaves on a sandstone rock near the river. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c03c_6c03-026.jpg
Notes: Any flower is remarkable at this time of year as the sun gets low and winter approaches - despite the perfect t-shirt weather today. This is native Cliff Desert Dandelion (Malacothrix saxatilis var. tenuifolia). Did you spot the tiny wasp in the middle-left? All insects are scarce now. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c03d_6c03-021.jpg
Notes:
Ripe fruit of native Toyon aka Christmas Berry aka California Holly (as in Hollywood) (Heteromeles arbutifolia). The shrubs flower in summer and mature in winter. They are a real gift for the birds during a hard time of year. Taste one and you will understand the word "astringent" - they dry your mouth out. They are tasty when cooked, and they make a good cider when steeped in boiling water. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 December 2006)

Many different shrubs have red berries in the fall. Toyon is edible - I don't know about the rest. Caveat emptor!

File.Name: 6c04a_6c03-039.jpg
Notes:
Looking close at a fall-color leaf of Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) in the Willow (Salicaceae) family along the Santa Ynez River near the first crossing. See my [Previous] photo. Autumn leaves look so perfect from a distance, but the defects are more interesting up close. This looks like someone has been shooting out the panes of an ornate window! (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 December 2006)

These trees don't make new pigments for their autumn colors. They break down and recycle their proteins and pigments for next year. This exposes the other yellow, orange, and red pigments that were already there, but hidden by the green chlorophyll. In time, nothing is left and the leaf becomes a husk, but the tree is ready to sprout new leaves next year. Such is life - it goes on.

See my stumper about "Beautiful Death":
   http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/03nov00.html

File.Name: 6c05a_6c03-055.jpg
Notes: Beautiful - or scary? Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) tending her nest in a bristly winter-ripe plant of Cocklebur (Xanthium strumarium) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. See my [Previous] photo. Even spiders are getting scarce in our almost-winter woods. We're still waiting for real rain in the mountains to bring on the new growing season. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 December 2006)
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Xanthium strumarium; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Xanthium strumarium; Asteraceae

File.Name: 6c05b_6c03-051.jpg
Notes: Bristly Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) tending her nest in a bristly winter-ripe plant of Cocklebur (Xanthium strumarium) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. The plant is said to be native, but it's weedy in disturbed places like the river wash. I had to plck dozens of the seed heads from my pants and socks after taking this photo. It`s said to be the original inspiration for velcro. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 December 2006)
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Xanthium strumarium; Asteraceae; velcro
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Xanthium strumarium; Asteraceae; velcro

File.Name: 6c06a_6c03-032.jpg
Notes: Fall-color leaves of Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) near the river. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c07a_6b19-054.jpg
Notes: Male "pollen cones" on a non-native pine tree (Pinaceae family) growing near an old homestead in the mountains. The pines and a few other unexpected naturalized plants are about the only clue that someone once lived here and called this place home. A typical woody pine cone is large and woody and female, with seeds or nuts. A male pollen cone (aka microstrobilus) is small and herbaceous, with lots of pollen. Sometimes you can hit a branch and see it release a cloud of yellow pollen! (San Marcos Pass, 25 November 2006)

File.Name: 6c07b_6b19-062.jpg
Notes: We usually notice the bright flowers, but the ripe seeds have their own beauty. These are the dandelion-like composite seeds of a native Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa var. Grindelioides) shrub in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2006)

File.Name: 6c07c_6b20-089.jpg
Notes: Winged seeds of a native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the Maple (Aceraceae) family. It's fun to knock the branches with a stick and watch the seeds flutter down like little helicopters. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2006)

File.Name: 6c08a_6c02-006.jpg
Notes:
Even at the end of our summer-dry season, there are native flowers to be found. These are well-named Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum), a shrub in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. There are no pollinators in the woods now that fit these remarkable flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2006)

The good news is that we're expecting rain tonight. I already felt a few drops!

File.Name: 6c09a_6c09-007.jpg
Notes:
Fallen Apple (Malus pumila) fruit in the Rose (Rosaceae) family lying in a compost of fallen autumn apple leaves in the garden. This is one way to spread seeds. The other way is for the tree to make delicious fruit that we carry and eat and spit out the seeds to grow. The parent apple tree appeared 25 years ago when we built our home from a spit seed or tossed core during lunch break. It has tasty fruit. The real Johnny Appleseed - aka John Chapman - would be proud! (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2006)

The clouds came down to the mountains today - good for the woods, but not for photos. After drizzle and clouds and wind all day - few photos - I'm hoping for real rain tonight.

File.Name: 6c09b_6c09-003.jpg
Notes: A tiny forest of fruiting moss (Bryophyta) sporophytes in the canyon. I can not tell a lie. This moss is growing on the concrete lid over an access point to our shared water system in the canyon. I arranged the oak leaves to look natural. Maybe there's a small leek that keeps them happy here? Most mosses, fungi, lichens, and seedlings in our mountains are dormant, waiting for real winter rain to end our summer dry-season. Maybe tonight? (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c09c_6c09-006.jpg
Notes: Old frond of summer dry-season Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) losing color in the woods just as the new winter wet-season ferns are waking up. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c10a_6c10-017.jpg
Notes:
A clutch of Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens) huddling on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the canyon, with raindrops. We had a huge convergence of these beetles a few months ago. They seem to be coming back for winter. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2006)

We had an inch of rain last night and more today. Today's photos show some of what the rain brings out!
CommonNames: Convergent Lady Beetles; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 6c10b_6c0-043.jpg
Notes:
Slugs have teeth? This Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) is sucking on a fallen plant stem in the woods. It sure looks like this hand-size terrestrial mollusk can extrude dentures as well as eye stalks! (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2006)

We had an inch of rain last night and more today. This is what the rain brings!

File.Name: 6c10c_6c0-032.jpg
Notes: An inch of rain last night, and already there are mushrooms in the woods. I have to find my books and relearn the mushrooms every year. Maybe this is Collybia? I love the curves of the mushroom and oak leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c11a_6c10-009.jpg
Notes: A circus tent of spider webs and raindrops on the top leaves of the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) just outside the upstairs bathroom window. It's a great view while sitting on "the throne". The perfect morning light only lasted a few minutes. No time to remove the screen, so I took this photo through the window glass. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c11b_6c10-00.jpg
Notes: Dying leaf of native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the woods, with raindrops. The bright red fungus looks like a line of advancing wildfire leaving a wasteland behind. That's pretty close to the truth! (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c12a_6c12-063.jpg
Notes:
Sleepytime for hummers...

This little male Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) is asleep on his favorite perch in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree above the garden. The sun is setting, but it's still dusk. I used "Slow Flash" turned down a stop and my 500 mm telephoto with an exposure of 1/15 second @ f/6.3 @ ISO 800. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c12b_6c12-058.jpg
Notes:
Mushrooms have legs! Not really, but this strange Earthstar (Astreus hygrometricus or kin) fungus growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) appears in the woods suddenly, like it crept in on starfish-like feet from somewhere else. It's a double-skinned puffball mushroom. The thick outer skin opens like a star and curves down to lift the inner thin-skined spore sack to expose the spores to the elements. The thin membrane and spores soon disappear, but the thick rinds persist in the woods for months, like washed-up shells on the beach. (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 December 2006)

I had some time after school today, but the sun was already too low. It's fun trying long exposures in the shade when there's no other choice. This exposure is 2.5 seconds @ F/22 @ ISO 200.

File.Name: 6c13a_6c10-030.jpg
Notes: Late berries of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Honeysuckle (Caprifoliaceae) family. The vines in the woods mostly finished making their berries two months ago, all eaten by birds now. This one plant still has a glow of life. The old flowers look ancient, carved from stone. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c13b_6c10-034.jpg
Notes:
New mushroom and fallen oak leaf in the woods after our first real winter rain. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2006)

I show the mushroom at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=17463479

File.Name: 6c14a_6c14-135.jpg
Notes: My after-school Hike Club kids - I recognize each of you by silhouette - on the rocks near Lizard's Mouth on top of the mountains as the sun goes down. There's ocean and at least one Channel Island in the background. It's a perfect moment to remember. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c14b_6c14-071.jpg
Notes: Very dead mouse shot against the sky today at school using a stack of chairs as my tripod. We teachers expect the usual Christmas gifts, but not perfect dead mice! Thanks Mark, it really is perfect. (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c15a_6c10-023.jpg
Notes: Like a battlefield map... Deciduous trees drop their leaves all at once, but evergreen trees like this Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia in the Fagaceae family) still drop old leaves one by one over the year. The tree absorbs what nutrients it can, and fungi take care of the rest. In time the husk of the leaf will fall to enrich the soil for more new life. So it goes. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c15b_6c15-151.jpg
Notes:
Inky Cap (Coprinus atramentarius or kin) mushrooms at the base of a Willow (genus Salix in the Salicaceae family) tree at school. Clumps of these mushrooms have been coming up under this tree for years. These mushrooms literally digest themselves or "deliquesce" into a black inky mess. They are said to be edible, but I'm not tempted. (Santa Ynez Valley, 15 December 2006)

Maybe it's coincidence, but this willow tree with the mushrooms is not healthy. Several large branches died last spring, and we discovered a crack in the trunk. We campaigned to keep the tree as a school symbol and a good home for cavity-nesting birds. After major pruning, it's still here.

File.Name: 6c16a_6c16-031.jpg
Notes:
Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) converging on a spike of a native grass near the creek. I believe the grass is Blue Wildrye (Elymus glaucus). The ladybugs aren't picky about their roost - I also found them today on oaks, blackberries, mugwort, canyon sunflowers and just about everything near the creek. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2006)

We had a huge convergence of these ladybugs in September. Then they disappeared. Now they're converging again for winter. I saw a few last week, and many more today. I guess they come together to mate, then depart to lay eggs, then come together again now to snuggle through the cold winter. Sounds good!
ScientificName: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Elymus glaucus
associatedTaxa: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Elymus glaucus

File.Name: 6c16b_6c16-025.jpg
Notes: A breaking wave of Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) crowding under a leaf of native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the canyon today. There's a storm coming tonight. Maybe the ladybugs know, and this is shelter. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2006)
ScientificName: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Rubus ursinus
associatedTaxa: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Rubus ursinus

File.Name: 6c16c_6c16-014.jpg
Notes: I lifted this pretty yellow mushroom from the ground in the woods today - the Wolf Spider (family Lycosidae) came up with it. These spiders hunt their prey without webs. At first I thought the mushroom was a delicious "Man on Horseback" (Tricholoma flavovirens), but the rusty spore-stained fibers on the stem prove that it is a Cortinarius mushroom - the first I've seen this winter. There are too many Cortinarius species - I won't try to guess. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c16d_6c16-040.jpg
Notes:
Tiny dark-stemmed "Horsehair Fungi" (Marasmius androsaceus or kin) mushrooms sprouting from the tip of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf in the woods. They look like long-legged surfers riding the nose of this oak leaf! These little 'shrooms have a connection with lichens - they have a remarkable ability to dry out and revive again after new rain. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2006)

It's been overcast and drizzly all day, with a storm moving in tonight. Today's photos are all long exposures with tripod, mostly using the self-timer to steady the camera. This photo is 1/8 second @ f/22 @ ISO 400.

File.Name: 6c16e_6c16-043.jpg
Notes:
This looks like a strange monochrome flower with in-turned petals, but it's really a strange mushroom with an up-turned cap. This is a tiny pencil eraser-size Inky Cap (Coprinus sp.) mushroom growing under the California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the woods. The margins of the cap look sugar-coated with spores. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2006)

There really are strange monochrome flowers - see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=15015682

File.Name: 6c16f_6c16-002.jpg
Notes: I was looking for tiny mushrooms in the fallen leaves when I found this belly-up male Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus sp. in order Orthoptera, family Gryllidae). Maybe it's too cold, or there's no food in the woods, or he's just used up his time. This may be the last of the summer insects, but there are new mushrooms in the woods. Life goes on. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c17a_6c17-054.jpg
Notes: Acrobatic male Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) bird in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree close to our upstairs porch. He appears to have a snack in his beak. A mixed flock of these warblers, kinglets, and a titmouse or two came through the woods today on the wind following our expected storm that never happened. I've seen these mixed flocks of songbirds before - I don't see them every day - I wonder how this works. It's windy today after our faux storm. Maybe the birds follow the storms to get an easier ride as they migrate south? (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c17b_6c17-049.jpg
Notes: Beautiful male Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) bird looking at me as I look at him from our upstairs porch. What remarkable colors! See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c17c_6c17-073.jpg
Notes: It takes a moment to realize what's wrong with this photo! This is a little Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula) bird in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our upstairs porch. These birds are such acrobats. I get neck pains just looking at this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c17d_6c17-084.jpg
Notes:
Little Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula) bird - not much larger than a hummingbird - in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our upstairs porch. They look like flycatchers, but they have a different characteristic behaviour as they flutter under the oak leaves looking for breakfast. The variable pitch of their feathers while hovering is amazing! (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2006)

Sure it's a lucky shot - but I took 150 photos of the birds today, kept 30, and I'm posting 5. It's that kind of luck.

File.Name: 6c17e_6c17-078.jpg
Notes:
A mixed flock of busy warblers and kinglets came through the woods today - see my [Next] photos. I've seen this pattern before. There's almost always a shy Oak Titmouse (Baeolophus inoratus) - aka "Plain Titmouse" - like this flying with the flock. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2006)

This Titmouse has his crest down flat. Compare my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=17251962

File.Name: 6c17f_6c16-029.jpg
Notes: I can't resist one more photo of the Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) covering this spike of native Blue Wildrye (Elymus glaucus) grass near the creek. Se my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2006)
ScientificName: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Elymus glaucus
associatedTaxa: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Elymus glaucus

File.Name: 6c18a_6c18-101.jpg
Notes:
Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) - 1 of 6

This is a hanging solstice banner of Monarch butterflies - strange fruit in the Eucalyptus trees - at the Ellwood Butterfly Grove near the beach. All Monarch butterflies west of the Rocky Mountains fly to California for winter and this is a favorite stop. Eastern Monarchs head for Cuba or Mexico. I first saw the butterflies almost 40 years ago in the same trees. This Eucalyptus grove is now protected, and I stayed behind the rope barrier, though I wanted to get closer for photos. I used my 500 mm telephoto lens for all of today's photos. (Ellwood Mesa, 18 December 2006)

This butterfly grove is more than half surrounded by houses just a few hundred meters away. These winter butterflies are so vulnerable to habitat loss, but this grove is now protected as a reserve. Find your special places and find a way to protect them!

The irony is that the butterflies roost in the introduced Blue Gum (Eucalyptus globulus or kin) trees - not native, but doing well here after 100+ years. It's an eco-quandry - remove the alien trees or disturb the native butterflies? I think the message is that we can't put it back together again - we have to make do with what we have and not lose more.
Locality: Eucalyptus globulus or kin) trees - not native

File.Name: 6c18b_6c18-124.jpg
Notes:
Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) - 2 of 6

Look close - these are Monarch butterflies hanging down, not leaves. The butterflies look almost gray in the shade until they open their wings - just like old leaves. (Ellwood Mesa, 18 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c18c_6c18-094.jpg
Notes:
Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) - 3 of 6

One Monarch butterfly is distinctive, but en masse they become some else, almost a single organism with many parts, a kind of cryptic camouflage to confuse preditors, like zebra stripes in the grass. (Ellwood Mesa, 18 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c18d_6c18-095.jpg
Notes:
Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) - 4 of 6

A hanging solstice banner of Monarch butterflies - strange fruit in the Eucalyptus trees - at the Ellwood Butterfly Grove where western monarch butterflies converge for winter. (Ellwood Mesa, 18 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c18e_6c18-126.jpg
Notes:
Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) - 5 of 6

Like looking out of a hobbit hole lined with butterflies. (Ellwood Mesa, 18 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c18f_6c18-137.jpg
Notes:
Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) - 6 of 6

I saw a few Monarch butterflies in the garden this fall, but I never got a photo, and they never laid eggs on our garden milkweeds like they usually do. Now I'm making up for it. I went down from the mountain to town for errands today and stopped by the Ellwood Butterfly Grove where western monarch butterflies converge for winter. It was a challenge to get this photo of just a few. See my [Next] photos. (Ellwood Mesa, 18 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c19a_6c19-147.jpg
Notes:
Frosty Cortinarius mushroom in the woods today at noon. It's been cold. The frost never did thaw on this hillside at home where the almost-solstice winter sun never shines. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2006)

It's fun shooting long exposures in these places where the winter sun never shines. This exposure is 4 sec @ f/32 @ ISO 200!

File.Name: 6c19b_6c19-140.jpg
Notes: Pretty little LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - probably little white-spored Mycena - pushing up through the fallen leaves in the woods on the native side of our garden. Mushrooms are just the fruiting bodies of fungi - like apples on an apple tree. The hair-like fungal mycelium and the thicker root-like rhizomorphs are visible below. See my [Previous] photo. I don't know about that white hair above - maybe I stroked my beard too hard! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c19c_6c19-156.jpg
Notes:
Tiny Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) mushrooms fruiting from a dead leaf in the wild part of our garden. Maybe "mushroom" is too big a word for these needle-thick fruiting bodies of this fungus decomposing the leaves. The white hair-like mycelial strands are "rhizomorphs" that are probably spreading the fungus between leaves. These tiny 'shrooms are only 1-2 mm thick. They grow a few inches tall, but it's hard to photograph anything that tall and thin. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2006)

This is a place where the sun never shines this close to the winter solstice. The exposure is 1.6 sec @ f/22 @ ISO 200.

File.Name: 6c19d_6c19-154.jpg
Notes:
These are ripe moss (Bryophyta) sporophytes in the canyon. I'm not sure if the caps fall off to release spores, or maybe they are perforated like tiny salt shakers to sprinkle them out. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2006)

"Sometimes a cigar is just a cigar." - attributed to Sigmund Freud, though it sounds more like Groucho.

File.Name: 6c19e_6c19-152.jpg
Notes: There are still a few flowers in our garden, though there is very little direct sunlight at this cold solstice-time of the year. I caught these at just the right time in a beam of sunlight through the trees. I believe this is the amazing Salvia holwayi native to the highlands of Guatamala and Chiapas. Our resident female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) perches in the oaks overhead and works these flowers every day. Our resident male hummer perches and works the hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") on the other side of our house. It's great entertainment watching them raid each other's flowers, but it must be hard for the hummers when the woods are cold and barren of flowers other than these. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c19f_6c18-119.jpg
Notes: Almost-solstice flowers of the non-native Blue Gum (Eucalyptus globulus or kin) trees in the butterfly grove in my [Previous] photos. The trees were introduced from Australia 100+ years ago. They have become part of the California landscape. There were Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) in the air, but I never saw one visit the flowers of the trees they hang on. (Ellwood Mesa, 18 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c20a_6c20-170.jpg
Notes:
Ripe Christmas Berries aka Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood) (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family. The native shrubs flower in summer and mature in winter. They are a gift for the birds during the hardest time of year. Taste one and you'll understand the word "astringent" - they dry your mouth out. But they are tasty when cooked, and they make a good cider when steeped in boiling water. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2006)

There are many shrubs with red berries in the fall. Toyon is edible - I don't know about the rest. Know what you're eating!
CommonNames: Christmas Berries; Toyon; California Holly; Rose

File.Name: 6c20b_6c20-166.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) in the canyon. The tree is related to avocado and cinnamon and sassafras - all in the Laurel (Lauraceae) plant family. You probably wouldn't pick the yellow-green flowers for a table setting, but they are interesting with six thick sepals (in one series, no petals) and 9 stamens - one for each petal and three extra inner stamens with a pair or orange glands at the base. On the solstice-cusp between our summer-dry and winter-wet seasons, any new flower in the woods is exciting. The leaves are good seasoning for a stew, but don't use too much! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2006)

The flowers remind me of little magnolias because of the thick "petals" that are really sepals. They are both considered "primitive flowers" - dinosaur flowers. The Laurales are closely related to (and once part of) the Magnoliales.

File.Name: 6c20c_6c20-184.jpg
Notes:
Most acorns don't become mighty oak trees. The two visible acorns on this Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae family) show holes made by some critter that has entered and consumed them. The little acorn in the middle never had a chance to develop. The other acorn cups are empty. Probably they were eaten, but maybe one will sprout? (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2006)

It's interesting to think that every oak - every critter! - must produce just a single reproducing offspring over its lifespan to maintain a stable population.

File.Name: 6c20d_6c16-037.jpg
Notes: Ripe seeds of a native - but weedy - composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family growing along the road in the canyon. The seeds are trying to fly away, but they are are stuck in spider webs. That happens. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c20e_6c18-091.jpg
Notes: Dead Man's Foot (Pisolithus tinctorius) - or some body part - mushroom growing along our road. Sometimes they come up through the road! The fruiting body is made up of many lentil-like chambers (peridioles) that are breaking up here to release rusty spores. You can see some bluish color if you look close. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a fabric dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c20f_6c16-004.jpg
Notes: There are forests of these little LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - probably white-spored Mycena 'shrooms - coming up through the fallen leaves in the woods. I took this photo a few days ago when there was rain in the forecast. The storm didn't happen and it's been very cold - the frost isn't melting. We need rain soon to keep the mushrooms coming. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c21a_6c21-219.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of a native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Heath (Ericaceae) family in the woods by the river. They look like a choir singing down to the earth. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 December 2006)

Happy solstice! Today is one of the Earth's special days. From now on the days are getting longer (north) or shorter (south). Enjoy them all!

File.Name: 6c21b_6714-196.jpg
Notes:
I took this photo last summer near the Summer Solstice, but it's really a Winter Solstice photo. This is the "Painted Cave" that's shown on California maps in our mountains. The site is a rock cave near home, and the rock art is part of a Native American Chumash pictograph (painted not chipped) panel. Age and meaning are unknown, but that sure is a sun symbol, and there are other rock art sites that align with the winter solstice sun. Makes sense. This is the shortest day of the year - we have to bring it back! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2006)

This rock art site now has bars to prevent damage, but the old graffiti is facinating. Look at that little CD-like circle in the lower-left:
   "Apr 1905
   Leonard Kellogg
   Goleta, Cal"

When graffiti gets this old, it's history. There's a Kellogg Avenue and Kellogg School in Goleta. But what was Leonard thinking when he tagged his name over the ancient Chumash art? I guess one lesson is don't leave your name and address! What about that punk face just above the graffiti?!

There's a dozen more hidden Chumash rock art sites I've found in our mountains on San Marcos Pass. I'm always looking for more. The old voices resonate on this solstice day.

File.Name: 6c21c_6c21-204.jpg
Notes: New leaves of Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the Gooseberry Grossulariaceae family getting ready for the new year. This plant has impressive bristles and spines! New life in the woods is the perfect sign on this dark Winter Solstice day. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c21d_6c21-210.jpg
Notes:
Heh - these are seeds of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) plant family. I cheated for this photo and rolled the seeds in my hands to remove the papery covering. The seeds look like little winter gourds underneath. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 December 2006)

Consider plamting poison oak if you live in the country. It keeps people out, and wildlife love it. BTW - I don't usually get poison oak unless I really ask for it!

File.Name: 6c21e_6c21-202.jpg
Notes: Little LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - coming up through fallen oak leaves and brambles in the woods by the river on this dark solstice day. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c21f_6c21-195.jpg
Notes:
Natural arrangement of fallen leaves of deciduous Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) on a stump near the Second Crossing at Paradise along the river. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 December 2006)

I went to the river looking for light on this Winter Solstice day - the dark day of the year. It was shady here too, so I did what I could with long exposures.

File.Name: 6c22a_6c22-221.jpg
Notes:
Little LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - pushing up through fallen oak leaves in the canyon. It's interesting how the little 'shrooms fit the leaves so perfectly. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2006)

We had a bit of rain last night, but today it's been very windy. It was too windy for photos, so I hiked down deep into the canyon to a shady place where I've never been before. This is the hardest time of the year to get new photos every day. We're still waiting for real rain.

File.Name: 6c22b_6c21-201.jpg
Notes: Little LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - probably white-spored Mycena 'shrooms - coming up through fallen oak leaves in the woods by the river. I could just as well have taken this photo at home. There are many little mushrooms in the woods, but few big ones ready for dinner. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c22c_6c21-200.jpg
Notes: I guess these dark-spored fragile mushroom are Psathyrella longipes or kin under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. Look close to see the white cottony veil remnants on the edge of the cap. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c22d_6c21-193.jpg
Notes:
Cantaloup-size Bear's Head aka "Lion's Mane Hericium" or "Old Man's Beard" (Hericium erinaceus) mushroom growing from the scar of a fallen branch ten feet up a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. I used my long lens. The mushroom has little icicle-like spines that remnd me of a hedgehog. It's said to be delicious when young, but I'd rather see it up in the tree, like a critter trying to enter its haven. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 December 2006)

I posted a photo of this same mushroom just emerging from the tree a month ago:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=17273387

File.Name: 6c22e_6c21-213.jpg
Notes: Strange fruit of Fish's Milkwort (Polygala cornuta var. fishiae). This is an uncommon native shrub with odd flowers and seeds in it's own Milkwort (Polygalaceae) family growing on a shady hillside by the river. There's a seed about to leave the pod on the left. This is near the fifth crossing in Paradise. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c22f_6c22-227.jpg
Notes: It was too windy for photos today, so I hiked down the canyon to a shady place where I've never been before. This is looking up at a Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) in the Willow (Salicaceae) family, leaning against a tree for my tripod. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c23a_6c23-014.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) with her face snuggled into a new urn-like flower of this native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Heath (Ericaceae) family in the woods. See my [Previous] photo. It's impressive how long the proboscis of the bee is to get to the good stuff in this flower! It's been cold in the mountains. I was surprised to find any flowers and pollinators today. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c23b_6c23-017.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working an urn-like flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Heath (Ericaceae) family in the woods. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c23c_6c23-026.jpg
Notes: Pretty Christmas colors - but these are not flowers but red leaves with an infection on Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the mountains. These are new galls of the Manzanita Fold Gall Aphid (Tamalia cowenii or kin) who live in cavities within the folded leaves. But there must be more to the story. The leaf in the lower-left has a red margin starting to change, though there's no room for aphids yet. What do the gall-forming aphids do to change the structure of these shrubs? Do they work along with a virus? Galls are so interesting as a natural kind of bioengineering. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2006)
CommonNames: Eastwood Manzanita; Manzanita Fold Gall Aphid

File.Name: 6c23d_6c23-011.jpg
Notes: It would be an interesting scavenger hunt to turn a bunch of naturalists loose to see who could find the most native flowers in the winter mountains when the summer flowers should be done - I would do well! This is California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) flower in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family still flowering in the woods after the solstice! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c23e_6c23-003.jpg
Notes:
Like a wave breaking over the woods... This is Cape Ivy aka German Ivy (Delairea odorata aka Senecio mikanioides) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. This vine is a bad invasive weed that climbs the trees and shrouds whole canyons near the coast. I found this colony by the bridge where West Camino Cielo road crosses San Jose Creek near home, ready to wash down the creek leaving sprouts the whole way. Some fool dumped their yard trimmings in the wrong place. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2006)

I try to avoid Christmas - too commercial - but I like the sentiments of peace on Earth and good will and giving. My present will be to remove this weed infestation on Christmas Day before it takes over. You should do the same.

File.Name: 6c23f_6c23-008.jpg
Notes: Done calyces of native Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina) shrub in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. These are the husks of the joined leaves that once held flowers and then ripe seeds. Their task is done. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c24a_6c16-033.jpg
Notes:
Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) snuggling in the rain last weekend. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2006)

It's a good reminder of what's important for the solstice holidays. Forget about buying stuff. Snuggle with friends, and do something worth doing.

Happy holidays
to all my Fotolog
friends and favorites!

File.Name: 6c24b_6c23-023.jpg
Notes: A natural Christmas wreath of native Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) family. The fully-formed flower buds will wait another month or two before opening next spring. The waxy buds are perfect for Christmas. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c24c_6c23-022.jpg
Notes: Ripe Christmas Berries aka Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood) (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2006)
CommonNames: Christmas Berries; Toyon; California Holly; Rose

File.Name: 6c24d_6c18-111.jpg
Notes: Christmas garlands of Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) hanging from leaves of non-native Blue Gum (Eucalyptus globulus or kin) trees at the Ellwood Butterfly Preserve last week. (Ellwood Mesa, 18 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c24e_6c23-027.jpg
Notes:
These Christmas colors are the sign of an infection of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the mountains. These are new galls of the Manzanita Fold Gall Aphid (Tamalia cowenii or kin) who will live in cavities within the folded leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2006)

See my photo yesterday:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=17724447
CommonNames: Eastwood Manzanita; Manzanita Fold Gall Aphid

File.Name: 6c24f_6c20-190.jpg
Notes: New flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. Christmas colors for the new year! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c25a_6c25-040.jpg
Notes:
Perfect spider Chrismas lights!

I was trying to get a photo of this tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) sitting in a beam of winter light. Too shaky. Then I realized the real photo is the rainbow patterns on the web in the lower left. It looks like a spectrogram. The colors must be caused by refraction from the vibrating web - the same vibrations that make the spider less than sharp even though I used my tripod and camera self-timer. Maybe you know "Roy G. Biv" - the rainbow colors. I don't quite get the rainbow colors in this photo, but here they are! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c25b_6c25-030.jpg
Notes:
The micro-world in the woods - perfect little LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - probably white-spored Mycena 'shrooms - under the California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) brambles. It's another world down here on the ground. Notice the oak catkin resting on the leaf at the bottom of the photo. The Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia)trees flowered last spring, but I know that wasp-galled catkins persist on the trees. That's my guess. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2006)

It always works when I think I've seen everything in the woods and can't find a new photo - get low in one place and look again!

File.Name: 6c25c_6c25-031.jpg
Notes: Little LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - probably white-spored Mycena 'shrooms - coming up through fallen oak leaves in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c25d_6c25-033.jpg
Notes: There's an old stumper - how do flies (order Diptera) get through the winter? The answer seems to be that they just hang out in protected places and wait until it gets warm. I believe it. We had a warm Christmas day, and here they are perched in the sun on the sandstone by the creek! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c25e_6c25-036.jpg
Notes:
I guess this is a Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus - or kin in the family Vespidae , order Hymenoptera) on the prowl in the oak leaves near the creek today. I was surprised to find any wasps in mid-winter, but it warmed up today for Christmas, and here it is - I guess - looking for moisture. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2006)

I'm looking for water too. The woods are already dry again after a few small rains. There's a "likely" chance of rain tomorrow night. We need it.

File.Name: 6c25f_6c25-032.jpg
Notes: Our weather turned warm for Christmas, and the Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - (Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) are waking up and falling to the oak leaves on the ground and feeling frisky. Compare my [Previous] photo from a week ago. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c26a_6c26-047.jpg
Notes: I was about to walk away looking for sun today - our home on the north side of the mountains is almost always in the shade near the solstice - but I noticed this little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working the still-flowering Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis) flowers in the garden. This little fly is not heavy enough to work the "lever arm" of the flower to bring down the stamens. I've been collecting late-season flowers, but these late insects are interesting too! (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c26b_6c26-057.jpg
Notes: Mighty new cone of native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) on the ridge near home. The long needles are prized for basket making. The big cones can weigh almost 10 pounds. I wouldn't camp under one of these trees! (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c26c_6c26-051.jpg
Notes: Dripping pine sap "icicles" - amber in the making - on a branch of native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) near home. The pine sap is dripping down from above. This is as close as we get to real icicles in the Santa Barbara mountains. I get nervous using my camera near these sticky "sapcicles"! (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c26d_6c26-059.jpg
Notes: Last summer's empty seed pods of BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus). I'm hoping for good rain tonight for next year's flowers and seeds! (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c26e_6c26-062.jpg
Notes: Looking under a dry Bolete mushroom under the mixed pines and oaks on the ridge. Maybe this is a Red-Cracked Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron or kin) mushroom. The woods (and this mushroom) are dry, but there's a good chance of rain tonight. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c26f_6c26-054.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family growing under the native Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) near home. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2006)

The flowers look so lush, but the woods are dry. The forecast is for rain tonight. We need it.

File.Name: 6c27a_6c27-071.jpg
Notes:
It's like a zen koan: how do you photograph the wind?

You can't see the wind, only things moving in the wind. I used my telephoto lens and tripod and set my camera as slow as possible - 1/15" @ f/32 @ ISO 200. The tree is native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family on the ridge above our house. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2006)

We had some rain last night, just 1/2 inch or so. It's been VERY windy today - gusts of 50+ mph (80 km/h) according to a neighbor's web-weather station. The trees are dancing!

File.Name: 6c27b_6c27-067.jpg
Notes: I guess this is a little Bushtit (Psaltriparus minimus) bird in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near the upstairs porch. It was so windy today, I was surprised to see (or photograph) any birds! I got this photo early during a lull as the wind was just kicking up. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c27c_6c17-065.jpg
Notes: I see these little Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula) birds almost every morning in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees near our upstairs porch. Morning coffee and a photo is a great way to start the day! (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c27d_6c17-051.jpg
Notes: Male Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) bird in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree close to our upstairs porch last week. I see a few of these beautiful winter birds in the trees almost every day. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c27e_6c25-046.jpg
Notes: Large Bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) guarding her nest under the eaves of our roof. The birds missed a few of these big spiders. I poked it and it didn't move. Maybe it's just a totem to protect the nest? Look how the web strands are anchored in little stars of web-stuff. It reminds me of a global or intergalactic network map! I used flash for this photo where the sun never shines. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c27f_6c20-158.jpg
Notes:
Ice crystals in the garden last week - the crystal world. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2006)

Today it's very windy but not frosty. I'm catching up with a few recent photos.

File.Name: 6c28a_6c29-092.jpg
Notes:
Native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata in the Mint (Labiatae) family) flowers catching the last sun of the day. They don't count as new native blooms since they flower all summer and into winter on the edges of our garden with the gift of extra water. Most of our summer-dry natives can't handle summer water, but these are doing well - and welcome on a dark winter day when flowers are scarce. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2006)

I'm pushing this photo back to fill a busy day with no time for photos. I see supplicant cartoon birds wearing shades and shrouds!

File.Name: 6c29a_6c29-077.jpg
Notes:
Like a cluster of rockets shooting off!

Nah, this is a late seed pod of non-native Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) in the Asclepiadaceae family finally letting go. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2006)

We have summer-dry and winter-wet seasons in our mountains. This winter is unusually dry so far - small rains always followed by drying winds. It's getting hard to find new photos to post every day.

File.Name: 6c29b_6c29-087.jpg
Notes:
I found this beautiful new Cortinarius mushroom pushing up through the leaves in the woods today. It has red-brown spores and a "cortina" of cobwebby hairs that cover and protect the gills rather than a membranous skin like store-bought Agaricus mushrooms. (See my [Previous] photo.) (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2006)

My books say this is the largest genus of gilled mushrooms. Most species are harmless, but a few are deadly poisonous, and they are very hard to distinguish because the mushrooms change so much as they age. I always take photos, but I don't collect them for the table.

File.Name: 6c29c_6c29-086.jpg
Notes:
Looking close at a new Cortinarius mushroom in the woods today - see my [Next] photo for the whole 'shroom. My books say this is the largest genus of gilled mushrooms. Most species are harmless, but a few are poisonous, and they are very hard to distinguish because they change so much as they age. I don't collect any of them for the table, but they are colorful and interesting to collect with my camera. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2006)

This photos shows the exquisitely contructed "cortina" that distinguished these mushrooms. Store-bought Agaricus mushrooms have a membranous skin or "veil" that covers the gills before they open. Cortinarius mushrooms have this "cortina" veil of cobwebby hairs that cover and protect the gills before they fall away. This specimen has a double-cortina. It has a "universal veil" of threads that cover the cap with a white deposit and extend down to the swollen base. It also has a "partial veil" of threads that cover the gills underneath the cap. In time, the threads will be stained red-brown by falling spores.

File.Name: 6c29d_6c29-082.jpg
Notes:
New Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom pushing up through the fallen leaves in the woods. These mushrooms are edible and delicious, but be sure you know what you're picking! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2006)

We've had some winter-rain in our summer dry woods, but not much. The mushrooms are still waiting for their time.

File.Name: 6c29e_6c29-075.jpg
Notes:
Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) - I think male - working flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2006)

I was pointing my camera the other way trying to get a photo of a different bird, when I heard this hummer right behind me - I was too close! This is not a perfectly sharp photo, but it captures the moment. I've been worried about our hummers after the huge winds. How do they survive the cold wind and rain?

File.Name: 6c30a_6c30-003.jpg
Notes:
Very late flowers of Longstem Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum) in the Polygonaceae family) still going in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2006)

We have summer-dry and winter-wet seasons here We have summer-dry and winter-wet seasons here in the Santa Barbara mountains. There's some overlap between late summer flowers like this buckwheat, and early winter flowers like the currant in my [Previous] photos. There's been rain in Oregon and snow in Colorado. We're still waiting for ours.

File.Name: 6c30b_6c30-019.jpg
Notes: New flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family in the woods. We're still waiting for real winter rain. It's shocking to find these beautiful flowering shrubs in the dry chaparral. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c30c_6c30-014.jpg
Notes: New berries growing on native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family in the woods. We're still waiting for real winter rain. It's shocking to find these beautiful flowering and now fruiting shrubs in the still dry chaparral. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c30d_6c30-008.jpg
Notes:
You never know what you'll find in the woods - I wouldn't want to find this under my seat at the movies!

The shrub is a native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). I believe this pretty furry insect gall is caused by the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus chrystallinus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. These interesting tiny wasps lay eggs that are nutured by the abnormal growth they initiate. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2006)
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Andricus chrystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Andricus chrystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 6c30e_6c30-024.jpg
Notes:
Tiny white mushrooms fruiting from a fallen branches of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2006)

I find mushrooms in the dark places where the sun never shines this near the solstice. This exposure is 2 seconds @ f/22 @ ISO 200.

File.Name: 6c30f_6c30-022.jpg
Notes: Another LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - in the woods. The cap is hygrophanous - it changes color and texture as it dry out. You can see the color change from the drier light margin to the wetter darker cap. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2006)

File.Name: 6c31a_6c31-037.jpg
Notes:
Out with the old and in with the new...

Here's a new "Little Brown Mushroom" (maybe Psathyrella?) fruiting from fallen leaves, and new leaves of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the woods. There's a tendril of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) that looks ready to yank the mushroom out. Life goes on.

It's another year.
Have a good one!

File.Name: 6c31b_6c31-035.jpg
Notes: Another LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - growing in the fallen oak leaves in the woods. I took several photos with my camera in place, and I can see the dark spots on the gills moving from photo to photo. They are tiny Springtail (order Collembia) insects celebrating life. Me too! (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2006)

File.Name: 5101a_5101-041.jpg
Notes: 9148407 Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) trees catching some afternoon glow on the Santa Ynez River above White Rock. The river is full of sediment from all the rain. I was thinking it looks perfect for tubing when two kids floated by on boogie boards with wet suits and fins and big smiles! I rafted the river from Gibraltor down to the first crossing during the "March Miracle" rains of 1991. All I can say is WAHOO! (Santa Ynez River, 1 January 2005)

File.Name: 5101b_5101-038.jpg
Notes: 9148324 Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) trees, with storm clouds and a moment of afternoon sunshine and the born-again Santa Ynez River flowing for the first time after several drought years! (Santa Ynez River, 1 January 2005)

File.Name: 5101c_5101-023.jpg
Notes: 9148314 Santa Ynez River flowing past winter-dormant Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) trees. The river has that pea-soup color because it is full of sediment. It's the first good flush in a few years. New Year, new river - happy day! (Santa Ynez River, 1 January 2005)

File.Name: 5101d_5101-021.jpg
Notes: 9148308 Debris wrapped around trees at the Santa Ynez River today. The river was a few feet higher yesterday, but it comes down fast when the rain stops. The water is not pretty - it's carrying a massive load of sediment (and old diapers etc.) that should add sand to the local beaches, but it will fill the *dammed* Lake Cachuma reservoir downstream instead. Beaches and reservoir and wildlife will all suffer loss. But I'm so happy we have a river again after several dry years without! (Santa Ynez River, 1 January 2005)

File.Name: 5101e_5101-043.jpg
Notes: 9148255 New Year's party at the Santa Ynez River in Paradise. New year, new river - what could be better! (Santa Ynez River, 1 January 2005)

File.Name: 5101f_5101-006.jpg
Notes: 9148230 The Santa Ynez River flowing a meandering path across the dry upper end of Lake Cachuma. It's a *dam* reservoir and the low "dammed" lake is a quarter mile beyond. With enough rain, these flats will all be under water. This is our river that should be bringing the native Steelhead Trout up to spawn. Well maybe, if the rain keeps coming as it has been all this week, enough to spill over the dam and open the channel at the river mouth! (Santa Ynez Valley, 1 January 2005)

File.Name: 5102a_4c31-084.jpg
Notes: 9153496 Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf caught in a roll of bird netting by the garden, with raindrops from the 20+ inches of rain this week. Today was dark and drippy and windy, with a new storm on the way, a good chance to think about my school classes that are about to start again. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2004)

File.Name: 5102b_5101-057.jpg
Notes: 9153406 Seedpods on a Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree against a stormy sky. This winter is wet, but the past few years were very dry. Some natives like this tree (and more in the archive) took the dry years as a signal to make extra seeds. Is this a way to predict the weather? (Santa Ynez River, 1 January 2005)

File.Name: 5102c_5101-046.jpg
Notes: 9153301 Willow stalks in the river. Every few years we get LOTS of rain, followed by years of little rain. Our "average rain" is just a math-number thing between drought and deluge. New residents get caught by surprise, as did these Willow (Salix) saplings trying to colonize the Santa Ynez River bed that has been dry since the 1998 El Nino floods. Native plants and animals are always pushing the edges into marginal territory. It's a good strategy for living with a changing climate that sometimes works and sometimes fails. We are not exempt. (Santa Ynez River, 1 January 2005)

File.Name: 5102d_5101-034.jpg
Notes: 9153186 Julie standing by the Santa Ynez River near White Rock with winter-dormant Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) trees on the other side. The river and creeks are running, and there's a forcast for another 4 to 8 inches of rain in the mountains tonight and tomorrow. Hang on! (Santa Ynez River, 1 January 2005)

File.Name: 5108a_5106-094.jpg
Notes:
9190931 Here's a glimpse under the skirts of this native California Peony (Paeonia californica) flower that has already shed its petals. It's so strange inside! There are two (sometimes three) large pistils that will bear seeds, and many stamens which seem to be falling away, and those strange orange "candy corn" things around the base of the flower. According to my flora, they are part of a "lobed nectary disk". Peonies are relatives of Buttercups in the family Ranunculaceae that have many stamens and pistils. My guess is those orange things are aborted pistils. I wonder how these closed flowers avoid self-pollination? See the [Previous] two photos for other stages of this flower. (Santa Ynez Valley, 6 January 2005)

"Oh the wind and the rain" - We've had 25+ inches of rain since Christmas! We had a break on Thursday afternoon when I got these photos near Lake Cachuma. It rained all day today, and a bigger storm is expected tonight. Woohoo!

File.Name: 5108b_5106-081.jpg
Notes: 9190602 California Peony (Paeonia californica) flowers always face the earth. This is about as open as they get until they drop their petals. In a wet year like this, that seems like a good adaptation for an early wildflower so the precious pollen doesn't get washed away! That opening with stamens within looks like an invitation for pollinators, but I haven't found any critters inside. Compare my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (Santa Ynez Valley, 6 January 2005)

File.Name: 5108c_5106-076.jpg
Notes: 9190526 Not-quite-open California Peony (Paeonia californica) flower. These native peonies usually hide under the chaparral, but this area near Lake Cachuma burned a few years ago to give them light. The perennial roots used to be collected for export to China as a medicinal herb. (Santa Ynez Valley, 6 January 2005)

File.Name: 5108d_5106-082.jpg
Notes: 9190465 Tiny "Little Brown Mushroom" - with a funnel-shaped "horn of plenty" cap and decurrent gills that grow down the stalk, maybe Clitocybe? Many of these little 'shrooms were growing on a rarely used trail - but only on the trail - among new wildflower seedlings. (Santa Ynez Valley, 6 January 2005)

File.Name: 5108e_5106-087.jpg
Notes: 9190225 Dark LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - maybe Psathyrella? - coming up in the woods with new grass seedlings after all our recent rains. (Santa Ynez Valley, 6 January 2005)

File.Name: 5108f_5106-095.jpg
Notes:
9190195 New flowers of Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus). It's a common native shrub with thousands-millions-gazillions of tiny white flowers that we usually only notice "en masse". The flowers are beautiful and intricate up close. This species has opposite leaves and grows on the north-facing side of our east-west mountains. A different species with alternate leaves takes its place on the south-facing slopes, compare:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8913141

Other similar white-flowered Ceanothus species grow along the beaches and summits and in the backcountry. This is the kind of biological diversity that makes local native plants so interesting. (Santa Ynez Valley, 6 January 2005)

File.Name: 5109a_5109-Rick Rushing.jpg
Notes:
9196780 Big water in the Santa Ynez River flowing over the first crossing in Paradise. I was busy at home today, but my friend Rick Rushing sent me this photo of what I'm missing. Thanks Rick!

I remember an old 1950s comic book - maybe Bugs Bunny? - it involved a tornado. The lesson was "Go where it's been, not where it's going." We live on top of the mountains, but the river is where all the water is going! (Santa Ynez River, 9 January 2005)

File.Name: 5109b_5109-002.jpg
Notes:
9196705 Our little waterfall at home only flows every few years when it REALLY pours, like this weekend. I had my camera steady on my monopod for this shot. I guess those streaks are raindrops falling at the same angle as the water? Or did I twitch just right? Exposure is 1/3 sec @ f/2.6 @ ISO 100. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2005)

We had trouble on our road today. I didn't want to risk my Nikon D70 in the rain, so I took my good ol' Olympus 2040 in a plastic bag to get some photos. Everything is wet. I tried to find dr spots on ,y t-shirt to wipe the lens for today's photos.

I dumped my coffee can rain guage too many times this weekend and lost count. Judging from Internet sources, we've had 18+ inches since Friday, with more coming!

File.Name: 5109c_5109-020.jpg
Notes:
9196596 The huge rains this weekend took a bite from our road in the canyon. This looks "iffy" to navigate, and expensive to repair.

"Oh the wind and the rain" - I dumped my coffee can rain guage too many times this weekend and lost count. Judging from Internet sources, we've had 18+ inches since Friday morning!

File.Name: 5109d_5109-013.jpg
Notes: 9196499 Storm water in the creek after huge rains this weekend. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2005)

File.Name: 5109e_5109-014.jpg
Notes: 9196484 Storm water in the creek after huge rains this weekend. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2005)

File.Name: 5109f_5109-006.jpg
Notes: 9196463 Storm water in the creek after huge rains this weekend. Note the suds caught in an eddy in the lower left. I'm pretty sure those suds are natural, not pollution. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2005)

File.Name: 5110a_5110-054.jpg
Notes: 9201573 The Santa Ynez River flowing into the upper end of Lake Cachuma. There's still a bit of snow on Mission Pine Mountain, though these have been warm storms. Look close. There's an old power pole on the island facing the river, with a Turkey Vulture on top trying to dry out by spreading its wings. Rain, rain, rain - 50+ inches since September. This looks like a good year to be a local naturalist with a camera! (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 January 2004)

File.Name: 5110b_5110-051.jpg
Notes:
9201523 The upper end of Lake Cachuma today. The water is earth-colored, but the hills and mountains are green and blue. (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 January 2004)

Compare my New Year's Day photo - just 10 days ago - at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=9148230

File.Name: 5110c_5110-039.jpg
Notes:
9201476 Bradbury Dam at Lake Cachuma today after LOTS MORE RAIN since Friday. The reservoir is full, but the dam managers ("dam dam") have raised the gates a bit to release water to make room for the next deluge expected tonight and tomorrow. It's leaking rather than spilling. Note all the debris. See my [Previous] two photos to see how this works. (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 January 2004)

At home on San Marcos Pass, we've had remarkable rainfall since Christmas. I lost count emptying my coffee can rain guage. The official number (so far) is 51.38 inches for the rain year since September. I think we had 25+ inches last weekend! We've been mentioned on TV. Mushrooms and wildflowers and insects, oh my!
Locality: so far) is 51.38 inches for the rain year since September.  I think we had 25+ inches last weekend!  We've been mentioned on TV.  Mushrooms and wildflowers and insects

File.Name: 5110d_5106-098.jpg
Notes: 9201408 It started raining on the day after Christmas this year, and rarely stopped. Here are the flood gates of Bradbury Dam at Lake Cachuma last week after LOTS of rain. The lake is almost up to the base of the closed flood gates. (Santa Ynez Valley, 6 January 2004)

File.Name: 5110e_4b21-110.jpg
Notes: 9201370 The flood gates of Bradbury Dam at Lake Cachuma back in November when the reservoir was down even further than you can see in this photo. We've had some drought years. Note how the huge gates lift UP to release water from the bottom. I don't know why they are pumping water INTO the dam! And where is that water coming from? Hilton Creek at the base of the dam is the last upstream holding pool for endangered Southern Steelhead Trout! I think of this as the "dam dam" (you know). Compare the next two photos! (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 November 2004)

File.Name: 5112a_5111-063.jpg
Notes:
9216145 Driving down Highway 154 to school yesterday. The last of our storms is finishing with a show after dumping 20+ inches of rain over the weekend. Look how bright it is under the rainbow, and that rainbow double faintly visible on the right side of the sign. Just after I took this photo I had to stop because it rained so hard! (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2005)

I love it. The highway is closed by slides and flood on the Santa Barbara side of San Marcos Pass, but it's empty and beautiful on my side of the mountains. I'm in no hurry to fix it!

File.Name: 5115a_5115-043.jpg
Notes:
9236012 I noticed a twig hanging on a spider web from a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. I looked closer and realized it's a caterpillar hanging from its thread. This is the butt-end, showing its "prolegs". I used my tripod, but the gentle breeze was enough to spin this critter around and around - and maybe the caterpillar helped. This is my best of many shots. It looks like something from under the sea. I hope I will always find surprises like this in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2005)

Now I'm stumped. This criter is hanging by a thread from it's HEAD-end. So where is the supporting thread coming from? Is it saliva?

File.Name: 5115b_5115-011.jpg
Notes: 9235921 Elegant new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) catching a beam of sunlight. Red sepals, white petals, and sagittate (arrowhead-like) male stamens hugging the delicate female pistils. Other flowers have three pistils. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2005)

File.Name: 5115c_5115-028.jpg
Notes: 9235822 Our woods are drying out after heavy rains since Christmas. Rain is good for mushrooms, but somehow the `shrooms know they can`t spread spores when it`s too wet, so they wait. Today I mostly found old soggy Bolete and Lactarius mushrooms. This brilliant new Russula emetica (or kin) mushroom is pushing up through oak leaves and native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens). (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2005)
ScientificName: Bolete; Lactarius; Russula emetica; Agrostis pallens
associatedTaxa: Bolete; Lactarius; Russula emetica; Agrostis pallens

File.Name: 5115d_5115-023.jpg
Notes: 9235688 New Chocolate Marasmius (Marasmius fuscopurpureus or kin) mushrooms pushing up through the soggy Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the canyon. I found these mushrooms before the rains. Marasmius 'shrooms have the remarkable ability to fully revive when moistened. They may have a connection with Lichens that also revive with rain. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2005)
ScientificName: Marasmius fuscopurpureus; Quercus agrifolia; Marasmius; Lichens
associatedTaxa: Marasmius fuscopurpureus; Quercus agrifolia; Marasmius; Lichens

File.Name: 5115e_5113-101.jpg
Notes: 9235669 There's magic when river meets ocean. I think of the end of the movie "Dead Man". This is our born-again Santa Ynez River flowing for the first time in years from the right into the ocean on the left. All the river dams are spilling after recent rains. Tides and waves make it complicated here at the river mouth. I hope anadromous salmon and steelhead trout and eels (lamprey) are swimming up the current to renew their ancient ritual of new life. *Sigh* - the bacteria count in this water is over the top. (Surf Beach, 13 January 2005)

File.Name: 5115f_5114-255.jpg
Notes: 9235553 Highway 154 will be closed for a while after the rain! This is how it looks on the Santa Barbara side of our mountains at the junction with Painted Cave Road and the Old Pass Road. It will take months to repair. I know it's a hardship for many, but I love it! I live high in the mountains and drive the other way to school. I`ve been 5 minutes later than usual to school every day this week. Hah! - I`m driving slower without all the $#@! highway traffic pushing me faster. Another reason to drive slow is that I`ve seen deer on the road every day this week. Deer adapt quickly to the change in traffic. There will be a slaughter when the highway opens again. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2005)

File.Name: 5116a_5116-088.jpg
Notes:
9509917 Sheet Web Spiders (Linyphiidae?) in the act of love. That's the male on the right. I have a "Spider Love" sequence at http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=9242198 and [Previous].

Don't go there if you have "Insect Fear" or "Arachnophobia"!
Locality:
