Don't go there if you have "Insect Fear" or "Arachnophobia"!

File.Name: 5116b_5116-080.jpg
Notes:
9242198 Spider love (1 of 6 today): Sheet Web Spiders (Linyphiidae?) in the act of love, so many legs entwined, transfering genetic material from palps to epigyne. It's a tender moment. I've never seen this before. I knew something was up when I spotted two spiders together on the same web on a native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) shrub. I felt like a voyeur, but I guess spiders watch too. I did some research and learned a lot about spiders. See my [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2005)

Anyone with "Insect Fear" or "Arachnophobia" should probably click away fast before the photo loads. Sorry Emma!
ScientificName: Linyphiidae; palps; epigyne; Ribes amarum; hoffmannii
associatedTaxa: Linyphiidae; palps; epigyne; Ribes amarum; hoffmannii

File.Name: 5116c_5116-082.jpg
Notes: 9242077 Spider love (2 of 6 today): The female Sheet Web Spider (Linyphiidae?) wants more, but the male - with palps in the air - has to recharge from its "sperm web". (See my [Previous] photo.) They repeated this "ritual of love" several times. The female was passive during the act, but she became aggresive as the male recharged. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2005)

File.Name: 5116d_5116-091.jpg
Notes: 9242013 Spider love (3 of 6 today): Male Sheet Web Spider (Linyphiidae?) on his "sperm web" recharging his palps with genetic material aka sperm. All male spiders deposit tiny drops of sperm on a special "sperm web" and then pick it up with the specialized leg-like palps on their head to deliver to their lady. You can see this spider's palps above its head. It's dipping its screen-right palp to the web, and you can see some web-fluff on the left palp that might be already charged. It's remarkable that there is no physical connection between the male's testes and the palps with which he will deliver his sperm to his lady. Consummation happens when he intoduces this genetic package from palp into the female's epigyne - the process known as "sperm induction". (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2005)

File.Name: 5116e_5116-077.jpg
Notes:
9241881 Spider love (4 of 6 today): This male Sheet Web Spider (Linyphiidae?) went back to his "sperm web" to recharge his palps with his genetic material aka sperm. All male spiders deposit tiny drops of sperm on a special "sperm web" and then pick it up with their palps to deliver it to their lady. In the photo you can see the swollen tips of his palps that will deliver the goods. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2005)

This is my favorite photo today, but it`s a sequence, so here it is in the middle.

File.Name: 5116f_5116-093.jpg
Notes: 9241787 Spider love (5 of 6 today): The male Sheet Web Spider (Linyphiidae?) on the right is snuggling with the female on the left as he starts to approach. He is carrying his genetic material in the swollen palps on his head-end on screen-left. Look how they are starting to entwine their legs! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2005)

File.Name: 5116g_5116-092.jpg
Notes: 9241722 Spider love (6 of 6 today): The male Sheet Web Spider (Linyphiidae?) on the right is transfering sperm from the palps on his head to the female's receptive epigyne opening located at the thorax-end of her abdomen just below her hind legs. The bar code-like color patterns are reflections from the trembling web during this long exposure. It's a tender moment. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2005)

File.Name: 5117a_5116-066.jpg
Notes: 9247246 The seasons move along like a giant unstoppable machine. It's still winter by the calendar, but days are getting longer and it's starting to feel like spring. These are delicate white flowers of Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica). This is our very first herbaceous wildflower of the new year at home. Look close and you can see that it has four long stamens and two shorter ones, like all members of the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family. We've had generous rain. I hope it will be a great wildflower year! (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2005)

File.Name: 5117b_5116-074.jpg
Notes: 9247198 Looking under the cap of a new Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus or kin) mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. Store-bought Agaricus mushrooms have a membranous skin or "veil" that covers the gills before they open. All Cortinarius mushrooms start with a veil of cobwebby hairs that covers and protects the gills. Look close and you can see an opening in the veil turned rusty-brown from the falling spores. When they get older, you can recognize the spore-stained color on the stem. This species is distinctive for having an extra "slime veil" over the cap and gills that leaves slimy bands on the stem. Said to be edible, but not choice. I'd rather admire it. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2005)
ScientificName: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Agaricus; Cortinarius
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Agaricus; Cortinarius

File.Name: 5117c_5115-017.jpg
Notes: 9247116 I still mostly find old soggy Bolete and Lactarius mushrooms in the woods after our heavy rains that ended a week ago. New mushrooms are just starting to appear as "mushrumps" pushing up through the oak leaves in the woods. I thought this was an edible Blewit (Clitocybe nuda), but it's definately a Cortinarius - maybe Cortinarius varius or kin? It has a viscid violet-tinged cap and a dry stem without a bulbous base. Maybe edible, definately pretty. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2005)
ScientificName: Bolete; Lactarius; Clitocybe nuda; Cortinarius; Cortinarius varius
associatedTaxa: Bolete; Lactarius; Clitocybe nuda; Cortinarius; Cortinarius varius

File.Name: 5117d_5115-030.jpg
Notes: 9247020 I guess this is Short-Stemmed Russula (Russula brevipes or kin) mushroom in the woods. The "Russula test" is to grab the stem and break it. If it snaps like a piece of chalk then bingo! The alternating short and long gills are distinctive - and beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2005)

File.Name: 5117e_5115-037.jpg
Notes: 9246964 Brilliant new Russula emetica (or kin) mushroom pushing up through the oak leaves in the woods. Mushrooms drop spores. They don't need to attract insects to be pollinated like flowers - so why are they sometimes so beautiful? (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2005)

File.Name: 5117f_5116-059.jpg
Notes:
9246933 Spider love is all about making spiderlings. (See my [Previous] photos.) Different spiders made these progeny. I don't recognize this web that is slung like a bridge between two branches a few feet apart with hundreds of these pretty critters. Is it a special web Mom makes for her spiderlings?

It's sobering to think how few of these will survive. Every adult spider needs to produce one breeding offspring in its lifetime to maintain the population. Nature is tough. Insects are scarce in the woods now in mid-winter - except for all those tiny Dance Flies and Fungus Gnats that dance in the sunbeams. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2005)

File.Name: 5117h_5115-035.jpg
Notes: 10648211 Solitary Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola or kin) mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The edge of the cap turned yellow when I rubbed it. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2005)

File.Name: 5121a_5121-208.jpg
Notes: 9276084 Pretty flowers of Henbit aka Giraffe's Head (Lamium amplexicaule) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. It's an ornamental weed from Eurasia that is becoming part of our woodlands. That's bad. But I don't get too upset about this alien that mostly behaves like a native. I guess that's my own personal triage with so many other nasty invading species. The scarlet caps of the new buds are beautiful. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 January 2005)

File.Name: 5121b_5121-204.jpg
Notes: 9276080 Tiny "Little Brown Mushrooms" with decurrent gills that grow down the stalk, maybe Clitocybe? It`s translucent in the late afternoon sunlight - like a lens - growing in the micro-jungle of new seedlings on the open ground in the woods. Note the bend in the stem. Maybe a deer or mouse (or photographer) walked by while it was growing? (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 January 2005)

File.Name: 5122a_5122-011.jpg
Notes: 10648176 These look like little mushrooms, but fungi are usually every color but green. These tiny Liverworts (Marchantia?) look like little palm trees. They are growing with mosses and ferns on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2005)

File.Name: 5122b_5122-014.jpg
Notes: 9281472 Yipes - the oak leaves have teeth! This is a Cup Fungus (maybe Peziza badia or kin?) growing up through the fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves along my trail up the creek. Some mushrooms have brilliant colors that attract attention, but this one is perfectly camouflaged. I don't get it. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2005)

File.Name: 5122c_5122-016.jpg
Notes: 9281435 First new flowers of deciduous Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2005)

File.Name: 5122d_5122-001.jpg
Notes: 9281412 "Me and my shadow... " I chased this Flower Fly (Syrphidae) from sunbeam to sunbeam. Then I realized it was repeating a path like a dragonfly, and I got the photo when it came back. I have no idea why this wasp-like syrphid fly is active at this time of year. Maybe they over-winter as adults (like house flies) and the warm dry weather since the deluge woke it up for a day? (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2005)

File.Name: 5122e_5122-013.jpg
Notes:
9281367 More tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - these happen to be white with beautiful textured caps, growing from under a decomposing oak branch in perpetual shade in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2005)

I also found this mushroom last year, compare:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=5486480

File.Name: 5122f_5122-021.jpg
Notes: 9281328 I think my monopod sank into a gopher hole just as I took this photo of an Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae) on an oak leaf, but sometimes the f*ups are keepers. You can still make out three body parts, the right number of legs, antennae up front. I can imagine this critter animated as a Miyazaki anime demon swirling as it moves. That's probably just how it looks to its prey! I was surprised to find it at this time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2005)

File.Name: 5123a_5121-203.jpg
Notes:
9287508 Tiny "Little Brown Mushrooms" with decurrent gills that grow down the stalk, maybe Clitocybe? They are translucent in the late afternoon sunlight - catching light like a lens focused down the stem. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 January 2005)

I was too busy today to get many photos, but I found new things in yesterday's photos. I love these tiny details I would never notice without my camera. It's one of those many things "I believe but can't prove" that nature and history and culture are all driven by tiny events we don't notice at he time - but they cascade from cause to effect to make big changes that effect everything.

It's on my mind. Edge put the question "What do you believe even though you cannot prove it?" to 120 leading intellectuals and scientists etc. The results are fairly short and downright provocative. Check out:
   http://www.edge.org/q2005/q05_easyprint.html

As the Grateful Dead sing, "We are the eyes of the world".

File.Name: 5123b_5123-031.jpg
Notes: 9287342 I'm really intrigued by these tiny Dance Flies and Fungus Gnats (etc.) that dance in the sunbeams in the woods. I'm still working to find the right exposure to capture them. This shot is F/32 @ 1/2 second @ ISO 200, with tripod and manual exposure and focus. I think I need a ND filter to get longer exposure times and trails. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2005)

File.Name: 5123c_5122-012.jpg
Notes:
9287240 New flower buds of native Tauschia arguta in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family. I took the photo to document the date. (I take lots of those photos.) Then I noticed that the flowers really are flowering, with tiny female twin-stigmas in the air. Male flowers appear first on most flowers - it's called "protandry" - so it's interesting to find flowers that swing the other way with "protogyny" - females first. A quick web search suggests this is a feature of the Apiaceae family. These "little differences" make the world go round. I never would have noticed this without my camera. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2005)

I'm getting spooked by looking close at these photos today. Is that gray thing a leaf or a caterpillar?!

File.Name: 5123d_5121-183.jpg
Notes:
9287038 You have to look very close at the center of the flower to appreciate this photo. I took some photos of this new Popcorn Flower (Plagiobothrys or Cryptantha in the Borage (Boraginaceae) family). Clicking in close on my computer, I noticed the tiny critter (maybe a Springtail (Collembola?) crawling out of the flower's throat. It's bright orange with dark eyes and long antennae. I never would have noticed it without my camera. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 January 2005)

Do these tiny critters matter?!

File.Name: 5123e_5121-209.jpg
Notes: 9286949 Tiny insect cigar-shaped Thrips (order Thysanoptera) marching across the large fertile pistils of this native California Peony (Paeonia californica) flower. I didn't notice them when I took the photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 January 2005)
CommonNames: Thrips (order Thysanoptera) marching across the large fertile pistils of this native California Peony

File.Name: 5123f_5116-061.jpg
Notes: 9286887 Unrolling leaflet of a new frond of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) in the woods. I was surprised that the spore-bearing sporangia are already in place. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2005)

File.Name: 5128a_5128-135.jpg
Notes: 9326860 First flowers of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the woods. So elegant - just two stamens and that serpent-tongue style to do the work of pollination. Compare the [Previous] photo. Hummingbirds visit these flowers, but I think they are engineered for large bees that will clasp the lower petal to get the sweet nectar and lever the flower`s sexual parts down on their back to deposit and receive pollen. It`s an ancient ritual that every flower performs in its own way. (Santa Ynez Valley, 28 January 2005)

File.Name: 5128b_5128-136.jpg
Notes: 9326854 This native California Peony (Paeonia californica) flower looks so primative - and maybe it is. It's in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family. This is the most ancient flowering plant family, with many male stamens and separate female carpels. Compare the [Next] photo to appreciate how flowers have evolved from clumsy to elegant. On the other hand, here it is. It's a simple plan for a flower that still works. (Santa Ynez Valley, 28 January 2005)

File.Name: 5128c_5128-115.jpg
Notes: 9326816 First flowers of Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata), with rain drops. The flowers come from those distinctive round umbrella-like leaves, but early leaves have a different shape. The leaves AND flowers make a tasty salad. It does well in the garden. Harvest a few leaves rather than the whole plants so they will keep producing! (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 January 2005)

File.Name: 5128d_5127-122.jpg
Notes: 9326784 These little raindrops are natural magnifying lenses on the hairs of a leaf of Lindley's Lupine (Lupinus bicolor). We have many different lupines. This one is small and kind of weedy, but it catches raindrops just like its more glamorous kin. Something about those lupine leaves... (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 January 2005)

File.Name: 5128e_5128-134.jpg
Notes: 9326745 A glimpse of the lichen micro-world on a twig of our native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) shrub. Lichens are little self-sufficient communities of algae and fungi that grow as one in a biological marriage. These orange caps look like little Ascomycete Cup Fungi - and they are probably a "fling" for the fungus partner making a few spores on its own without its algal partner. Why do some trees and shrubs always support more lichens than others? (Santa Ynez Valley, 28 January 2005)

File.Name: 5128f_5128-141.jpg
Notes: 9326634 Tiny "Little Brown Mushrooms" growing up among the new seedlings of our future spring wildflowers along the trail. (Santa Ynez Valley, 28 January 2005)

File.Name: 5129a_5129-010.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 10748765 Oak leaf spines and mushroom curves - maybe the mushroom is Ribbed Elfin Saucer (Helvella queletii). (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2005)

File.Name: 5129b_5129-026.jpg
Notes: 9334940 First flowers of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the canyon. The perfect urn-shaped flowers remind me of those old-time Easter sugar eggs that you peer inside to see something wonderful. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2005)

File.Name: 5129c_5129-006.jpg
Notes: 9334876 Beautiful leaf-colored Elfin Saddle (Helvella sp.) mushrooms in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. It has a fluted stalk like the Helvella lacunosa in the [Previous] photo. Maybe it's the Ribbed Elfin Saucer (Helvella queletii), though the cap is not dark. When I first saw it from above, I thought it was a Cup Fungus (Peziza). But it has a stem and the fertile surface is on the outside of the cup. Whatever - it sure is pretty with the sun behind! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2005)
CommonNames: Elfin Saddle; Coast Live Oak; Ribbed Elfin Saucer; Cup Fungus
ScientificName: Helvella; Quercus agrifolia; Helvella lacunosa; Helvella queletii; Peziza
associatedTaxa: Helvella; Quercus agrifolia; Helvella lacunosa; Helvella queletii; Peziza

File.Name: 5129d_5129-032.jpg
Notes: 9334762 Strange Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa) mushroom in the woods. I usually call it the "Brain Mushroom" because that`s what it looks like. The brain-like cap and deeply fluted stalk are distinctive. Said to be edible, but I haven't tried it. I reckon nature needs all its brains to survive the human onslaught. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2005)

File.Name: 5129e_5129-008.jpg
Notes: 9334709 New frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens). The edible new fronds are usually called "fiddleheads". This looks more like a gnarly talon clutching the seeds of life. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2005)

File.Name: 5129f_5129-013.jpg
Notes: 9334681 It almost feels like spring here in central California. The willows are starting to flower along the creek, but only on the very tip-tops of the trees. I found this female pistillate flower catkin on the ground. It's easy to recognize a willow, but tough to figure species. I guess this is Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis). (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2005)

File.Name: 5129g_5129-019.jpg
Notes: 9334630 A new yearling sprout of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the canyon today. "Many are called but few are chosen." These seedlings take years to mature. From the dozen new seedlings that came from hundreds of seeds, I hope one will mature and flower to repeat the cycle. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2005)

File.Name: 5130a_5130-033.jpg
Notes:
9342856 This tiny LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - happens to be blushing red. Maybe it's Mycena acicula (or kin). You can judge the size by the spines on the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the foreground, each about 1 mm long. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2005)

It's an "in between" time in our woods, like that pause before talking. It's warm, the ground is damp, and everything is growing after the huge rains this month, but I didn't find many flowers or mushrooms this weekend. Maybe nature is being smart. After our last El Nino rains, we had a foot of March snow!

File.Name: 5130b_5130-043.jpg
Notes: 9342748 Strange Early Morel (Verpa bohemica or kin) mushroom in the woods under California Bay (Umbellularia californica) and Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The fertile cap is attached only at the top, and it shrivels up like the wicked witch in Oz. (See [Previous] photo.) The pretty stem is hollow and falls apart when you try to pick it. Said to be edible, but my mushroom book says ominously: "When eaten in large amounts (or on several successive days), it can produce a loss of muscular coordination." No thanks - I know easier ways to do that! (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2005)
CommonNames: Early Morel; California Bay; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Verpa bohemica; Umbellularia californica; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Verpa bohemica; Umbellularia californica; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 5130c_5130-040.jpg
Notes: 9342692 Nature really does have a sense of humor! This kind of looks like a sea slug lost in the woods. It`s really an old Early Morel (Verpa bohemica or kin) mushroom. See my [Next] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2005)

File.Name: 5130d_5129-001.jpg
Notes: 9342646 Strange Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus or kin) "mushrooms" - fungi at least - growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). They look like they're walking through the woods! They start out round like a puffball. The outer rind splits and curves backwards into rays that actually lift the fruiting body from the ground. The thin white inner sac soon releases its spores and vanishes, though you can see some remnants here. They must open fast. I always find the old rinds with no connection to the earth, as though they really did walk here. As the species name suggests, they are natural hygrometers that open and close with changes in humidity. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2005)

File.Name: 5130e_5130-045.jpg
Notes:
9342553 New flowers of Sour Grass aka Bermuda Buttercup (Oxalis pes-caprae). Maybe those lines on the flowers are "honey guides" that lead insects to the sweet nectar. They have clover-like leaves and a pleasant tart taste. The flowers are tasty in a salad, but they contain oxalic acid that is cumulative and toxic in quantity, so don`t eat too many! (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2005)

Pretty flowers, but it`s another one of those invasive weeds trying to take over our wildlands. We`re doing our best to get rid of it up here in the mountains. These will soon be gone. MisterGoleta has another photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/mistergoleta/?pid=10749048

File.Name: 5130f_5130-046.jpg
Notes: 9342389 First flowers of California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) in the canyon. The leaves have an intense Bay Laurel taste and are useful for cooking if you don't overdo it. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2005)

File.Name: 5131a_5131-075.jpg
Notes:
9355189 First new flowers of Common Fiddleneck (Amsinkia menziesii var. intermedia). It's native but kind of weedy. This is a classic "scorpioid inflorescence" - the flower stalk is curled up like a scorpion`s tail or a fiddle's neck, with new flowers opening as the stem unrolls and seeds developing below. (Santa Ynez Valley, 31 January 2005)

Today's photos are all from the woodland near the "dam view" at Lake Cachuma. Days are getting longer and the sun is getting higher after the solstice, and sometimes I have time and light to stop after school. It feels like spring!

File.Name: 5131b_5131-065.jpg
Notes: 9355001 Looking into a new flower of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum Xanti). It's the solanum way for the male stamens to clutch the female pistil all around. This population of shrubs near Lake Cachuma is interesting for approaching the endemic var. hoffmanii with nearly glabrous stems. Look close at the top of the stamens to see a tiny cigar-shaped Thrip insect in the order Thysanoptera. I love these tiny critters I only notice after taking my photos. (Santa Ynez Valley, 31 January 2005)
ScientificName: Solanum Xanti).  It's the solanum way for the male stamens to clutch the female pistil all around.  This population of shrubs near Lake Cachuma is interesting for approaching the endemic var. hoffmanii; Thysanoptera
associatedTaxa: Solanum Xanti).  It's the solanum way for the male stamens to clutch the female pistil all around.  This population of shrubs near Lake Cachuma is interesting for approaching the endemic var. hoffmanii; Thysanoptera

File.Name: 5131c_5131-071.jpg
Notes: 9354816 New flowers of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia). The leaves of this shrub might look like just another scrub oak as you hike past, but the flowers and bright red berries are something else. Be a naturalist and notice the differences! (Santa Ynez Valley, 31 January 2005)

File.Name: 5131d_5131-061.jpg
Notes: 9354789 New flowers of Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus). It's a common native shrub with opposite leaves and gazillions of tiny white flowers that we usually only notice "en masse" like snow on the mountains. But the individual flowers are so beautiful and intricate and sexy up close. Yellow stamens, purple pistlis, and that sweet-looking doughnut-shaped nectar gland at the base to entice pollinators. (But what pollinators?? Insects are scarce this early in the growing season!) (Santa Ynez Valley, 31 January 2005)

File.Name: 5203a_5203-128.jpg
Notes: 9382365 Looking close at a new flower of Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) in the woods near Lake Cachuma. This is a primrose relative in the Primulaceae family. We all learn about sepals and petals and stamens and pistils in school. But it's astounding how the pattern changes from flower to flower with infinite variety within the limited rules we call "plant families". This is what got Darwin thinking. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 February 2005)

File.Name: 5203b_5203-118.jpg
Notes:
9382269 New flowers of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora). The backlight lets you see through the skirts for a glimpse of "the goodies" hidden inside - stamens and pistil. The flowers have tiny yellow petals below that look inviting. I wonder how pollination happens.

This plant is a "green root parasite" in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family, related to Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja). You usually have to crawl under the chaparral to find it, but I was lucky to find this specimen today along a trail near Lake Cachuma. See [Previous] photos for more info. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 February 2005)

File.Name: 5203c_5203-121.jpg
Notes: 9382190 Brilliant red leafy bracts of Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora). You can see some developing flowers down under. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 February 2005)

File.Name: 5203d_5203-135.jpg
Notes: 9382169 New plants of Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora). This interesting mushroom-like native in the Figwort Family (Scrophulariaceae) is a root-parasite of chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in this place. It has some green, but the foliage is mostly red. I reckon it's a three-way relationship between plant-fungus-shrub. I wonder if every partner benefits? Click [Next] for closer photos. The lily-like leaves in the background are native Soap Plant (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) with wavy leaves. See [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 February 2005)
CommonNames: Indian Warrior; Figwort Family; Chamise; Soap Plant
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Scrophulariaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Chlorogalum pomeridianum
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Scrophulariaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Chlorogalum pomeridianum

File.Name: 5203e_5203-140.jpg
Notes: 9382114 Wavy new leaves of native Soap Plant (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) with new grass. It's a spring bulb in the Lily (Liliaceae) family. Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) is another lily-relative coming up now with straight leaves. As the names suggest, distinguishing them was an important lesson for native Americans kids. There's another view in the [Next] photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 February 2005)
CommonNames: Soap Plant; Lily; Chaparral Death Camas

File.Name: 5203f_5203-123.jpg
Notes: 9382049 New California Buttercups (Ranunculus californicus) in the afternoon light. Yellow is the hardest color to get right with a digital camera. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 February 2005)

File.Name: 5205a_5205-010.jpg
Notes: 9688754 Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus) - or some close kin in the Vespidae family. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2005)

File.Name: 5205b_5205-003.jpg
Notes:
9396314 "Catenaries and dirigibles..."

The catenary is the mathematical curve of the spider web from the top of the flower - or any suspended string or bridge cable. The dirigible (more like a hot-air balloon - but close enough) is the first Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii) flower in our canyon on the north-slope of the mountains. It feels like spring. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2005)

"Catenaries and dirigibles" is a line from Joanna Newsom's song "Bridges and Balloons" on her album "The Milk-Eyed Mender". It has the chorus
   O it was a funny little thing
   to be the ones to've seen.

That seems a good anthem for us photographers who HAVE seen, and share with others what we see. Recommended. (But she might be an acquired taste!)

File.Name: 5205c_5205-007.jpg
Notes: 9396242 Looks like a studio shot, but it's just a humble California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) with morning light in the woods at home. The flowers are just starting to appear. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2005)

File.Name: 5205d_5205-018.jpg
Notes: 9396224 First flower of native Wild SweetPea (Lathyrus latifolius) showing its goodies. I reckon it's has been open for a while. Stamens and pistil start out hidden in the magenta-tipped "keel" at the bottom of the pea-shaped flower. They only stand up like this at the end of their time, maybe to allow the flower to self-pollinate if that's the only action in town that day. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2005)

File.Name: 5205e_5205-016.jpg
Notes:
9396146 "Are you looking at me?"

Yes I was, and this Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae?) insect seemed to be looking back at me. This critter is resting on a branch of flowering Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) in the canyon. It's spooky to make eye contact with an insect! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2005)

File.Name: 5205f_5205-005.jpg
Notes: 9396094 I guess this is a little black Bumblebee (Bombus) working a flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii). It looks perfectly fitted to the flower in this photo, with abdomen touching pistil ready to deliver pollen. But look again. This bee is taking a shortcut to the nectar. The white "petticoats" are the real petals, and the red "skirts" are the petal-like sepals. I watched this bee for a while, and sometimes it took the shortcut and sometimes took the direct path. I'm still not sure how it transfers pollen. There was another black and yellow bumblebee working the same flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2005)

File.Name: 5205g_5205-005.jpg
Notes: 9396017 I reckon this is an early Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus) - or some close kin in the Vespidae family. This wasp is still kind of groggy in midwinter, so it's tracking the sun by hugging a new leaf of native Fiesta Flower (Pholistoma auritum), and it let me get close. Antenna and fore-foot are in such intimate contact with the leaf hairs. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2005)

File.Name: 5206a_5131-082.jpg
Notes:
9401288 New seeds of native Lacepod or Fringepod (Thysanocarpus curvipes) in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family, catching perfect backlight as the sun gets low. They look like little Julia Set fractals. (Santa Ynez Valley, 31 January 2005)

Gloomy day - just as well, I have work to do. Today's photos are good ones from last week in the woods near Lake Cachuma.

File.Name: 5206b_5131-074.jpg
Notes: 9401227 Developing seeds of these native Lacepod or Fringepod (Thysanocarpus curvipes) mustards are made for afternoon backlight with the sun behind! (Santa Ynez Valley, 31 January 2005)

File.Name: 5206c_5121-190.jpg
Notes: 9401195 Tiny new flowers of native Lacepod or Fringepod (Thysanocarpus curvipes). It's in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family, with four petals and six stamens - four long and two short, just like every other mustard. New flowers fade and become seedpods as the stalk keeps growing with more new flowers on top rising above the old below. I guess life is like that - though I'd rather think of myself as ripe! (Santa Ynez Valley, 31 January 2005)

File.Name: 5206d_5203-137.jpg
Notes: 9401117 New flowers of native Poison Sanicle (Sanicula bipinnata). The common name sounds scary, but the leaves taste just like Cilantro, and I've been munching them for years. We have many different Sanicle plants in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family. All plants in this family have flowers in umbrella-like umbels. All our Sanicles have distinctive round umbel-heads, easy to recognize once you know them. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 February 2005)

File.Name: 5206e_5131-056.jpg
Notes: 9401043 New flowers of Wild Cucumber aka California Manroot (Marah fabaceus) - called "manroot" because it grows from a huge underground tuber that can get at least child size. It's a vine that clambers over native shrubs and dies back in winter. The flowers have a frosted but immature look. (Santa Ynez Valley, 31 January 2005)

File.Name: 5206f_5203-141.jpg
Notes: 9400972 New flowers of Windmill Pink (Silene gallica) in the Pink (Caryophyllaceae) family. It's a widespread weed from Europe, but it's not very invasive and rather pretty up close. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 February 2005)

File.Name: 5207a_5207-025.jpg
Notes: [bugsncritters] 9702013 A herd of tiny yellow Springtail (Collembola) insects on a new flower of native Baby Blue-Eyes (Nemophila menziesii var. integrifolia) in the woods. (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 February 2005)

File.Name: 5207b_5207-022.jpg
Notes: 9407936 New leaflets of California Maiden-Hair Fern (Adiantum jordanii) growing in the woodland near Lake Cachuma. Spores will develop under the recurved edges of the leaflets that are plainly visible with this backlight. The stems feel polished, like metal. You can wear them if you have pierced ears! This woodland species is different from the other maidenhair ferns that grow along creeks and waterfalls. (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 February 2005)

File.Name: 5207c_5207-035.jpg
Notes:
9407890 New flower of native Baby Blue-Eyes (Nemophila menziesii var. integrifolia) in the woods near Lake Cachuma. A herd of tiny yellow Springtail (Collembola) insects are hopping away. Sorry I crashed the party! (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 February 2005)

File.Name: 5207d_5207-034.jpg
Notes: 9407861 New flower of Red Maids (Calandrinia ciliata) hiding in the spring growth along my trail in the woods. It's a beautiful native flower with slightly succulent leaves in the Purslane (Portulacaceae) family. It's a bit weedy, and favors disturbed places like the margin of this trail. Look close where the petals come together under the stamens to spot a pretty yellow Springtail (Collembola) insect. These tiny insects are everywhere, but I usually only notice them in close photos after the fact. See my [Next] photo for more. (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 February 2005)

File.Name: 5207e_5207-024.jpg
Notes:
9407787 New flower of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora). It's displaying its ripe red-tipped female pistil out the end, and you can follow the style back along the top of the backlit flower. Those dark shadows inside are hidden male stamens. I don't know how pollination happens. (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 February 2005)

This beautiful plant is a "green root parasite" in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family, related to Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja). You usually have to crawl under the chaparral to maybe find it by luck. I was lucky to find this colony along a secret trail near Lake Cachuma.

I have more photos of this flower and info at
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=9382269
and [Previous] to that.

File.Name: 5207f_5207-042.jpg
Notes: 9407712 First flower bud of Parry's Larkspur (Delphinium parryi ssp. maritimum) in the woods near Lake Cachuma. It's ready to open - like it will blast off its stalk and fly away! A few flowers were already open in the woods, but I have to follow the afternoon sunbeams for photos. Note the spider webs. There are new webs everywhere, but few insects. (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 February 2005)

File.Name: 5207g_5207-022.jpg
Notes: 9407667 New flower of Johnny-Jump-Up (Viola pedunculata). These bright native wildflowers with leonine markings have secrets. Follow a single flower stem down to the base and pinch it off and chew on it. It tastes like wintergreen! Another secret is hidden in the throat of the flower. I'm still trying to get that photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 February 2005)

File.Name: 5217a_5210-093.jpg
Notes:
9467657 First flowers of Woolly Paint Brush (Castilleja foliosa) growing on a sandstone outcrop. The bright red "petals" are colorful leafy bracts that enclose the slender yellow-green flowers that are visible here. This was a gloomy day, just right to bring out the color in a long exposure. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2005)

I missed fotolog this last week. I was ready to post today's photos a week ago, and it's still a struggle tonight. Oh well, st*ff happens, and there's always a another chance for a new photo.

File.Name: 5217b_5210-092.jpg
Notes: 9467608 New flowers of Woolly Paint Brush (Castilleja foliosa) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. It was drizzling, just right to bring out the colors. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2005)

File.Name: 5217c_5210-087.jpg
Notes: 9467036 I've never seen so many native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) flowers in one place. This interesting native is related to Penstemon and Paintbrush - all in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. It's a root-parasite of chaparral shrubs, probably Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa) in this place, though I also find it under other shrubs. It has some green, but the foliage is mostly red. I reckon it's a three-way relationship between plant-fungus-shrub. I wonder if every partner benefits? (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2005)
CommonNames: Indian Warrior; Penstemon; Paintbrush; Figwort; Eastwood Manzanita

File.Name: 5217d_5210-084.jpg
Notes: 9466894 New red-capped Russula emetica (or kin) mushroom pushing up a large "mushrump" in the oak leaves in the woods. It's pretty, but poisonous, as the species name suggests. It has a not-quite-pleasant hot acrid taste if you nibble the cap. Despite all the rain this year, I'm finding few new mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2005)

File.Name: 5217e_5210-082.jpg
Notes: 9466814 Mutant carrots from Farmer Jacob's organic garden near school. Jacob is a former student from many years ago. (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 February 2005)

File.Name: 5217f_5207-026.jpg
Notes: 9466685 New leaflets of California Maiden-Hair Fern (Adiantum jordanii) growing in the woodland near Lake Cachuma. New spores will develop under the recurved edges of the leaflets that are plainly visible with this backlight. I'll bet phi and the Fibonacci numbers are involved. (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 February 2005)

File.Name: 5218a_5212-005.jpg
Notes:
9475179 Tiny beetle (Coleoptera) feeling its way around a new Peach Tree (Prunus persica) flower in the garden. (D'oh - not apricot!) It's a big world for this little critter! (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2005)

I was ready to post today's photos a week ago, but there were server problems. Oh well, st*ff happens. It was raining then and it's raining now. There's always a another chance for new photos tomorrow!

File.Name: 5218b_5212-002.jpg
Notes: 9475074 Little raindrops strung like pearls on an Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) spider web in the woods. They are really more like mist-drops - really tiny. Each drop is a reflection of the sky - but in this small photo, you can only see the reflection in the largest drop in the top right. Here is another physics stumper. Why do the tiny mist-drops stay separate, even strung along the almost vertical webs? Shouldn't they all run together and fall away? Is it the sticky web or electric/static charge between the drops? (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2005)
ScientificName: Araneidae) spider web in the woods.  They are really more like mist-drops
associatedTaxa: Araneidae) spider web in the woods.  They are really more like mist-drops

File.Name: 5218c_5212-008.jpg
Notes: 9474930 Raindrops hanging on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the woods. I think I see myself (upside down) taking the photo in the reflection of every drop. The starburst patterns are natural - at least they were in the original photo and not "photoshopped" in during post-production. Are they a reflection of the diaphram blades in my lens? (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2005)

File.Name: 5218d_5212-014.jpg
Notes: 9474836 Nothing catches raindrops like a lupine! This is a leaf of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the canyon. The drops are beautiful. Sure it's just reflection and refraction, but there are stumpers here. Why do some drops magnify the hairs inside but others reflect the world outside? Why are there two suns reflected in the drop in the lower left, but just one sun in the other big drops? Why do the lupines catch and hold raindrops so perfectly? Why is nature so beautiful? (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2005)

File.Name: 5218e_5212-027.jpg
Notes: 9474732 Reflections in a raindrop caught on the hairy new leaves of this little Lupine (maybe Lupinus nanus?) in the woods after a rain. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2005)

File.Name: 5218f_5212-021.jpg
Notes: 9474642 Tiny flower and buds of Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata), with rain drops. The flowers come from these distinctive round umbrella-like leaves, but early leaves are different. The leaves AND flowers make a tasty salad. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2005)

File.Name: 5219a_5219-005.jpg
Notes: 9481756 Raindrops on "Old Man Cactus" (Opuntia - many species) spines in the garden this morning on a rainy day. Hey, what do you mean "old"?! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2005)

File.Name: 5219b_5219-012.jpg
Notes:
9481534 It was drizzling and windy, but I took my camera out of the bag to capture this tiny fly resting on a male staminate flower catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) down by the creek. I used my tripod, but it's not much good when everything is blowing in the wind! This fly is in the sub-order Nematocera that includes mosquitoes, gnats, crane flies, and many more. Maybe this is one of those tiny flies that dance in the sunbeams? Look - its head is all eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2005)

I cheated and flipped the photo 90 degrees clockwise to put the fly on top. Tilt your head right, and the way the fly is clasping the flowers with its legs will make more sense.

File.Name: 5219c_5219-016.jpg
Notes: 9481395 I always hope to be surprised by what I find when I look close in the woods, as I hike my trails again and again. These tiny white mushrooms under live oak and madrone leaves look like pom-poms or folded headphones or earmuffs. They have a texture like crystalline sugar candy. Maybe they are new White Elfin Saddle (Helvella crispa) or kin? I'll check back as they mature. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2005)

File.Name: 5219d_5219-001.jpg
Notes: 9481318 A delicate Inky Cap (maybe Coprinus lagopus?) mushroom in the woods. The margin is turning up and in, splitting the cap into wedges along the gills. You can see the cap starting to "deliquesce" or auto-digest itself into black "ink" in the top-right. We've had a few days of rain and drizzle. This is one of the few new mushrooms to appear. Mushrooms are the fruiting bodies of fungi with the job of dispersing tiny spores. Somehow they know that rain is not good for releasing spores, and they wait. I expect more mushrooms next week as the stormy weather clears. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2005)

File.Name: 5219e_5219-004.jpg
Notes: 9481234 Candy Cap (Lactarius camphoratus or kin) mushrooms are coming up on the native side of our garden. They have a sweet spicy smell and taste. Despite much rain this year - or maybe because of it? - mushroms are scarce. I'm still waiting to collect a basket full of Chanterelles - or any mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2005)

File.Name: 5219f_5212-035.jpg
Notes:
9481056 A conference of three kingdoms - the mushroom might be Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila) under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees; the green plant is Bedstraw (Galium aparine); look close to find the tiny insect waving it's antennae in a "V" for victory salute on top of the highest green leaf! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2005)

Biologists make the rules, but sometimes the partitions are wrong. Bedstraw "should" have whorls of 4 or 8 leaves. I count 6 and 7 in this photo.
CommonNames: Oak-Loving Collybia; Coast Live Oak; Bedstraw
ScientificName: Collybia dryophila; Quercus agrifolia; Galium aparine
associatedTaxa: Collybia dryophila; Quercus agrifolia; Galium aparine

Notes: [bugsncritters] 9787300 Tiny fly resting on a male staminate flower catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) down by the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2005)

File.Name: 5220f_5205-012.jpg
Notes: 9487900 I call these dull Lepidoptera "Rain Moths" - I see them by the dozens or hundreds after every rain. This one got snared in a Sheet Web Spider (Linyphiidae?) web. I spooked it with my camera and it broke free. Isn't that a basic rule of physics that observing an event changes it? (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2005)

File.Name: 5221a_5221-044.jpg
Notes:
9497225 New White Russula (Russula albidula or kin) mushroom pushing up through the soggy oak leaves in the woods today. Look very close and you might notice the tiny Springtail (Collembola) insect with antennae on the upper right of the cap margin and the very tiny mushroom on the stick below the center. I'm sure I'm missing most of the action! (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2005)

"Oh the wind and the rain" - we had 5 inches of rain last night, and almost 10" for the weekend. All is well. I hiked with an umbrella today and got these new photos. I'm running out of ideas for rain shots, but the mushrooms and critters keep surprising me!

File.Name: 5221b_5221-022.jpg
Notes: 9497119 I was trying to get a photo of this tiny LBM - "Little Brown Mushroom" - maybe Psathyrella with dark spores? I didn't notice the tiny snail at the bottom until I got back to my computer and looked closer. I guess it's a native Land Snail (Gastropoda) with a flat shell. It is NOT one of those big common intoduced pesky Garden Snails with a vertical shell that are so common at school. That's just about all I know about our local snails. Pleased to meet you Mr/Ms land snail - please don't eat our garden! (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2005)

File.Name: 5221c_5221-026.jpg
Notes: 9496970 First caps of "Clustered Woodlover" (Naematoloma fasciculare) mushrooms that I've seen in the canyon, growing on dead wood of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and coming up through the oak leaves. The mushrooms are pretty and poisonous - also very bitter, you couldn't eat much! Note that little fly on the edge of the cap. I guess our tastes differ. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2005)

File.Name: 5221d_5221-027.jpg
Notes: 9496841 I almost walked past this pretty little mushroom growing from a rotting oak log in the wood pile, surrounded by Polypore "Bracket fungi" - maybe Turkey Tail (Trametes versicolor aka Coriolus versicolor). Maybe this pretty mushroom is the edible Deer Mushroom (Pluteus cervina). I didn't collect it for a spore print, but the gills look a bit flesh/pink colored under the cap, which matches the species description. Maybe it is, maybe it isn't - but it sure is elegant! (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2005)
ScientificName: Polypore; Trametes versicolor; Coriolus versicolor; Pluteus cervina
associatedTaxa: Polypore; Trametes versicolor; Coriolus versicolor; Pluteus cervina

File.Name: 5221e_5221-025.jpg
Notes: 9496733 Green leaf of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Diplacus longiflorus) reaching out to Gilled Polypore (Lenzites betulina) mushrooms growing from a decaying oak log in the canyon. The mushrooms look like typical bracket or polypore fungi from above, but they have real gills instead of pores. Notice the slimy mass of green algae at the base. I think these woodland 'shrooms always grow with green algae. Are they partners? Is this how lichens start? (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2005)
ScientificName: Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Lenzites betulina
associatedTaxa: Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Lenzites betulina

We have perfect Banana Slug weather - it hasn`t stopped drizzling or pouring all day. It's too wet for mushrooms! No chance for new photos today, and I`m tired of rain shots. These photos from yesterday and the last week or two all have some color!

File.Name: 5222b_5211-096.jpg
Notes: 9503533 New flowers on a fruit tree at school, with rain drops. I think the tree is a Plum (Prunus). Beautiful flowers, tasty fruit. (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 February 2005)

File.Name: 5222c_5207-028.jpg
Notes: 9503477 First flowers of Parry's Larkspur (Delphinium parryi ssp. maritimum) in the woods near Lake Cachuma, with the sun behind. (Santa Ynez Valley, 7 February 2005)

File.Name: 5222d_5203-113.jpg
Notes: 9503459 Looking close at the "goodies" of a native Fiesta Flower (Pholistoma auritum) flower in the woods near Lake Cachuma. Looks unreal, but there it is. We take our photos day after day and look close and see what's there and try to capture it. As the Dead sing, "We are the eyes of the world" - capture the moment or it's gone forever. Didn't Hegel say that? (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 February 2005)

File.Name: 5222e_5212-011.jpg
Notes: 9503357 This darn Fuschia plant in the garden is still flowering after drought and deluge and frost and our dog Mojo! Other fuschias have failed. We're cloning this one with many cuttings. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2005)

File.Name: 5222f_5221-050.jpg
Notes:
9503295 Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) exploring his/her world. Actually it's trying to get away from the nosey photographer! (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2005)

Today is perfect Banana Slug weather. It hasn't stopped drizzling (or more) all day!

File.Name: 5223a_5223-063.jpg
Notes: 9511667 I shouldn't make these flowers look so beautiful - but they are. This is Asphodel or Onionweed (Asphodelus fistulosus) growing along the highway on my way to Santa Barbara today. It's pretty, but it's a federally listed noxious weed. I first noticed it in the 1970s when I started learning our native plants and couldn't find it in the Munz Flora. I've been watching it spread over the mountains ever since. Pretty, but not welcome. Don't encourage it. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 February 2005)

File.Name: 5223b_5223-056.jpg
Notes: 9511515 New fruit on native Lemonade Berry (Rhus integrifolia) growing on the lower south-facing slopes of our mountains. This native shrub in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) family grows on the beaches and up into the foothills. It has flat leaves, but in the mountains I find its kin Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) with folded "taco" leaves. Both shrubs have "sorta" edible fruit. The white coating is sweet/sour at first, but soon turns bitter. It's another "suck and spit" fruit, a great design for seed propagation! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 February 2005)

File.Name: 5223c_5223-057.jpg
Notes: 9511366 Waxy new flowers of native Lemonade Berry (Rhus integrifolia). See my [Next] photo for more info. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 February 2005)

File.Name: 5223d_5223-070.jpg
Notes: 9511315 Native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) coming up in the canyon. They remind me of those paper trees you make by rolling up newspapers and pushing them out. With luck or grace, this lily will grow 10 feet high with dozens of flowers to make seeds and nourish the swallowtail butterflies next summer. "Many are called but few are chosen." Deer and insect parasites and foolish neighbors all take their toll. I hope this lily will flower and mature to repeat the ancient cycle. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2005)

File.Name: 5223e_5223-065.jpg
Notes:
9511218 Strange and beautiful Slime Molds (Myxomycetes) starting to produce spore-bearing sporangia structures on a rotting Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) log in in the woods. Look close in the upper-right and see that the white sausage-like fruiting bodies are each supported by two hair-like stalks at the base. On the left you can see a new mass of fruiting bodies emerging from the gooey plasmodium, like something in a sci-fi/horror movie! I'll check back in a few days to see what they become. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2005)

These remarkable critters are part animal and part fungus. Single-celled amoeba-like protists live in decaying wood, but they sometimes get an "urge to merge" and stream together dissolving their cell-walls to form a multinucleate mass of protoplasm called a "plasmodium" that rebuilds itself as these mushroom-like structures that will release spores that will become more amoeba-like protists. It's a perfect strategy for microscopic single-celled organisms to move the relatively huge distance from one decaying log to another.

File.Name: 5223f_5223-066.jpg
Notes: 9510957 Pretty little mushshrooms growing from horse apples - maybe Stropharia or Panaeolus? In case you're wondering, they don't stain blue. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2005)

File.Name: 5224a_5224-104.jpg
Notes: 9517020 New flowers bursting from the bud of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the canyon. I've used this quote from Anais Nin before, but it's so perfect: "It takes courage to push yourself to places that you have never been before... to test your limits... to break through barriers. And the day came when the risk it took to remain tight inside the bud was more painful than the risk it took to blossom." (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2005)

File.Name: 5224b_5224-100.jpg
Notes: 9516981 New flower of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha). It's a climbing vine in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family, with many stamens and carpels. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2005)

File.Name: 5224c_5224-094.jpg
Notes: 9516944 Zeller's Bolete (Boletus zelleri) mushroom - growing from a crack in a sandstone outcrop! The Firesign Theatre once did a piece called "Everything You Know Is Wrong" - that's what I thought when I found this mushroom growing from a crack in a sandstone outcrop with moss and lichen. I think all our Boletes are mycorrhizal partners with trees. This one must be tapped into an oak's root growing through a crack in the rock? This photo in deep shade (1/2 sec. @ f/16) doesn't do the colors justice. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2005)

File.Name: 5224d_5224-092.jpg
Notes: 9516853 There were gazillions of these tiny LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - growing on wood scraps in a section of chaparral that was cleared for a fire break last summer. I don't know what they are. Those skinny green leaves on the right are the linear basal leaves of native Claytonia parviflora. The real Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) also has linear leaves, but not nearly so long or tapered at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2005)

File.Name: 5224e_5224-087.jpg
Notes: 9516795 New flower of "Red Maids" (Calandrinia breweri) growing with its kin "Miner's Lettuce" (Claytonia parviflora) on a two year old burn in our mountains. Both plants have slightly succulent leaves and are in the Purslane (Portulacaceae) family. There is another Red Maids (Calandrinia ciliata) that is kind of weedy and grows in the fields at school. There is also another more familiar Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) with fused upper leaves that approach teacup size. The plant in the photo is different, with smaller fused leaves and longer basal leaves. (See [Next] photo.) Both species in the photo grow mostly on burns. That's what biodiversity is all about. It's a fact of life if you look close to see it. We shouldn't take this for granted. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2005)
CommonNames: Red Maids; Miner's Lettuce; Purslane; Red Maids; Miner's Lettuce
ScientificName: Calandrinia breweri; Claytonia parviflora; Portulacaceae; Calandrinia ciliata; Claytonia perfoliata
associatedTaxa: Calandrinia breweri; Claytonia parviflora; Portulacaceae; Calandrinia ciliata; Claytonia perfoliata

File.Name: 5224f_5224-079.jpg
Notes: 9516599 New flowers of native Tauschia arguta in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family. It's sometimes called Southern Umbrellawort. That's a horrible common name, but its says something about how the flowers grow as "umbels on umbels", like fractal umbrellas on umbrellas, stalks on stalks. The dry summer seeds have an interesting caraway taste. (San Marcos Pass, 24 February 2005)

File.Name: 5225a_scale_barbie_m1--10.jpg
Notes:
9524231 Barbie meets Godzilla! I'm actually working on my school science fair project. Yeah I'm the teacher, but I always do my own project. I want to understand the scale of the macro photos I post here. Today I took a series of photos of Barbie and Godzilla at different magnifications, and then again of a centimeter ruler at the same magnifications. That green scale bar is actually a cleaned up image of the actual ruler.

This image is at 1:10 magnification with my 105mm Micro-Nikkor lens, about 49.5 inches from my camera sensor plane to Barbie's eye. My Nikon D70 camera sensor is 23.7mm x 15.6mm, so the scene should be 237mm x 156mm. I didn't crop this photo, but I did resize it to 500x332 pixels from the original 3008x2000. And how big is the image on your screen? It should be possible to measure the size of Godzilla'a teeth and the diameter of Barbie's hair by measuring the image on the screen or counting pixels. I'm not quite there yet - but the DMS science fair isn't until March 11. Yipes - I'll be busy for the next two weeks! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2005)

I'm really interested in the scale of my previous photos. But I can't find focus distance or magnification data in the jpeg image EXIF headers. Could it be hidden in a "makernote" field or somewhere else? That's a BIG omission if it's not there. Help!

File.Name: 5225b_scale_barbie_m1-1.jpg
Notes: 9524216 Here is Barbie at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my 105mm Micro-Nikkor lens. That means the image on my D70 camera 23.7mm x 15.6mm digital sensor is the same size as the original. The sensor captures the image in 3008 by 2000 pixels, so I can crunch the numbers to measure the original... I think I got the scale bar right! Click [Next] to see what I'm up to. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2005)

File.Name: 5225c_scale_barbie_m6-1.jpg
Notes:
9524167 Did you know the gleam in Barbie`s eye is heart-shaped and about 0.7mm wide? But what is that 1.0mm long green thing in the corner is that seems to be taking aim - Cupid's arrow?

Sorry it's so dusty. I didn't notice until seeing the photos. I guess that's the point. I know that green text color is too hard to read, I`ll change it - the scale bar is 2 mm wide. This photo is magnified 6 times from what appeared on my digital camera sensor and much more on your computer screen. Click [Next] to see what I`m up to. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2005)

File.Name: 5305a_5305-003.jpg
Notes:
9588142 Like a flower inside a flower - the Sanctum Sanctorum, the Holy of Holies - this is the feathery female stigma of a Crocus flower in the garden, with stamens below. Collect thousands of these stigmas, and you have saffron. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2005)

I've been busy with school stuff this week. And the rain just keeps coming. We have now had 64.89 inches of rain at home on San Marcos Pass this year since September! The woods are wet and green. Creaks are running. Mushrooms are soggy and confused. Flowers are just beginning. I've even seen a few butterflies. This will be a great year to be a naturalist-photographer!

File.Name: 5305b_5305-029.jpg
Notes:
9587886 Soggy Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms growing from the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves. The fresh edible mushrooms are blue above and below, but they lose their color after rain and with age. Blue or not, they usually have that "blewit look". (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2005)

I've been fooled by Cortinarius:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=9247116

File.Name: 5305c_5305-026.jpg
Notes: 9587753 Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica or kin) mushroom on a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) branch in the woods. Some of those dark spots move! They might be tiny Mites (Acari) and Springtail (Collembia) insects. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2005)
CommonNames: Witch's Butter; Coast Live Oak; Mites (Acari) and Springtail
ScientificName: Tremella mesenterica; Quercus agrifolia; Acari; Collembia
associatedTaxa: Tremella mesenterica; Quercus agrifolia; Acari; Collembia

File.Name: 5305d_5305-015.jpg
Notes: 9587726 Large Cup Fungi (Pezizaceae or kin) growing from Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the canyon. They are very common along our road this year, growing to teacup size, sometimes in cauliflower-like clusters. Do the woods have ears? (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2005)

File.Name: 5305e_5305-023.jpg
Notes: 9587660 Little Cup Fungus (Pezizaceae or kin) growing from a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) stick in the woods. It doesn't have a stalk, and the ouside looks crystalline. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2005)

File.Name: 5305f_5305-028.jpg
Notes: 9587614 This elegant mushroom looks drawn with charcoal and chalk. Maybe it is Woodland Inky Cap (Coprinus lagopus) or kin. Touch it and it falls apart. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2005)

File.Name: 5305g_5305-034.jpg
Notes: [mykologos] 11237904 Lace and leather - Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom growing from Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2005)

File.Name: 5312a_5312-005.jpg
Notes: 9644927 Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly on a leafy flower cup of Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the canyon. It's larval food plant (Dudleya lanceolata) is growing nearby. I've seen a few other butterflies in the air, but this is the first one I've managed to capture light from this year. Many "Blues" are mostly gray, but this one is brilliant. It feels like spring! (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2005)
ScientificName: Philotes sonorensis; Claytonia perfoliata; Dudleya lanceolata
associatedTaxa: Philotes sonorensis; Claytonia perfoliata; Dudleya lanceolata

File.Name: 5312b_5312-031.jpg
Notes: 9644836 New Anemone flower in the garden. This looks like a ritual dance. But there's more to it. The dark purple - almost black - male stamens start out short and flat (and yellow). The stamens lengthen and curve in to delicately deliver pollen to the female pistils in the center as the flower matures - just in case the flower is still barren. Seems like good insurance for an early flower that might appear before its pollinators. Many flowers can self-pollinate if other-pollination fails. Some do nothing else. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2005)

File.Name: 5312c_5312-016.jpg
Notes: 9644736 New flower of Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the canyon, and an open flower behind. It's a native shrub in the Phlox (Polemoniaceae) family that grows on rocky banks. The new flowers are twisted up like little pinwheels. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2005)

File.Name: 5312d_5312-015.jpg
Notes: 9644681 Native Sanicle (maybe Sanicula crassicaulis?) in the canyon. We have many different Sanicle species near home. They are in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family, and they all have distinctive round umbel-heads, easy to recognize once you know them. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2005)

File.Name: 5312e_5312-022.jpg
Notes: 9644442 Native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) - flower and thorn. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2005)

File.Name: 5312f_5312-025.jpg
Notes: 9644412 New flowers of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) against the sky. The individual flowers are tiny but intricate, like little pentagonal origami treasure boxes. Fog was moving in and out today here in the mountains. Several times I glanced at this shrub thinking the flowers were a swirl of fog. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2005)

File.Name: 5313a_5311-075.jpg
Notes:
9655582 My poster for the Dunn Middle School science fair last Friday. I'm the science teacher, but I always do my own project along with the kids. This year I made a spreadsheet calculator to figure the scale size in my macro photos. I've been busy. I'll put it on the web when I have time. (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 March 2005)

Here's my project as a stumper:

My Nikon D70 digital camera with 105mm macro lens can be a digital ruler to measure tiny things like the bee's knees and the fly's eyes - if I can figure out the scale. I can focus to almost 12 inches to get a 1/1 reproduction ratio so the real thing is the same size as the image on my camera sensor. My digital sensor is 2.37 by 1.56 cm, divided into 3008 by 2000 pixels. Suppose I then crop out a 1504 pixel square image and resize it to 500 pixels for Fotolog. What's the real size of the critter that fills the image? How big is a pixel? How many pixels long should a 1 centimeter scale bar be?

File.Name: 5313b_5313-036.jpg
Notes: 9655392 Elfin Saddle aka Hooded False Morel (Gyromita infula) mushroom on the wild edge of our garden. This false morel is POISONOUS - it contains MMH (MonoMethylHydrazine) which is carcinogenic and used as rocket fuel! It's so strange that mushrooms contain such *interesting* compounds. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2005)

File.Name: 5313c_5313-018.jpg
Notes: 9655264 New flower of Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the canyon, and a mature flower behind. It's a native shrub in the Phlox (Polemoniaceae) family that grows on rocky banks. The new flowers come twisted from the bud in a beautiful way. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2005)

File.Name: 5313d_5306-036.jpg
Notes:
9655236 Looking down on the Goleta Slough salt marsh with UCSB and Isla Vista in the background. I went up in a small plane to photograph the San Marcos Foothills land for www.sanmarcosfoothills.org/. As soon as we got in the air, our pilot Jim felt a power glitch and requested emergency landing. Thanks Jim! Our flight lasted about ten minutes. I took this shot through the window as we took off. (Santa Barbara, 6 March 2005)

I've driven past the Goleta Slough too many times to count without seeing these intricate channels. I really want to kayak in there!

File.Name: 5313e_5305-017.jpg
Notes: 9654814 Stalks of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the canyon. They are crunchy and succulent and edible like any lettuce. They are doing well in this wet year. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2005)

File.Name: 5313f_5303-012.jpg
Notes: 9654760 Tiny weevil (family Curculionidae) beetle with elephant-like snout on a flower of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora). (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2005)

File.Name: 5319a_5314-055.jpg
Notes:
9706674 Strange flower heads of Owl's Clover (Castilleja densiflora). Most of the color is from the leafy bracts, not the flowers. That yellow flower in the center is a ringer for Alice the Goon from EC Segar's classic Popeye comics! (Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2005)

Busy busy busy... science fair was last week, and tomorrow I'm off on a school backpack trip across Joshua Tree National Park for the week. I'll get a few photos - despite the rain!
